t: Kontze / Konze / Contzen Family Tree — Trendelburg & Deisel
d: Visual family tree of the Kontze / Konze / Contzen surname pool in the Trendelburg and Deisel parishes of Churhessen. Searched April–May 2026 against Charles Konzen's 26 January 1835 birth window — no candidate baptism found; the tree is retained as the project's reconstruction of the Konze surname pool.
b: Kontze / Konze / Contzen Family Tree — Trendelburg & Deisel Skip to main content KONTZE / KONZE / CONTZEN FAMILY TREE Trendelburg & Deisel, Kurhessen — Reconstructed from Archion Register 220096 Compiled 1 April 2026 · Searched against Charles's 26 January 1835 birth window — no candidate found (Sessions 18–50, April 2026; Peter Schräder verdict 12 April 2026) Jed Johnson & Peter Schräder · last updated 6 June 2026 Search complete — no Konze candidate identified. Between Sessions 18 and 50 of the April 2026 sprint, every documented Konze / Kontzen male in the Trendelburg parish complex (including the sons-of-Andreas window 1807–1818 that this tree's earlier "highest-priority research action" called for) was tested against Charles's 26 January 1835 birth window. Peter Schräder's verdict, 12 April 2026: “Charles is not a legitimate son of the Konze family.” The Entry 195 emergency-baptism candidate (Deisel KB 218842, January 1835) was also closed on 4 May 2026 when Peter re-read it as a Köster day-labourer's child, not a Konze (see Entry 195 source page ). This tree is retained as the project's reconstruction of the Konze surname pool ; Charles is not a documented descendant of any branch shown. June 2026 update. A surname-distribution sweep confirmed the Konze pool is confined to one tight Diemel-valley cluster (Trendelburg · Deisel · Friedrichsfeld · Stammen · Hümme — all within ~4 km), and a previously-undocumented Stammen Kontze household was found (Christian Ludwig Kontze × Anne Gertrud Temme, m. 1833; only child stillborn 1835 — shown below). For the full people-by-parish listing with FamilySearch IDs, see The Konze family, by town ; for the geography, the Hessian parish map . d: Who runs the Charles Research project, why, how to get in touch, and how to cite the work.
b: About this project — Charles Research Skip to main content Skip to main content ← Charles Research / About this project About this project Who runs it, why, how to contact us, and how to cite the work. This is a descendant-led, source-rigorous transatlantic genealogy investigation. It is not affiliated with any university or genealogical society. All sources are listed; all conclusions are held in the project’s explicit Confirmed / Working / Open / Eliminated taxonomy. Authors Project lead Jed Johnson Great-great-grandson of Charles Konzen / Charles Gannsen / Charles Johnson Sr. Initiated the current investigation in August 2025 after the September-2025 discovery of the Charles Sr. portrait by descendant Maximilian J. Responsible for site authorship, the surname-trail analysis, and the U.S.-side records work (NARA, Fold3, FamilySearch, Ancestry, Find a Grave, St. Marcus parish records). Contact: jedsjohnson@gmail.com · corrections, descendant submissions, and primary-source leads are all welcome — see how to contribute . ORCID: pending mint (see note ) Research collaborator Peter Schräder Independent genealogist based in Trendelburg, Hesse, Germany. Conducts the German-side parish work for the project: Stammtafel construction in the Trendelburg / Friedrichsfeld / Helmarshausen / Hofgeismar parish complex, Archion register transcription, Auswanderer-Beleg searches via Hessisches Staatsarchiv Marburg / Arcinsys / LAGIS . Originator of the “Freundburg, Kurhessen ” → Trendelburg phonetic decoding (correspondence with author, 26 February 2026; reaffirmed 6 May 2026). Correspondence is routed via Jed Johnson; please use the address above for first contact. ORCID: pending mint (see note ) ORCID identifiers are placeholders pending mint. Both authors will register at orcid.org/register and the IDs will be search-replaced through the JSON-LD, this page, and the Zenodo deposit. Why this site exists Charles never named his exact German parish on any U.S. record.

d: A one-page formal academic abstract of the Charles Konzen genealogical investigation: question, method, evidence, working conclusion, and gaps.
b: Abstract — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Abstract Abstract A formal one-page summary of the investigation — question, method, evidence, working conclusion, and acknowledged gaps. Print-ready; designed for citation in academic and genealogical work. ⤓ Download PDF Methodology → Subject Charles Konzen (also recorded in U.S. records as Gannsen, Gonsen, Konsen, Jansen, Conson, Johnson) — 26 January 1835 – 16 December 1903 † . Compiled by Jed Johnson, with Peter Schräder of Trendelburg as German genealogist. Period of investigation March 2026 – present (active). Foundation work 2017–2020. Last updated 25 May 2026 Citation See Methodology · How to cite Question This study investigates the German origin of Charles Konzen , a Hessian-born sergeant of Battery B, 2nd Missouri Light Artillery, who emigrated to the United States in 1859, served in the Union Army from December 1862 to December 1865, married Auguste Kreichelt at St. Marcus German Evangelical Church in St. Louis on 7 January 1866, fathered seven children documented in the parish register between 1866 and 1880, was awarded a federal pension for war-onset rheumatism in 1891, and died in St. Louis on 16 December 1903 (per the St. Marcus parish burial register #3393 — Doc N in the Doc A–O crosswalk ; the death certificate Doc A records 17 Dec, a 1-day discrepancy resolved in favour of the parish register per errata · 17 May 2026 · death & burial ) and was buried at Old Saint Marcus Cemetery 19 December 1903. 1 Two questions structure the investigation: what was Charles's German surname before its multiple American renderings , and which Hessian parish recorded his baptism ? Method The investigation is conducted in alignment with the Board for Certification of Genealogists' Genealogical Proof Standard — reasonably exhaustive research; complete and accurate citations; analysis and correlation of the collected information; resolution of conflicting evidence; soundly reasoned wri

d: A formal bibliography of every primary source, archive, database, and secondary work cited in the Charles Research project.
b: Bibliography — Charles Research Skip to main content Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Bibliography Bibliography A formal index of every primary source, archive, database, and secondary work cited in the project. Citations follow NGSQ short form; see Methodology · Citation style for the long-form template. The investigation rests on three classes of primary sources (military, parish, and U.S. vital), one class of derivative aggregator (Ancestry, FamilySearch, Find a Grave), and a small set of secondary literature on the political and demographic context of mid-nineteenth-century Hesse-Kassel. Each source on this page is hyperlinked to its imaged or indexed presentation on this site, where one exists. 1 · U.S. military and pension records Compiled Service Record of Charles Gannsen. Pvt./Sgt., Battery B, 2nd Missouri Light Artillery (Union) · Record group 94, M405, file G11-422819443E · NARA, Washington, D.C. Mail-order packet received May 2026; 25 pages of carded muster rolls and special-orders entries. Imaged on this site at Sources › NARA . Where Fold3 readings disagree with the NARA originals, the NARA reading is canonical. Civil War Pension File of Charles Gannsen. F41-523680269E · NARA, Washington, D.C. Full pension file: declaration, affidavits of seven affiants — August Derr, Christian Meyer, Henry Stoll, Michael Nolan, Henry Niemeier, Theodore Gottlob, and "John Heitzenberger" (as written by the affidavit clerk; surname-family identified as Hitzelberger — individual identity remains open); medical examinations; address-history sequence; rejection and re-filing correspondence. (Adam Herweck appears elsewhere as the 1866 marriage witness, not as a pension affiant.) Imaged at Sources › Pension File . Fold3 — Missouri Volunteer Union Service Records. t: Charles's Birth Date Across the U.S. Records — Charles Research
h: Charles's Birth Date Across the U.S. Records
d: Every U.S. record that states or implies Charles Konzen's age or birth date — enlistment, censuses, pension declarations, death certificate, and church burial register — sourced and ranked by genealogical confidence. The figures scatter across 1833–1841; the canonical 26 January 1835 rests on a single post-mortem parish entry.
b: Charles's Birth Date Across the U.S. Records — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Birth Date Across the U.S. Records Charles's Birth Date Across the U.S. Records Ten American records, and almost as many different birth dates — from 1833 to 1841. Each one sourced, cross-checked against the original scans, and ranked by genealogical confidence. A single, framing caveat governs this whole page: no independent birth or baptism record for Charles has ever been found — the German parish baptism remains the project's most-wanted document . Every figure below traces back to Charles's own testimony or, at his death, to his family's. None is a true original, firsthand record of the birth event . They are best read together as a cluster, not as competing facts. 10 U.S. records 1833–1841 spread of stated years 1 record with a full date 26 Jan 1835 working anchor How these are rated — by genealogical standards The confidence ratings here follow the evidence-analysis model used in professional genealogy — the Source · Information · Evidence framework — together with the Genealogical Proof Standard (GPS) , rather than an ad-hoc scale. Three independent classifications are applied to each record, with respect to the birth fact specifically : Source Is the document an original (created at the event, or a contemporaneous register) or a derivative (a later copy, abstract, or compiled record)? Information Is the birth detail primary (from someone with firsthand knowledge of the birth) or secondary (reported second-hand)? Evidence Does it answer the birth-date question directly (states an age or date), indirectly (must be inferred), or as negative evidence (an expected record’s absence)? The decisive point under these standards: not one of these records carries primary information about Charles's birth. No informant — not even Charles himself — witnessed his own 1835 birth; every figure is second-hand knowledge carried within the family. So all ten

d: A reverse-chronological log of every significant finding, transcription, identification, and structural change to the Charles Research site.
b: Changelog — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Changelog Changelog A reverse-chronological log of every significant finding, transcription, identification, and structural change to the project. Latest at the top. Each entry is anchored by date so collaborators can cite a specific version. The home page carries the latest three or four findings as a feed; this page is the canonical archive. Entries are tagged by confidence: Confirmed means a primary source is attached, Working is the leading current hypothesis, Eliminated is a candidate ruled out, and Structure records site- and methodology-level changes. June 2026 19 June 2026 Structure New reference page collates every U.S. record of Charles’s birth date and ranks them by the Genealogical Proof Standard; two source-attribution errors and a pension-declaration mis-reading corrected. A new reference page, Charles’s birth date across the U.S. records , gathers the six birth-bearing documents (Docs A, B, D, M, N, O) into one comparative table and ranks each by genealogical confidence under the Genealogical Proof Standard and the Mills source / information / evidence model. Its load-bearing finding: none of these records carries primary information about the birth — every figure (enlistment age, census ages, three pension-file ages, death certificate, burial register) traces to Charles or his family, so all are secondary, direct evidence, and the stated years scatter across 1834–1841 . The canonical 26 January 1835 stays Working . Two corrections fell out of the build. (1) The life-timeline and a home-page Key-Evidence pointer credited the 26 Jan 1835 date to Charles’s “pension affidavit” ; the pension gives only ages — the full date is from the St. Marcus burial register (Doc N, #3393) . b: Life Timeline · Charles Gannsen in America Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Charles in America / Life Timeline Life Timeline — Birth to Burial 26 January 1835 (Kurhessen) → 16 December 1903 (St. Louis) · 68 years CHARLES IN AMERICA 38 years in St. Louis · 18 events documented ★ At a Glance From an unknown port of arrival to a sergeant's stripes, a German Evangelical wedding, seven children, and a working watchman's life on the north side of St. Louis. Charles's American life ran 44 years — from 1859 immigration to his 1903 death. The 16-year gap before 1859 (childhood, confirmation, possible apprenticeship, emigration prep) sits inside the German-side investigation; everything below is anchored in U.S. primary records. The full timeline 26 January 1835 Born in Kurhessen — Deisel/Trendelburg area, Hofgeismar Kirchenkreis . Day and month from the St. Marcus burial register (entry no. 3393); the year is corroborated by the censuses. (The pension file gives only inconsistent ages, not this date — see birth date across the records .) St. Marcus burial register #3393 (Doc N) ~1843–1859 A 16-year documentation gap. Childhood, confirmation, possible apprenticeship, and emigration all sit inside this window. Inferred — no records yet found 1859 Immigrated to the United States. Census-reported year; departure and arrival ports remain unknown. Hamburg or Bremen are most likely for a Kurhessen emigrant. 1900 U.S. Federal Census 2 December 1862 Enlisted as L. Charles Gannsen in the Missouri 2nd Regiment, Battery B, Light Artillery (U.S. Army). Birthplace stated: "Freundburg, Kurhessen." Civil War enlistment record 1862–1865 Served through the Civil War, rising from Private to Corporal to Sergeant (promoted 7 November 1863), then reduced back to Private on 15 March 1865 mid-detachment. Discharged 20 December 1865 as a Private. Sustained injuries documented in the pension file: hernia, hearing loss, rheumatism. Pension medical examinations · NARA Compiled Service R

It served not only the Gannsen family but a documentable cluster of figures from Charles's wider social world — including Jacob Reck, husba
b: Old St. Marcus Cemetery — Charles Gannsen in America Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Charles in America / Old St. Marcus Cemetery Old St. Marcus Cemetery — A Parish-Circle Burial Cluster 6638 Gravois Rd. · now St. Marcus Commemorative Park · German Lutheran/Evangelical burial ground CHARLES IN AMERICA More than just Charles's plot ★ At a Glance Old St. Marcus Cemetery (6638 Gravois Rd., now St. Marcus Commemorative Park ) was the burial ground of the German Lutheran/Evangelical St. Marcus parish circle. It served not only the Gannsen family but a documentable cluster of figures from Charles's wider social world — including Jacob Reck , husband of Charles's 1868-baptism sponsor, who is buried just five years before Charles in the same consecrated south-side ground. The Charles–Jakob Reck pairing is the strongest physical evidence yet that Charles's parish-circle social bonds persisted from the 1860s baptism font to the 1900s graveyard. Cemetery Old St. Marcus · 6638 Gravois Rd. · now St. Marcus Commemorative Park Confirmed parish-circle burials 6 individuals (Gannsen + Reck + 4 Goetz women) Charles's burial 19 Dec 1903 · St. Marcus burial #3393 Family plots ★ Plot No. 7 (9 burials, 1867–1948) + Plot No. 8 (Charles Jr. 1950) — 10 interments total — NOT under perpetual care Headstones Most removed when site became a city park · register + obituaries are the better evidence base ★ Burial register source — the Gansen / Johnson family in Plot No. 7 Zoom: − 100% 150% 200% 300% + 100% ↗ Open full-resolution ⬇ Download Old St. Marcus Cemetery perpetual-care burial register — page spread 2 / 3 (FamilySearch film 8198626, frame 00006). Left page: Schneider plot (No. 5), Otto plot (No. 6), and the Gansen / Johnson family plot (No. 7) with nine entries 1867–1948 (the family's ten interments span 1867–1950, the tenth being Charles Jr. in adjacent Plot No. 8). Two of the seven names are themselves evidence: the firstborn Friedrich Wilhelm (named for the deposed Electo
b: The Seven Children · Charles Gannsen in America Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Charles in America / The Seven Children The Seven Children — Gannsen-Kreichelt Generation 2 Born 1866–1880 · all baptized at St. Marcus German Evangelical Church · 4 surviving past childhood CHARLES IN AMERICA Surnames recorded as Gonnson · Johnson · Konsen ★ At a Glance All seven children of Charles Gannsen × Auguste Kreichelt were baptized Lutheran at St. Marcus Church , with surnames recorded variously as Gonnson , Johnson , and (critically, in 1875) Konsen . Two of the seven names are themselves evidence: the firstborn Friedrich Wilhelm (named for the deposed Elector of Hesse the same year he lost his throne) and the sixth child Erwin Carl Konsen (whose 1875 baptism preserves the German Hesse spelling of the surname). Three children — Friedrich Wilhelm (16 months), Maria Josephine (22), and Auguste (18) — died young; four survived into the 20th century. ⚜ A name that points home — Friedrich Wilhelm Gonnson · 20 June 1866 → 2 September 1866 Charles and Auguste Kreichelt married at St. Marcus Church on 7 January 1866 . Their first child was born 20 June 1866 and christened 2 September 1866 as Friedrich Wilhelm Gonnson . Per the FamilySearch tree custom-fact attribution (Jed S Johnson, Aug 2021): "Named after Friederich Wilhelm Pillmann, one of the baptism sponsors." The same summer of 1866 saw the political destruction of Charles's homeland. The day-by-day timeline is striking: 17 June 1866 : Prussia declares war on Hesse-Kassel — start of the Austro-Prussian War ~19 June 1866 : Prussian troops occupy Kassel, the Electoral capital ★ 20 June 1866 : Friedrich Wilhelm Gonnson born in St. Louis — three days after Prussia's declaration of war, ~one day after Kassel falls. Trans-Atlantic telegraph not yet operational; steamship news ~10–14 days behind. Charles in St. Louis almost certainly did not yet know about the war. 23 June 1866 : Elector Friedrich Wilhelm I is transfer

d: Charles's residences across thirty-eight years in St. Louis (1865–1903), reconciled across three independent record sets: (a) his own handwritten address-history letter inside the Civil War pension file (Page 29, c. 1898); (b) the 1880 and 1900 federal censuses; and (c) the 1871 and 1895 St. Louis c
b: St. Louis Residences — Charles Gannsen in America Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Charles in America / St. Louis Residences St. Louis Residences — 1865 to 1903 Six documented addresses across 38 years · LaSalle Park / Soulard → Kosciusko (with one 1880 north-side detour to Baden) CHARLES IN AMERICA Reconciled across pension file, 2 censuses, 2 directories ★ At a Glance Charles's residences across thirty-eight years in St. Louis (1865–1903), reconciled across three independent record sets: (a) his own handwritten address-history letter inside the Civil War pension file (Page 29, c. 1898) ; (b) the 1880 and 1900 federal censuses; and (c) the 1871 and 1895 St. Louis city directories. Six of the seven verified addresses cluster on the south side of St. Louis, between downtown and the river: 1518 S. 7th in LaSalle Park (1865–69), then 2409 S. Broadway, 2412 S. 2nd, 2408 DeKalb, and 168/170 Sidney all in modern Kosciusko (1874–1903). The 1880 census places the family at 1534 Jackson Street — in the south-central Ward 9 district, where the period Jackson Street ran between Columbus and DeKalb across Sidney St (not the modern north-side Jackson Street in Baden). The two DeKalb Street numbers visible in the secondary records (2405 rear, 2407 front) are adjacent buildings on the same lot. St. Louis residences — mapped Charles's residences across thirty-eight years in St. Louis (1865–1903), reconciled across three independent record sets: (a) his own handwritten address-history letter inside the Civil War pension file (Page 29, c. 1898) ; (b) the 1880 and 1900 federal censuses; and (c) the 1871 and 1895 St. Louis city directories. The earliest addresses cluster in the historically-German Soulard / LaSalle Park district south of downtown; the family then settled long-term on the Kosciusko blocks closer to the river — DeKalb Street and Sidney Street — where Charles spent most of his post-1890 life. b: St. Louis Residences & Landmarks Map — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Charles in America / St. Louis map Charles in St. Louis — residences & landmarks Every documented St. Louis address from Charles's 1862 enlistment through his 1903 burial — homes, parish, work circle, and burial — plotted on the modern street grid. Charles spent forty-one years in St. Louis, almost all of it in the densely-German neighbourhoods south of downtown — Soulard, Frenchtown, and the lower South Side. The map below shows every residence the pension file's address-history recorded, the parish church where his seven children were baptised, the social and work circle he kept (Anheuser-Busch via Erwin Spraul), the photo studio where his only known portrait was taken, and the cemetery where he was buried in December 1903. Reading the map Residence Documented home address from the pension file's address-history sequence. Parish St. Marcus Evangelical Church — site of the 1866 marriage and seven baptisms. Work circle Anheuser-Busch brewery (via Erwin Spraul, 1875 godfather), photo studio. Burial Old Saint Marcus Cemetery, now St. Marcus Commemorative Park. Pension witness / friend Theodore Gottlob, Erwin Spraul — documented social-circle figures. Address-by-address detail 1518 South 7th Street Home · 1865–1869 38.609 N · 90.207 W · LaSalle Park First documented St. Louis address per the pension file's address-history sequence. Charles & Auguste lived here in the early years of marriage. The gap between this address and 718 Gates is the leading explanation for why the 1870 census has not located their household. Open in Google Maps 718 Gates Avenue Home · 1871– ~38.61 N · 90.215 W Second documented address. The Gannsen family lived here in the early 1870s, the period that produced the 1870 Katherine Carolina and 1872 Auguste baptisms (both recorded by Pastor Braschler — the "smoking gun" pair). DeKalb Street Home · later years ~38.6 N · 90.21 W · Soulard

d: Fourth-born child · also recorded on FamilySearch as "Auguste Johnson"
b: Auguste Gonsen (1872–1890) — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Charles in America / The Seven Children Auguste Gonsen Fourth-born child · also recorded on FamilySearch as "Auguste Johnson" Born 4 March 1872 · Died 1890 · age 18 ★ At a Glance CHILD 4 of 7 · DIED YOUNG FS L5N2-M8Y Charles and Auguste's fourth child, named directly after her mother. She lived 18 years and died young in 1890 — the same year as her older sister Josephine. Her 1890 death record is the single American attestation of the full Hessian z-form spelling “Konzen”, and the bridge to the German parish-register surname pool. Auguste Gonsen (b. 4 March 1872, d. 1890 at age 18) was the fourth-born child of Charles & Auguste — named directly after her mother. Christened 23 June 1872 at St. Marcus with sponsors Georg Kreichelt , Louise (Henriette) Kreichelt , and August Kreichelt — the most "in-family" baptism of the seven (all three sponsors are Auguste's parents and eldest brother). Surname rendered Gonsen — distinct from sister Katherine's 1870 "Gansen". The same scribe (Pastor Braschler) wrote two different vowels for the same family in two different baptisms two years apart — the project's strongest evidence of unstable surname-rendering. Why this matters for understanding Charles: Her 1890 death record records her maiden name as " geb. Konzen" — born Konzen, with a z — making this the single American attestation of the full Hessian z-form Konzen documented in the Trendelburg parish complex. Together with her brother Erwin Carl's 1875 baptism reading Konsen (with s, Pastor Hoffmann's phonetic capture), the two records bracket the surname trail with the two closest matches to the documented Hessian Kontzen / Konzen pool. The 1890 death record is keystone evidence that the family kept the older Hessian -en suffix in continuous use 1875–1890, in two parallel orthographic variants. (The 1890 reading was previously transcribed Konzen → Konsen → Konzen across May 2

d: Seventh-born and youngest child · also recorded on FamilySearch as "Bertha Johnson"
b: Bertha Jansen (1880–1959) — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Charles in America / The Seven Children Bertha Jansen Seventh-born and youngest child · also recorded on FamilySearch as "Bertha Johnson" Born 25 December 1880 · Died 1959 · lived to 78 (see errata · 17 May 2026 for 1879→1880 correction) ★ At a Glance CHILD 7 of 7 · YOUNGEST · LATER "BERTHA JOHNSON" FS KGM3-RDK Charles and Auguste's seventh and youngest child, born Christmas Day 1880. She lived to seventy-nine, married into the Johnson family, and outlived all of her siblings. Bertha Jansen (b. 25 December 1880, d. 1959) was the seventh and youngest child of Charles & Auguste — later known as Bertha Johnson . Charles's 1898 pension declaration lists her as "Bertha Gannson, born 20 December 1880" (5-day pension drift from the church record). Christened 4 September 1881 at St. Marcus with the sole named sponsor Bertha Barbara "Babette" Kreichelt (her aunt and namesake). Surname rendered Jansen — the J-form persists into the final baptism, anchoring the eventual "Johnson" Anglicization. Why this matters for understanding Charles: The 1880 baptism is the terminal entry in the seven-baptism series and the latest documented "Jansen" rendering before the family fully Anglicized to Johnson. The pension declaration's "Bertha Gannson" spelling shows that even by 1898 — twenty years later — Charles himself or his pension-clerk still used a German-form variant. The Jansen → Johnson resolution happens entirely in the post-1881 American civil-registration record. t: Erwin Carl Konsen / Charles Johnson Jr. (1875–1950) — Charles Research
d: Sixth-born child · later "Charles Johnson Jr." · the only baptism that preserved the Hesse-style surname Konsen
b: Erwin Carl Konsen / Charles Johnson Jr. (1875–1950) — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Charles in America / The Seven Children Erwin Carl Konsen ★ Sixth-born child · later "Charles Johnson Jr." · the only baptism that preserved the Hesse-style surname Konsen Born 27 August 1875 · Died 1950 · lived to 74 ★ At a Glance CHILD 6 of 7 · LATER "CHARLES JOHNSON JR." · ★ KEY EVIDENCE FS KL18-V2Y Charles and Auguste's sixth child and the one who, as an adult, carried his father's name into the next generation as Charles Johnson Jr. He lived to seventy-four and married Katherine Misch. His 1875 baptism is the keystone phonetic evidence record for the surname investigation: the parish clerk wrote his surname as Konsen — the closest American phonetic capture of the German Hessian spelling found anywhere on the U.S. record trail, and the phonetic-s sibling of his sister Auguste's 1890 death record reading Konzen (the verbatim Hesse-parish form and the closest American match overall). Erwin Carl Konsen (b. 27 August 1875, d. 1950 at age 74) was the sixth-born child of Charles & Auguste — later known as Charles Johnson Jr. Christened 3 October 1875 at St. Marcus with the sole named sponsor Erwin Spraul (Anheuser-Busch Superintendent), with Charles Sr. listed as listed co-sponsor — the only Gannsen baptism with no Kreichelt-side sponsor at all. Surname rendered Konsen — the unique Hesse-style spelling that breaks the J-form / G-form drift pattern of the other six baptisms. Why this matters for understanding Charles: This baptism is the strongest American phonetic surname-preservation evidence in the project, paired with sister Auguste's 1890 death record (the verbatim Konzen entry) as the project's strongest current surname-preservation evidence (the 1890 Konzen reading was itself revised across May 2026 re-examinations — see errata ). The "Konsen" spelling is the only one of the seven baptisms that ties Charles back to the Hofgeismar Kirchen

d: First-born child of Charles Gannsen & Auguste Kreichelt · died as an infant
b: Friedrich Wilhelm Gonnson (1866–1867) — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Charles in America / The Seven Children Friedrich Wilhelm Gonnson ⚜ First-born child of Charles Gannsen & Auguste Kreichelt · died as an infant Born 20 June 1866 · Died 26 October 1867 · ~16 months ★ At a Glance FIRST-BORN · CHILD 1 of 7 · DIED AS INFANT FS LB5X-RYC Charles and Auguste's firstborn son. He arrived in June 1866 — just months after their wedding — and died as an infant 16 months later. He never knew his six younger siblings. Friedrich Wilhelm Gonnson (b. 20 June 1866, d. 26 October 1867 at age 16 months) was the first-born child of Charles & Auguste, born three days after Prussia declared war on Hesse-Kassel and christened on 2 September 1866 — about six weeks after news of the deposition of Elector Friedrich Wilhelm I would have reached St. Louis. The baby's documented namesake is his godfather Friedrich Wilhelm Pillmann (per the standard Lutheran firstborn-naming convention); the name happens also to be that of the deposed Elector, an echo discussed below. Sponsors: F.W. Pillmann, Wilhelmine Ebeling Leue , and the maternal grandparents Georg & Henriette Kreichelt . Surname rendered Gonnson — same spelling as Charles's January 1866 marriage record. Why this matters for understanding Charles: The 1866 timing alignment with the Austro-Prussian War and the Hesse-Kassel deposition is a suggestive cultural detail consistent with a Hesse-Kassel origin — but it is not, on its own, diagnostic of it: Friedrich Wilhelm was a very common German given-name pair, and the documented namesake here is the godfather, Friedrich Wilhelm Pillmann. The two readings below (deliberate invocation vs. meaningful coincidence) are both interpretations of Charles's intent, which the record does not settle. The geographic identification of Charles's origin rests on the 1862 enlistment and 1866 marriage records (Key Evidence Cards 2–3), not on this naming. FamilySearch

h: The Seven Children of Charles Konzen & Auguste Kreichelt
d: Each baptized at St. Marcus German Evangelical Church, St. Louis · the surname spelling drifted across five distinct baptism-register renderings (Gonnson · Gansen · Gonsen · Jansen · Konsen) — the children's-baptism subset of the eight-attestation surname trail.
b: The Seven Children — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Charles in America / The Seven Children The Seven Children of Charles Konzen & Auguste Kreichelt Each baptized at St. Marcus German Evangelical Church, St. Louis · the surname spelling drifted across five distinct baptism-register renderings — the children's-baptism subset of the eight-attestation surname trail . Charles and Auguste's seven children Charles and Auguste married at St. Marcus German Evangelical Church on 7 January 1866. Over the next fourteen years they had seven children — four daughters and three sons. The eldest, Friedrich Wilhelm, died as an infant. Two daughters — Maria Josephine and Auguste — died young, in their early twenties, both in the same year (1890). The other four children — Katherine Carolina, Louise, Erwin Carl, and Bertha — lived into the twentieth century; three of them lived past seventy. Each child is profiled below in birth order. A note on the surnames you'll see across these pages: six of the seven baptisms used a different surname spelling for the family — only the 1875 baptism of Erwin Carl Konsen preserved the Hessian K-form (the spelling closest to the original German). The variety is itself the evidence: it tells us the spelling came from Charles's spoken voice rather than from any one pastor's pen. See Key Evidence, Card 1 for the full surname-trail argument. 7 children baptized at St. Marcus 15y first to seventh 1866 → 1880 3 died young (infant · 22 · 18) 4 lived into the 20th century 5 baptism-clerk spellings 1 ★ Konsen 1875 baptism ★ The surname-preserving baptism 1875 · Erwin Carl Konsen Of the seven children, only one was baptized under the Hessian spelling. On 27 August 1875 , the St. Marcus parish clerk recorded Charles and Auguste's sixth child as Erwin Carl Konsen — the "K" + "-en" form that matches the Konze / Konsen / Konzen surname pool of the Hofgeismar Kirchenkreis parish registers (1773 onward). d: Second-born child · also recorded on FamilySearch as "Josephine Maria Johnson"
b: Maria Josephine Gonnson (1868–1890) — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Charles in America / The Seven Children Maria Josephine Gonnson Second-born child · also recorded on FamilySearch as "Josephine Maria Johnson" Born 20 February 1868 · Died 1890 · age 22 ★ At a Glance CHILD 2 of 7 · DIED YOUNG FS LTSG-XWW Charles and Auguste's second child and the first to survive infancy. She lived 22 years and died young in 1890. Her 1868 baptism is one of the eight U.S. records that preserve a phonetic spelling of the family surname. Maria Josephine Gonnson (b. 20 February 1868, d. 1890 at age 22) was the second-born child of Charles & Auguste — the first to survive infancy. Christened 1 June 1868 at St. Marcus , with sponsors Jakob Reck & his first wife Josephine née von Delien , Maria Agnes Götz , and Georg Kreichelt . The infant carries Josephine née von Delien's first name as her middle namesake. Surname still rendered Gonnson — same as her older brother's 1866 baptism. Why this matters for understanding Charles: This is the only one of the seven baptisms with non-Kreichelt-side sponsors dominating (3 of 4 sponsors are non-kin: the Reck couple + Götz). It signals a meaningful Charles-side parish-circle friendship — the Pfalz-Reformed Reck couple plus the Pfälzer / Hessen-Darmstadt / Baden Götz network. Josephine's individual identity is the only open sponsor ID in the project. t: Friedrike Catharina Caroline Amalia Gansen / Katherine Carolina (1870–1949) — Charles Research
d: Third-born child · later known as Katherine Carolina Johnson · pension file calls her "Kate Ruddy" (married name)
b: Friedrike Catharina Caroline Amalia Gansen / Katherine Carolina (1870–1949) — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Charles in America / The Seven Children Friedrike Catharina Caroline Amalia Gansen Third-born child · later known as Katherine Carolina Johnson · pension file calls her "Kate Ruddy" (married name) Born 5 April 1870 · Died 1949 · lived to 79 ★ At a Glance CHILD 3 of 7 · LATER "KATE RUDDY" FS L5N2-98F Charles and Auguste's third child. She lived to seventy-nine, married into the Ruddy family, and was known to her family as Kate. Her 1870 baptism is the famous “Braschler smoking-gun” record — the same pastor who would baptise her sister Auguste two years later wrote two different spellings of the family name, evidence that the spelling came from Charles's own voice. Friedrike Catharina Caroline Amalia Gansen (b. 5 April 1870, d. 1949 at age 79) was the third-born child of Charles & Auguste — later known as Katherine Carolina Johnson , then "Kate Ruddy" after marriage. Charles's 1898 pension declaration lists her as "Kate Ruddy, born 8 April 1870" (3-day pension drift from the church record). Christened 22 May 1870 at St. Marcus with sponsors Friedrich Pillmann , Catharina Albrecht , Carl Kreichelt , and Amalia Münke (Maria Emma Mincke). Surname now rendered Gansen — first vowel-shift away from Gonnson. Why this matters for understanding Charles: The 1870 baptism is the densest in-law-network sponsorship in the seven-baptism series — Pillmann had married Albrecht's sister 7 weeks earlier; Catharina Albrecht would marry Carl Kreichelt 10 months later; Amalia Münke was already August Kreichelt's wife. Four sponsors stitch the Pillmann / Albrecht / Kreichelt / Mincke families into a single sponsor pool. The infant carries multiple namesakes (Catharina Albrecht, Amalia Münke). FamilySearch profile L5N2-98F Born 5 April 1870 · St. Louis Baptized 22 May 1870 · St. Marcus Doc H p. 285, entry 6644 Surname spelling Gansen American

d: Fifth-born child · later "Louise Foster" (married name) · also recorded on FamilySearch as "Louise Johnson"
b: Louise Jansen (1873–1947) — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Charles in America / The Seven Children Louise Jansen Fifth-born child · later "Louise Foster" (married name) · also recorded on FamilySearch as "Louise Johnson" Born 26 December 1873 · Died 1947 · lived to 74 ★ At a Glance CHILD 5 of 7 · LATER "LOUISE FOSTER" FS LZXC-DFN Charles and Auguste's fifth child. She lived to seventy-four, married into the Foster family, and was known to her family simply as Louise. Louise Jansen (b. 26 December 1873, d. 1947 at age 74) was the fifth-born child of Charles & Auguste — later known as Louise Johnson , then "Louise Foster" after marriage. Charles's 1898 pension declaration lists her as "Louise Foster, born December 1873." Christened 22 February 1874 at St. Marcus with sponsors Louise Henriette Kreichelt née Weis (her grandmother and namesake) and Barbara "Babette" Kreichelt (her aunt, then age 13). Surname now rendered Jansen — the first appearance of the J-form spelling, marking the start of the German→Anglicization drift toward "Johnson." Why this matters for understanding Charles: The 1873 baptism is the first surname appearance of Jansen — the J-form that would persist on subsequent baptisms (Erwin's "Konsen" exception aside) and ultimately resolve to "Johnson" in American records. The shift from Gonnson / Gansen / Gonsen to Jansen tracks the parish-clerk's progressive Anglicization of a German Konze -line surname over 7 years. t: Battery B, 2nd Missouri Light Artillery — Charles Gannsen's Civil War unit
h: Battery B, 2nd Regiment, Missouri Light Artillery 🇺🇸 Union Army
d: Battery B, 2nd Regiment Missouri Light Artillery — Charles Gannsen's Civil War unit. A Union Army U.S. Volunteer artillery battery raised in St. Louis, attached to the Department of the Missouri, with garrison and field service at St. Louis, New Madrid, Franklin, Rolla, and the Powder River Expedition (1862–1865).
b: Battery B, 2nd Missouri Light Artillery — Charles Gannsen's Civil War unit Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Civil War / Battery B, 2nd Missouri Light Artillery Battery B, 2nd Regiment, Missouri Light Artillery 🇺🇸 Union Army Charles Gannsen's unit · 1862–1865 · U.S. Volunteer artillery, Department of the Missouri CIVIL WAR Charles served 2 Dec 1862 – 20 Dec 1865 ★ At a Glance Battery B, 2nd Regiment, Missouri Light Artillery was a Union Army U.S. Volunteer light-artillery battery raised in St. Louis at the start of the Civil War. The 2nd Missouri Light Artillery as a regiment was organized between September 1861 and February 1862; Battery B was one of its twelve lettered batteries. Charles Gannsen enlisted in Battery B on 2 December 1862 and served with the unit through 20 December 1865 — a clean three-year enlistment covering the entire arc from Charles's first muster-in to his honorable discharge. The battery was attached to the Department of the Missouri (headquartered in St. Louis), with most of its 1862–1865 service concentrated within Missouri: garrison duty at the St. Louis forts, two tours at the strategic Mississippi River post of New Madrid (Sept–Nov 1863 with battery; April 1864 – March 1865 with Charles detached), and a late-war re-equipping as cavalry that took the unit to Omaha and the Powder River Expedition in summer 1865 — without Charles, who in spring 1865 was on detached duty at Rolla / Fort Wyman and then at Department HQ St. Louis through November 1865. t: Fort Wyman / 64th Illinois Mystery — Research Log · Charles Gannsen Civil War
h: Fort Wyman / "64th Illinois" Mystery — Research Log
d: Charles's 1890 pension declaration claims he was injured by heavy lifting at Fort Wyman, Rolla, Missouri while detailed as drill sergeant for the 64th Illinois Infantry in Heavy Artillery tactics.…
b: Fort Wyman / 64th Illinois Mystery — Research Log · Charles Gannsen Civil War Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Civil War / Where Charles Served / Fort Wyman Research Log Fort Wyman / "64th Illinois" Mystery — Research Log A May 2026 forensic investigation: pension claim vs. War Department roll search vs. NARA muster cards vs. free-source unit-history checks · who was Charles drilling at Fort Wyman in 1865? CIVIL WAR Last updated 6 May 2026 ★ At a Glance Charles's 1890 pension declaration claims he was injured by heavy lifting at Fort Wyman, Rolla, Missouri while detailed as drill sergeant for the 64th Illinois Infantry in Heavy Artillery tactics. The carded record places Charles at Rolla, Missouri from 27 March through 1 July 1865 (~3 months) under G.O. No. 82 / S.O. No. 14, Hd Qrs Dept of the Mo. Charles arrived at Rolla after his ~11-month New Madrid Tour 2 (24 April 1864 – 27 March 1865) ended. Fort Wyman was the main Union fortification at Rolla, so this is consistent with the pension claim's "Fort Wyman" wording — but the literal word "Wyman" does not appear on any muster card. On 1 July 1865 Charles transferred from Rolla to Department HQ St. Louis under S.O. No. 159; he was relieved 4 November 1865 by S.O. No. 92. The unit-being-drilled question is still open; the strongest candidate is the 49th Wisconsin Volunteer Infantry (at Rolla 13 March – August 1865), which overlaps Charles's 27 March – 1 July Rolla window almost exactly. Status as of 6 May 2026 Place: CORROBORATED with caveat. The cards explicitly place Charles at "Rolla, Mo." from 27 March – 1 July 1865 under G.O. No. 82 / S.O. No. 14, Hd Qrs Dept of the Mo. Fort Wyman was the main fort at Rolla, so the pension claim's "Fort Wyman" wording is consistent with the carded record. The literal word "Wyman" does NOT appear on any muster card — the previous "first verbatim Wyman hit anywhere" finding was a transcription error and is retracted. t: Fort Wyman, Rolla, Missouri — Late-War Detached Service · Charles Gannsen Civil War
d: Fort Wyman, Rolla, Missouri — Charles Gannsen's late-war detached service from 27 March through 1 July 1865 (~3 months) under G.O. No. 82 of the Department of the Missouri. The fort named in his pension claim's heavy-lifting / right-side injury narrative.
b: Fort Wyman, Rolla, Missouri — Late-War Detached Service · Charles Gannsen Civil War Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Civil War / Where Charles Served / Fort Wyman, Rolla Fort Wyman, Rolla, Missouri Late-war detached service · 27 March – 1 July 1865 · ~3 months · the fort named in Charles's 1890 pension claim CIVIL WAR Place consistent with carded record · "Wyman" word not literally on cards ★ At a Glance Charles served at Rolla, Missouri — where Fort Wyman was the main Union fortification — on detached service from 27 March 1865 through 1 July 1865 , about 3 months , under G.O. No. 82 (Fold3 reading) / S.O. No. 14 (NARA reading) of the Headquarters of the Department of the Missouri. He arrived at Rolla after the long ~11-month New Madrid Tour 2 ended on 27 March 1865, and left Rolla on 1 July 1865 when transferred to Department HQ St. Louis under S.O. No. 159. Fort Wyman is the fort Charles named in his 1890 pension declaration as the location of the heavy-lifting incident that produced his right-inguinal-hernia disability claim. The carded record places Charles at "Rolla, Mo." for this window — consistent with the "Fort Wyman" pension wording, since Wyman was the main fort there. The literal word "Wyman" does not appear on any muster card. Period 27 Mar – 1 Jul 1865 (~3 months) Rank during detail Private (reduced 15 Mar 1865 from Sgt) Governing order G.O. No. 82, 27 Mar 1865, Hd Qrs Dept of Mo. (Fold3); S.O. No. 14, 24 Mar 1865 (NARA) Significance Place named in pension claim's heavy-lifting / right-side injury narrative What the fort looked like — the 1865 plan drawing ★ No photographs of Fort Wyman are known to survive. The fort's design is documented by the 1865 scale drawing made by Capt. William Hoelcke , engineering officer of the Department of the Missouri, the year Charles served at the post. The original drawing is held by the U.S. National Archives in Washington, D.C. and was scanned and digitized by the Curtis Laws Wilson Library at Misso

The current working interpretation is that both are clerk's-pen distortions of a German-language original: "Freund
b: The "Freundburg, Kurhessen" & "L." Mysteries · Charles Gannsen Civil War Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Civil War / The "Freundburg" & "L." Mysteries The "Freundburg, Kurhessen" & "L." Mysteries Two clerk's-pen oddities on the 1862 enlistment card that anchor the entire German-side search CIVIL WAR · NAMING Both run back to a German-language original ★ At a Glance The 1862 Civil War enlistment card records Charles's birthplace as "Freundburg, Kurhessen " and his name as "L. Charles Gannsen" . Neither piece of text appears anywhere else in his life. The current working interpretation is that both are clerk's-pen distortions of a German-language original: "Freundburg" = phonetic guess at Trendelburg · "L." = first letter of his German baptismal name (likely Ludwig or Lorenz) later abandoned in favor of the more American "Charles." Together they concentrate the entire German-side candidate-family search inside the Hofgeismar Kirchenkreis , looking for a boy whose first name begins with L. Source 1862 Compiled Service Record (Doc O) · NARA M405 "Freundburg" appears Once · only on the 1862 enlistment "L." initial appears Once · only on the 1862 enlistment Points to Hofgeismar Kirchenkreis search radius · L-names priority (working hypothesis) The "Freundburg, Kurhessen" mystery The 1862 enlistment card records Charles's birthplace as "Freundburg, Kurhessen." No place by that name has ever existed in 19th-century Hesse. The 2020 AncestryProGenealogists journal initially proposed reading it as Freudenberg , a small place near Schierstein on the Rhine — but the Schierstein parish baptism index for the 1830s contained no candidate matching Charles, and that lead was eliminated. The current working interpretation reads "Freundburg" as a Civil-War clerk's phonetic mishearing of Trendelburg , the parish town immediately north of Deisel inside the Hofgeismar Kirchenkreis. ★ Peter Schräder's "Trendel → Freund" reading (Feb 2026 emails) The leading interpretation

b: The Kreichelt & Herweck Connection — Battery B at the Wedding · Charles Gannsen Civil War Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Civil War / The Kreichelt & Herweck Connection ⚜ The Kreichelt & Herweck Connection Battery B at the wedding · 7 January 1866 · St. Marcus Evangelical Church, St. Louis CIVIL WAR · KEY STORY The single most consequential fact about Charles's service ★ At a Glance The single most consequential fact about Charles's Civil War service is that he served alongside the family he would later marry into, and that one of his Battery B comrades stood as a formal witness at the wedding. The 7 January 1866 St. Marcus marriage register entry names four wedding witnesses — and Battery B accounts for at least three people in the wedding party. The civilian-life social network that sustained Charles for the rest of his life was forged in a Missouri artillery battery during the war. Date 7 January 1866 · 18 days after Charles's discharge Battery B at the wedding 3+ veterans Senior witness Adam Herweck (Battery B Sergeant + recruiter) Father-in-law Georg Kreichelt (Battery B veteran) The wedding-party roll call Charles Gannsen himself — enlisted 2 Dec 1862, discharged Sergeant 20 Dec 1865. Adam Herweck — fellow Battery B veteran. Born 16 July 1831 in Lampertheim, Hessen, Germany (parents Peter Herweck × Catharina Margaretha Schollmeier). His NARA M405 Compiled Service Record places him as a Sergeant on detached recruiting service at St. Louis in November–December 1862 — the exact time and place of Charles Gannsen's 2 December 1862 enlistment. The Captain commanding Battery B wrote in a 9 December 1862 letter from New Madrid: "Adam Herweck, of my Company, [is on] recruiting service at St. Louis" — documentary confirmation that Adam recruited Charles into Co. B. Named first in the witness column of the 7 Jan 1866 St. Marcus register entry — the wedding's senior groomsman. Single at Charles's wedding (age 34); married Marguerite Wamsganz 21 July 1868

t: New Madrid, Missouri — Field Posting (Twice) · Charles Gannsen Civil War
d: New Madrid, Missouri — Charles Gannsen's primary field posting, in two tours: Sept – Nov 1863 with Battery B (source of hearing loss + rheumatism per Derr/Meyer affidavits) and 24 April 1864 – 27 March 1865 detached (~11 months). The late-war Rolla / Fort Wyman detachment (27 Mar – 1 Jul 1865, ~3 months) followed.
b: New Madrid, Missouri — Field Posting (Twice) · Charles Gannsen Civil War Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Civil War / Where Charles Served / New Madrid New Madrid, Missouri Field posting (twice) · Sept – Nov 1863 with battery + 24 April 1864 – 27 March 1865 detached · ~14 months total CIVIL WAR Second tour newly resolved May 2026 by NARA mail-order packet ★ At a Glance Charles served two distinct tours at New Madrid, Missouri — the strategic Mississippi River fort captured by Pope's army in March 1862. Tour 1 (Sept – Nov 1863) with Battery B is the source of the hearing-loss and rheumatism disabilities he later claimed (sworn to in 1891 by 1st Sgt. August Derr and Pvt. Christian Meyer , both fellow Battery B veterans). The tour ended with Charles's promotion to Sergeant on 7 November 1863. Tour 2 (24 April 1864 – 27 March 1865, ~11 months) was a long detached service at the same Mississippi River garrison — newly resolved by the May 2026 NARA mail-order packet (PG 09 Card B), correcting an illegible Fold3 reading. After Tour 2 ended on 27 March 1865, Charles transferred to Rolla / Fort Wyman for ~3 months under G.O. No. 82. Tours Two — Sept–Nov 1863 + 24 Apr 1864 – 27 Mar 1865 (~14 months total) Significance Source of hearing loss + rheumatism (Derr/Meyer affidavits) Status Tour 1: with battery · Tour 2: detached Promotion Pvt → Sgt promotion 7 Nov 1863, end of Tour 1 ★ Driving Tour of Civil War Sites of New Madrid (brochure) The official New Madrid Chamber of Commerce visitor brochure — sixteen numbered Civil War sites in town. The page below shows the street-map side with sites 1–8 (the historical museum, the Mississippi River observation deck, the Hurt-Stepp House, Fort Thompson, Hatcher Cemetery, Hatcher House, Sand Hill Cemetery, and the Siege Batteries). Click the image for that PDF, or jump to the site-by-site breakdown below covering sites 9–16. "Driving Tour of Civil War Sites of New Madrid, Missouri" — street-map side (sites 1–8). Published

h: Where Charles Served — Places, Postings & Historical Context
d: Charles spent his entire three-year enlistment within roughly 100 miles of the St. Louis levee. He never marched south to Vicksburg, never went east to fight at Chattanooga, and was already discharged before his battery rode west to the Powder River Expedition. The carded muster rolls (Fold3 / NARA
b: Where Charles Served — Places & Postings · Charles Gannsen Civil War Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Civil War / Where Charles Served Where Charles Served — Places, Postings & Historical Context Battery B, 2nd Missouri Light Artillery · 2 December 1862 – 20 December 1865 CIVIL WAR · 3 YEARS ~100 miles of the St. Louis levee ★ At a Glance Charles spent his entire three-year enlistment within roughly 100 miles of the St. Louis levee . He never marched south to Vicksburg, never went east to fight at Chattanooga, and was already discharged before his battery rode west to the Powder River Expedition . The carded muster rolls (Fold3 / NARA M405, 25 pages) put him on detached service for most of 1863-1864 — at the St. Louis smallpox quarantine, at the Department of the Missouri's adjutant office, and inside St. Louis-area garrison forts. The hypothesis: Charles's literacy and German-English bilingual capability made him too valuable in the city to send to the field. Total stations 3 St. Louis postings + New Madrid (1 sept-nov 1863) Field action New Madrid garrison only · no campaign battles Detached duty Smallpox hospital · A.A.G. office · Marine Hospital Pension disabilities All originate at New Madrid (Sept-Nov 1863) Individual location pages Each major posting in Charles's three-year enlistment has its own dedicated page with overview, detailed service notes, historical context, verbatim muster-card quotes, and (where applicable) a research log: Primary station · 1862–1865 St. Louis Garrison Forts Charles's enlistment, the bulk of his service, and his discharge — 2 Dec 1862 through 20 Dec 1865, with the battery rotating through the ten earthwork forts ringing the city. Detached · 20 Feb – Apr 1863 St. Louis Smallpox Hospital First detached posting after enlistment. ~10 weeks of hazardous nursing / quarantine duty by Special Order of Maj. Gen. Samuel R. Curtis. Field posting (twice) New Madrid, Missouri Two tours at the strategic Mississippi River fort:

t: Service Timeline · Charles Gannsen Civil War & Pension
d: Three years that anchor everything we know about Charles. He enlisted in St. Louis on 2 December 1862 as L. Charles Gannsen, served Battery B of the Missouri 2nd Light Artillery to discharge in December 1865, married Auguste Kreichelt the next month — daughter of Battery B veteran Georg Kreichelt who himself had served an early-war 3-month enlistment in the same unit before Charles arrived, and li
b: Service Timeline · Charles Gannsen Civil War & Pension Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Civil War / Service Timeline Service Timeline — Enlistment to Death 2 December 1862 – 16 December 1903 · 41 years from enlistment to death CIVIL WAR · PENSION · LIFE All cited to primary documents ★ At a Glance Three years that anchor everything we know about Charles. He enlisted in St. Louis on 2 December 1862 as L. Charles Gannsen , served Battery B of the Missouri 2nd Light Artillery to discharge in December 1865, married Auguste Kreichelt the next month — daughter of Battery B veteran Georg Kreichelt who himself had served an early-war 3-month enlistment in the same unit before Charles arrived, and lived on the resulting pension — eventually $12 / month — for the last twelve years of his life. Two primary sources carry the entire factual record: the one-page Compiled Service Record (Doc O) and the 40-page pension file F41-523680269E . Service at a glance Soldier name (as recorded) L. Charles Gannsen Unit 2nd Regiment, Missouri Light Artillery, Battery B Side Union (U.S. Volunteer) Enlisted 2 December 1862, St. Louis · age 23 as recorded (calculated 27 per canonical 26 Jan 1835 birth; conflict-resolution block on Key Evidence Card 8 ) Discharged 20 December 1865 · rank: Private (reduced from Sgt 15 Mar 1865) Total service ~3 years 18 days Rank progression Private → Corporal → Sergeant (7 Nov 1863) → Private (15 Mar 1865, reduced) Birthplace as recorded "Freundburg, Kurhessen " — long read as a clerk's mishearing of Trendelburg Physical description 5'5" · fair complexion · blue eyes · blond hair (1862 enlistment; the 1890 pension declaration later records 5'10" and complexion "light") Pension certificate No. 684,507 (issued November 1891 under the Act of June 27, 1890) Pension rate $12.00 / month (≈ $425–435 / month in 2026 dollars ; ≈$5,100/year — about 30% of a common laborer's annual income in 1891) Disabilities of record Right inguinal hernia · rheumatism ·

t: Smallpox Hospital — Research Log · Charles Gannsen Civil War
d: Smallpox Hospital research log — investigating two open questions about Charles Gannsen's February-April 1863 detached service at the St. Louis Smallpox Hospital: did he serve as a nurse after a sickness, and where was the original mainland facility?
b: Smallpox Hospital — Research Log · Charles Gannsen Civil War Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Civil War / Where Charles Served / Smallpox Hospital / Research Log Smallpox Hospital — Research Log Investigating two open questions: did Charles serve as a nurse after a smallpox sickness, and where was the original mainland facility? CIVIL WAR Last updated 6 May 2026 ★ At a Glance Charles's hospital sequence has two distinct events in winter 1862–1863, both documented on the carded record and corroborated by the pension file: (1) a sickness admission to the Regimental Hospital from 22 December 1862 — only 20 days after his enlistment — and (2) a detached service assignment to the Smallpox Hospital from 20 February 1863 through ~early May 1863 under personal Special Order of Maj. Gen. Samuel R. Curtis. Question 1 — was the Smallpox-Hospital detail a recovered-patient-as-nurse role? The sickness-then-staff sequence is now established (correcting an earlier reading on this site that missed the Dec 22 sickness annotation). What is NOT established is whether the original Dec 1862 illness was specifically smallpox — the muster card says only "Sick in Regt Hospital," not naming the disease. The patient-first-nurse-after hypothesis is therefore PARTIALLY SUPPORTED (sequence yes; specific smallpox-immunity rationale still unconfirmed). Question 2 — which facility for the detached service? Most-likely the St. Louis city Smallpox Hospital on Quarantine Island (renamed Arsenal Island in 1866), in the Mississippi River opposite the U.S. Arsenal at Second & Arsenal Streets — not the mainland City Hospital at Lafayette & 14th, and not Sunflower Island near Alton (a separate Alton-prison facility upstream). Status as of 6 May 2026 (revised) Q1 — patient-first-nurse-after hypothesis: PARTIALLY SUPPORTED. User correction (6 May 2026 evening): the Nov–Dec 1862 muster card (NARA PG 6 right card / Fold3 PG 21) explicitly records "Sick in Regt Hospital since Dec 22, 1862" unde

t: St. Louis Smallpox Hospital — Detached Service · Charles Gannsen Civil War
d: St. Louis Smallpox Hospital — Charles Gannsen's first detached posting, 20 February through ~early May 1863, by Special Order of Maj. Gen. Samuel R. Curtis. ~10–11 weeks as hospital staff (not as a patient), most likely at the city Quarantine Hospital on Quarantine Island in the Mississippi River.
b: St. Louis Smallpox Hospital — Detached Service · Charles Gannsen Civil War Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Civil War / Where Charles Served / St. Louis Smallpox Hospital St. Louis Smallpox Hospital First detached posting · 20 February – ~early May 1863 · ~10–11 weeks · by Special Order of Maj. Gen. Samuel R. Curtis CIVIL WAR Hospital STAFF (per cards) · location: most likely Quarantine Island ★ At a Glance Charles's first five months of Civil War service were dominated by hospital settings — first as a patient , then as staff at a different (smallpox-quarantine) hospital. (1) Patient at the Regimental Hospital from 22 December 1862 — only 20 days after his enlistment — illness not specified on the muster card. (2) Detached service at a St. Louis Smallpox Hospital from 20 February through ~early May 1863 (~10–11 weeks) under personal Special Order of Maj. Gen. Samuel R. Curtis . The cards uniformly use the staff-assignment idiom for the Smallpox Hospital phase ("on det. service at" / "attached to") with Curtis as issuing authority — Charles was assigned as hospital staff, not as a smallpox patient at that facility. Whether the original Dec 1862 sickness was itself smallpox (which would make the Smallpox Hospital staff detail a recovered-immune-nurse role) is undetermined by the carded record. Most-likely Smallpox-Hospital location: the city Quarantine Hospital on Quarantine Island (renamed Arsenal Island in 1866), in the Mississippi River opposite the U.S. Arsenal at Second & Arsenal Streets. Patient period 22 Dec 1862 – ~late Jan 1863 (Regt Hospital, illness not specified) Staff period 20 Feb – ~9 May 1863 (~10–11 wks at Smallpox Hospital) Authority for staff detail Special Order, Maj. Gen. Samuel R. Curtis Most likely facility Quarantine Hospital · Quarantine Island, Mississippi River What a Civil War Mississippi-theater hospital looked like No photographs of the St. Louis Quarantine Hospital itself are known to survive — Quarantine Island was wash

t: St. Louis Garrison Forts — Primary Station · Charles Gannsen Civil War
d: St. Louis garrison forts — Charles Gannsen's primary Civil War station. Enlisted 2 December 1862, served the bulk of his three-year enlistment with Battery B at the forts ringing St. Louis, mustered out 30 August 1865, discharged 20 December 1865.
b: St. Louis Garrison Forts — Primary Station · Charles Gannsen Civil War Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Civil War / Where Charles Served / St. Louis Garrison Forts St. Louis Garrison Forts Primary station · 2 Dec 1862 – 20 Dec 1865 · the bulk of Charles's three-year enlistment CIVIL WAR Multiple sub-postings inside St. Louis ★ At a Glance St. Louis was Charles's primary station throughout his three-year Civil War enlistment. He enlisted at St. Louis on 2 December 1862 (age 23) and was discharged here on 20 December 1865 . Most of his service ran inside the St. Louis garrison-forts system that ringed the city, with the battery rotating between specific forts and providing artillery support to the Department of the Missouri. Punctuated by three out-of-city detached postings (Smallpox Hospital briefly in early 1863; New Madrid in fall 1863 and again in spring 1864; Fort Wyman / Rolla March – November 1865), but the through-line was St. Louis. Period 2 Dec 1862 – 20 Dec 1865 (with detachments) Status With battery (1st Battery B → 2d Battery B) Strategic role Garrison defense of Union logistical capital Significance Enlistment, marriage, muster-out — the anchor of his Union service The garrison forts and the Union's St. Louis military complex The "St. Louis garrison" wasn't a single fort but a system: ten numbered earthwork forts (Fort No. 1 through No. 10) ringing the western approaches to the city, plus the federal St. Louis Arsenal on the riverfront and the post at Jefferson Barracks seven miles south. Battery B rotated through this network on garrison duty. No photographs of Fort No. 1–10 are known to survive — the period visual record is dominated by Frank Leslie's and other illustrated newspapers, plus the surviving arsenal and barracks structures. "Fortifications at St. Louis, Missouri" — period engraving from a sketch by war artist Henri Lovie . A small Union outpost on the western approaches to the city, with one cannon visible, the U.S. flag fly

d: The St. Marcus marriage register entry for Charles Gannsen × Auguste Kreichelt — image 72 of the 1865-1867 register at FamilySearch — lists four witnesses to the right of the bride and groom's origin information ("aus Deifse, Kreis Cassel, Churhessen, und Clausthal, Königr. Hannover"). The pastor's
b: The Four Wedding Witnesses — St. Marcus, 7 Jan 1866 · Charles Gannsen Civil War Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Civil War / The Four Wedding Witnesses The Four Wedding Witnesses St. Marcus Marriage Register · 7 January 1866 · Pastor G.W. Wall officiating CIVIL WAR · MARRIAGE 18 days after Charles's discharge ★ At a Glance The St. Marcus marriage register entry for Charles Gannsen × Auguste Kreichelt — image 72 of the 1865-1867 register at FamilySearch — lists four witnesses to the right of the bride and groom's origin information ("aus Deifse, Kreis Cassel, Churhessen , und Clausthal, Königr. Hannover"). The pastor's signature line below them reads Cop: G.W. Wall . Of the four, one is a confirmed Battery B comrade (Adam Herweck), one is from a recurring Gannsen-baptism family (Anna Goetz), and two have not yet been individually identified. Date 7 January 1866 · 18 days after Charles's 20 Dec 1865 discharge Source St. Marcus Marriage Register 1865-1867, image 72 of 246, entry No. 676 FS image group 007769184 Officiant Pastor G.W. Wall The four witnesses, in register order ⚜ Senior witness · Battery B Adam Herweck Named first in the witness column. Charles's Battery B comrade and most likely his recruiter into the unit (per the 9 Dec 1862 Captain's letter from New Madrid). Born 16 July 1831 Lampertheim, Hessen. Same rank trajectory as Charles. See the dedicated Adam Herweck profile for the full deep-dive. FamilySearch K4DW-PXM Open ID · multiple Philipp Webers Philipp Weber Second witness. Surname & given name common in St. Louis German community of the 1860s; specific identification not yet pinned down (multiple Philipp Webers in 1860 St. Louis directories — one a brewer, one a labourer, one a butcher). No FamilySearch tree person yet matched ★ Recurring Gannsen-baptism family Anna Goetz Third witness — likely the senior bridesmaid. The Götz / Goetz family appears at multiple Gannsen child baptisms — Maria Agnes Götz sponsored Maria Josephine Gannsen

d: The project's load-bearing claims on one screen — confidence, primary source, and the specific evidence that would change each claim. The one page a peer reviewer can read to assess what the investigation is willing to defend.
b: Claims Dashboard — Charles Research Skip to main content Skip to main content Charles Research / Claims Dashboard Claims Dashboard Every load-bearing claim of the investigation on one screen — what the project is willing to defend as Confirmed , Working , or Open ; the primary source for each; and the specific evidence that would change it. ★ New in v1.1.0 · 29 May 2026 This Claims Dashboard is new in v1.1.0. So is the worked-example Andreas Konze identity disentanglement on Germany & Origins, the Doc A–O crosswalk anchoring the 1-day Doc A vs Doc N death-date discrepancy, and a pre-deploy maintenance harness that gates every release on canonical-claim and internal-link checks. See errata · 2026 for the change history or changelog for the dated log. A peer reviewer's question — "what, exactly, do you believe and on what basis?" — answered in one table. Every row links to the evidence page that supports it and (where applicable) the errata entry that records its promotion history. 5 Confirmed 9 Working 1 Open 1 Interpretive 16 Load-bearing claims Confirmed primary-source attestation; multiple independent corroborants Working strong convergent evidence; single-source or pending corroboration Open no specific candidate identified; documented search ground Interpretive reading of motive or context; consistent with the evidence, not a falsifiable fact claim · See the full confidence taxonomy for promotion criteria. Load-bearing claims Claim Confidence Primary source What would change it Identity — who Charles was The American-life identity spine: the 1862 enlistee “Charles Gannsen” ≡ the 1875 baptism informant ≡ the 1903 decedent “Charles Johnson” — one man across the U.S. record set. This is the part of the identity equation carried by a continuous American documentary chain. It does not by itself name the German-side surname or origin — those are the separate Working claim below. Confirmed Pension file F41-523680269E + St. Marcus parish registers + 1900 c

d: How descendants and researchers can contribute findings, corrections, primary-source leads, or family-tree additions to the Charles Research project.
b: Contribute or correct — Charles Research Skip to main content Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Contribute Contribute or correct If you have something to add, correct, or contradict — here’s how to get it into the research. This site is a working document. Descendants, researchers, archivists, and curious readers are all encouraged to push back. Every conclusion on the site is held in an explicit Confirmed / Working / Open / Eliminated state precisely so that new evidence can move things between states without rewriting the surrounding case. Send everything to: jedsjohnson@gmail.com Replies in English or German both fine. Please put “Charles Research —” at the start of your subject line. Four contribution pathways If you are a descendant or family member Add or correct your branch of the family The Family Tree tab and the All people index show ~80 named individuals across Charles’s descendants and his St. Marcus / Kreichelt social network. If your line is not represented, or a date / name / relationship is wrong, please send: Your relationship to Charles (e.g. “great-great-granddaughter via Erwin Carl Konsen / Charles Johnson Jr.”). Names, dates, places — with sources where possible. A FamilySearch profile ID (e.g. LZXC-6GB ) is ideal but not required. Any photographs, documents, or family stories you would be willing to share. The September 2025 discovery of the Charles Sr. portrait is the standing example of how much one descendant photograph can change the project. If you are a genealogist or local historian Submit a primary-source lead or a candidate-family elimination The Open Questions page lists the four primary sources still unrecovered (Charles’s German baptismal entry, his 1859 trans-Atlantic passenger manifest, the Hessian Auswanderer-Beleg , and the 1855 Stammrolle entry). The parish map shows every Hessian parish that has been searched and the result. Useful contributions: Pointers to a parish, register, or archival series w

d: The collaboration trail between Jed Johnson and German genealogist Peter Schräder of Trendelburg — every research letter, finding, and analytical breakthrough.
b: Schräder Correspondence — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Schräder Correspondence Schräder Correspondence The collaboration trail with the German-side researcher. The German-side primary research is led by Peter Schräder , an independent genealogist based in Trendelburg, Hesse. Their joint reasoning across the Konze-Kontzen surname pool, the parish complex of Churhessen, and the “Freundburg, Kurhessen” phonetic decoding is documented in a long-running correspondence dating from late 2025. The full thread lives on the home-page Schräder Correspondence tab ; this page indexes the standalone milestones. 26 February 2026 The “Freundburg, Kurhessen” phonetic decoding Peter’s original observation that the otherwise-meaningless place-name on Charles’s 1862 enlistment register is best decoded as Trendelburg — the German Tr- cluster heard as English Fr- , the syllable -endel- normalised to the familiar German Freund- . This is the geographic anchor of the working hypothesis. 7 April 2026 Andreas Konze identity disentanglement Peter’s analysis of the “multiple Andreas Konze” problem in the Mainz / Trendelburg surname pool. Read the full reply . For background on Peter’s role in the project and the terms of the collaboration, see About this project . For the latest open question Peter is investigating, see Open Questions .

b: Peter Schräder Correspondence Timeline — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Schräder Correspondence / Peter's Email Timeline Peter Schräder · Correspondence Timeline Active research phase: August 2025 – present · 100+ emails ONGOING — multi-year collaboration Last updated 3 May 2026 ★ Who Peter is Peter Schräder is a Hessian genealogist who has been the principal collaborator on this investigation. The active research phase began in August 2025 and has run continuously since. This page captures the chronology of the back-and-forth so that any prior topic can be located quickly. Peter's working operating principles Geographic constraint. Pre-train Hesse: an 1859 emigrant from the north would travel Trendelburg → Karlshafen on the Weser → Bremen, NOT to Le Havre. Filters U.S.-arrival records. Phonetic constraint. Charles answered an English-speaking clerk in German. "Trendelburg" became "Freundburg" / "Trendelb-urg" / "Deisse." "Konsen" became "Gonnson" / "Gannsen." Many surname matches require allowing for this distortion. "Someone has to be Charles." Peter's recurring methodological reminder. Each elimination is progress; the real Charles still exists in the records somewhere, by some name. Dead-end discipline. Peter is rigorous about confirming eliminations through to a death record or alternative U.S. trace, not just absence of evidence. b: Zusammenfassung — Charles-Forschung Skip to main content ← Charles-Forschung / Zusammenfassung (DE) Zusammenfassung Eine formelle Kurzfassung der Untersuchung — Forschungsfrage, Methode, Quellen, Arbeitsergebnis und anerkannte Lücken. Druckfertig; für Zitate in akademischen und genealogischen Arbeiten geeignet. English version ⤓ PDF herunterladen (englisch) Forschungsobjekt Charles Konzen (in den US-Akten auch als Gannsen, Gonsen, Konsen, Jansen, Conson, Johnson erfasst) — 26. Januar 1835 – 19. Dezember 1903. Verfasser Jed Johnson, mit Peter Schräder aus Trendelburg als deutschem Genealogen. Untersuchungszeitraum März 2026 – heute (laufend). Vorarbeiten 2017–2020. Stand 29. Mai 2026 (v1.1.0; diese deutsche Fassung übersetzt v1.0.0 vom 8. Mai 2026, mit auf Stand v1.1.0 aktualisierter Namensspur) Zitierweise Siehe Methodik · Zitiervorschlag (englisch). Hinweis: Für die neuesten Ergebnisse (Mai 2026) siehe die englische Claims Dashboard — sie enthält v1.1.0-Material (Andreas-Konze-Entflechtung, Doc A–O-Crosswalk, Wartungs-Harness), das in dieser deutschen Übersetzung von v1.0.0 noch nicht abgebildet ist. Forschungsfrage Die Untersuchung gilt der deutschen Herkunft von Charles Konzen , einem in Hessen geborenen Sergeant der Battery B, 2nd Missouri Light Artillery, der 1859 in die Vereinigten Staaten auswanderte, von Dezember 1862 bis Dezember 1865 in der Unionsarmee diente, am 7. Januar 1866 in der St.-Marcus-Gemeinde (deutsch-evangelisch) in St. Louis Auguste Kreichelt heiratete, zwischen 1866 und 1880 sieben im Kirchenbuch verzeichnete Kinder hatte, 1891 eine bundesstaatliche Pension wegen kriegsbedingten Rheumas erhielt und am 19. Dezember 1903 in St. Louis verstarb. 1 Zwei Leitfragen gliedern die Arbeit: Wie lautete Charles' deutscher Familienname vor seinen vielfachen amerikanischen Schreibweisen? und In welchem hessischen Kirchenbuch ist seine Taufe verzeichnet? English version Quellen & Archive Archion Digitalisierungs- und Recherche-Plattform der evangelischen Landeskirchen Deutschlands; im Projekt zentrale Quelle für hessische Kirchenbücher (Trendelburg, Deisel, Friedrichsfeld, Helmarshausen, Hofgeismar). Arcinsys Online-Recherchesystem der hessischen Staatsarchive; im Projekt für Bestandsanfragen zu Auswanderer-Belegen und Stammrollen verwendet. LAGIS Landesgeschichtliches Informationssystem Hessen — landeshistorisches Quellen- und Karten-Portal mit Ortslexika, Pfarreien-Verzeichnissen und Stadtarchiv-Findmitteln. NARA National Archives and Records Administration der USA. Im Projekt: Compiled Service Record (M405) und vollständige Pensionsakte (F41). FamilySearch Kostenfreie Genealogie-Plattform der Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints; im Projekt für US-Personenstandsakten und Stammbaum-Verknüpfung. Find a Grave Crowd-getragene Grabstätten-Datenbank; im Projekt für die Identifikation von Old Saint Marcus Cemetery, St. Louis. Akten- und Registerarten Kirchenbuch (KB) Pfarreiliches Personenstandsregister vor Einführung der staatlichen Standesämter (in Hessen-Kassel zumeist 1. Januar 1875); enthält Tauf-, Trau- und Sterbeeinträge. Auswanderer-Beleg Behördlicher Genehmigungsschein für die Auswanderung; in Hessen-Kassel im 19. Jh. dem zuständigen Landratsamt vorzulegen. Bei Charles bislang nicht aufgefunden. Stammrolle Militärisches Verzeichnis der wehrpflichtigen Männer eines Jahrgangs; relevant für Auswanderungs-Spurensuche, da Auswanderung ohne Erfüllung der Wehrpflicht aktenkundig ist. d: The project's genetic evidence in full: a FamilyTreeDNA Big Y-700 result (haplogroup I2-M223, terminal subclade I-Y38863) and AncestryDNA and MyHeritage autosomal estimates — what each does and does not show about Charles Konzen's Hessian origin and surname.
b: DNA Evidence — Charles Research Skip to main content Charles Research / DNA Evidence DNA Evidence What genetic testing adds to a documentary investigation — a FamilyTreeDNA Big Y-700 on Charles's direct paternal line and autosomal estimates from two vendors (AncestryDNA and MyHeritage) on a descendant — and, just as important, the limits of what each can show. Genealogy and genetics answer different questions. The records reconstruct who Charles was; DNA tests whether the biological line agrees. This page reports both tests in full, including the matches they did not produce, so a reader can weigh the genetic evidence against the documentary conclusions on the claims dashboard . Bottom line. The DNA to date is consistent with Charles's documented German origin but does not confirm either the parish (Hesse / Trendelburg) or the surname (Konzen). The Y-DNA places his strict paternal line in a young (~1450 CE) subclade whose nearest relatives are — so far without exception — Ashkenazi-Jewish lineages — pointing to a possible Ashkenazi-Jewish origin in the deep male line. On the strength of only two-to-three tested members this is held as a working line of inquiry, not a conclusion ; it concerns deep ancestry that sits beneath , and does not contradict, the documented Hessian-Evangelical family. The autosomal estimates — from two independent vendors, AncestryDNA and MyHeritage, in agreement — are heavily Germanic but, taken from a descendant, average every ancestral line and cannot single out Charles's. No match yet ties the line to a documented Konze family, though a German-resident paternal cluster at 3rd-cousin depth (MyHeritage, June 2026) is under active correspondence — not yet assigned between Charles's line and Auguste Kreichelt's. The origin and surname conclusions therefore remain Working — see the confidence taxonomy — and the single highest-yield next step is to recruit a documented Konze male from the Trendelburg–Deisel area to Y-test.
