b: Errata & Revisions — Charles Research Skip to main content Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Errata Errata & Revisions A reverse-chronological list of prior assertions on this site that have been revised, corrected, or withdrawn. Each entry names the original claim, the corrected reading, and the evidence that prompted the revision. The Confirmed / Working / Open / Eliminated taxonomy elsewhere on the site is the live state of the work; this page is its history. Genealogical claims revise. The eight-attestation surname trail recorded here was not assembled in a single sitting — it is the survival, after thirteen candidate families and several mistaken readings, of what remained when each new piece of evidence was confronted with the prior interpretation. We list the revisions formally so that anyone citing an earlier version of this site, an earlier email from one of the authors, or an earlier Family Tree pull-down can locate the change and the warrant. Entries are tagged major when the revision changes a public-facing conclusion, minor when it corrects a transcription, date, or attribution, and withdrawn when a hypothesis was retired without a replacement. 2026 Moderate 19 June 2026 · transcription / image mismatch · the “Doc O” source page transcribed the wrong card — its Age field is blank The Military Record (Doc O) page transcribed “age 23,” “Company G,” and “Freundburg, Kurhessen” — but the image actually displayed is a Missouri AGO service-abstract card whose Age field is blank and which names no birthplace. t: Card 1 — Trendelburg burial register 220096 · K-section — Charles Research
d: The convergent-evidence basis for the May 2026 surname conclusion: KONZEN. Trendelburg parish surname-pool documented continuously from 1773; the parish-internal cross-reference note 'siehe auch K. Contze, Cortze, Cordes, Contz' establishes orthographic interchangeability across K- and C-initial forms. The eight distinct American Charles-family renderings all converge on this German source.
b: Card 1 — Trendelburg burial register 220096 · K-section — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Key Evidence / Card 1 Card 1 · ★ Project conclusion Trendelburg burial register 220096 · K-section 1773–1824 · Trendelburg, Churhessen The convergent-evidence basis for the May 2026 surname conclusion: KONZEN. Trendelburg parish surname-pool documented continuously from 1773; the parish-internal cross-reference note ’siehe auch K. Contze, Cortze, Cordes, Contz’ establishes orthographic interchangeability across K- and C-initial forms. The eight distinct American Charles-family renderings all converge on this German source. The Trendelburg parish burial register Kirchenbuch 220096 (1773–1824), accessed via Archion.de, contains the K-section family pool from which Charles Konzen most likely descends. Six specific Kontzen burials are documented (Philipp Kontzen 1773; Johann Henrich Kontzen 1773; Johann Friedrich Kontzen 1795; Andreas Kontzen I and II in the 1795 marriage / baptism on the same register image; Ludwig Kontzen 1824). Three Kontze burials add weight (Arnold Philipp 1780, Andreas 1800, child Johannes 1808). Two Contze primary entries (Andreas Contze 1800; Johann Christoph Contze 1808) document the C-initial variant. The parish’s own clerk, in the burial-index alphabetical apparatus, wrote the cross-reference note “siehe auch K. Contze, Cortze, Cordes, Contz” — establishing that the parish itself treated the K-, C-, and -en variants as orthographically interchangeable. This is what makes the eight American Charles-family renderings (Gonnson, Gansen, Gonsen, Konsen, Gonson, Jansen, Konzen, Conson) all readable as descendants of one German surname. Convergent-evidence reading: the 1875 St. Marcus baptism of Erwin Carl Konsen (Card 6) preserves the Hessian -en form; the 1890 death record of his sister Auguste reads “Auguste Somers geb Konzen ” — the full Hessian z-form, fifteen years later, by a different scribe. t: Card 2 — Civil War enlistment record · 'Freundburg, Kurhessen' — Charles Research
d: The first mention of a town and state anywhere in Charles's documentary record. Peter Schräder's 26 February 2026 phonetic re-reading decoded 'Freundburg' as a clerk's mishearing of Trendelburg. The working geographic anchor of the entire investigation.
b: Card 2 — Civil War enlistment record · 'Freundburg, Kurhessen' — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Key Evidence / Card 2 Card 2 · 1st Kurhessen reference Civil War enlistment record · 'Freundburg, Kurhessen' 2 December 1862 · St. Louis The first mention of a town and state anywhere in Charles’s documentary record. Peter Schräder’s 26 February 2026 phonetic re-reading decoded ’Freundburg’ as a clerk’s mishearing of Trendelburg. The working geographic anchor of the entire investigation. 🔍 Enlistment register · 2 Dec 1862 · NARA Compiled Service Record (M405, file G11-422819443E) Open scan in new tab ↗ Loading high-resolution scan… Enlistment register · 2 Dec 1862. Battery B, 2nd Missouri Light Artillery enlistment register entry, 2 December 1862, naming Charles's birthplace as 'Freundburg, Kurhessen' — decoded by Peter Schräder as Trendelburg. On 2 December 1862, Charles enlisted in St. Louis with Battery B of the 2nd Missouri Light Artillery (Union). The enlistment register names his birthplace as “Freundburg, Kurhessen” — a town that does not exist on any Hessian map. The phonetic decoding originally proposed by Peter Schräder in his 26 February 2026 letter reads the entry as a clerk’s mishearing of Trendelburg . The German Tr- cluster (an unfamiliar onset to an English-speaking American clerk) heard as Fr- ; the meaningless syllable -endel- normalised to the familiar German word Freund- (“friend”); the trailing -burg preserved exactly. This decoding has been reaffirmed by Schräder in subsequent correspondence (6 December 2026). The political designation “Kurhessen” on this record is the first of four explicit Kurhessen / Hesse-Kassel references across Charles’s U.S. documentary record (the other three: 1866 marriage register “Churhessen” on Card 3; 1866 firstborn naming on Card 4; 1868 naturalization renouncing the King of Prussia on Card 5). Surname recorded as Gonnson. With Card 2’s Freundburg decoding, this triangulates the answer to a 5×5 km square inside the Kreis Hofgeismar. 🔍 St. Marcus marriage register · 7 Jan 1866 · St. Marcus Evangelical Church marriage register, page 134–135, entry No. 676 Open scan in new tab ↗ Loading high-resolution scan… St. Marcus marriage register · 7 Jan 1866. St. Marcus Evangelical Church marriage register, 7 January 1866, recording 'Carl Gonnson' aus Deisse, Kreis Cassel, Churhessen, marrying Auguste Kreichelt — Pastor Wendelin Wall officiating. St. Marcus Evangelical Church marriage register, A.D. 1866 — page 134-135, entry No. 676 (running entry №4): the marriage of Carl Gonnson & Auguste Kreichelt , recorded 7 January 1866 by Pastor Georg Wendelin Wall (Württemberger; St. Marcus 1850–1866). Charles’s stated origin: “aus Deisse, Kreis Cassel, Churhessen” — the village of Deisel in the Trendelburg parish complex, inside the Electorate of Hesse-Kassel. This is the entry that ties the May 2026 Konzen surname conclusion to a specific Hessian territorial reference. Triangulated with Card 2’s “Freundburg” (= Trendelburg) decoding, the answer is bounded to a 5 km × 5 km square inside the Kreis Hofgeismar. The surname rendering “Gonnson” here is the first of eight distinct American captures of Charles’s German surname (Gonnson 1866 · Gansen 1870 · Gonsen 1872 · Konsen 1875 · Gonson 1880 · Jansen 1881 · Konzen 1890 · Conson 1896). All eight converge on the Trendelburg Kontzen / Contzen pool. Related links Full card on the homepage Auguste Kreichelt biography Deisel village page ← Card 2 Civil War e

Pastor Wendelin Wall. Seven children, every baptism recorded at St. Marcus German Evangelical Church , the German-Evangelical congregation on DeKalb Street descended from the Reformed-Lutheran tradition of Kurhessen / Hesse-Kassel: Friedrich Wilhelm (1866-1867) · Maria Josephine (1868-1890) · Katherine Carolina (1870-1949) · Auguste (1872-1890) · Louise (1873-1947) · Erwin Carl Konsen (1875-1950) · Bertha (1880-1959). The seven baptismal entries collectively yield five of the eight distinct surname renderings: Gonnson (Wall) · Gansen (Braschler) · Gonsen (Braschler again, two years later, different vowel — the “Pastor Braschler smoking gun” that proves the spelling came from Charles’s mouth, not the pastor’s pen) · Konsen (Hoffmann) · Jansen (Nollau). The sponsorship pattern is structurally diagnostic. Across all seven baptisms — fifteen named sponsors across seventeen sponsorship slots — not a single sponsor traces to Charles’s side of the family . Every kin sponsor is from Auguste’s Kreichelt-Albrecht-Pillmann-Mincke side. German-Lutheran baptismal sponsorship in thi

t: Card 5 — U.S. Naturalization record — Charles Research
d: Charles's formal U.S. citizenship grant. The renunciation language is politically loaded: he formally renounces 'the King of Prussia', not the Elector of Hesse — confirming that he both knew his homeland of Kurhessen had been absorbed by Prussia in 1866 and made the formal civic acknowledgment of that fact.
b: Card 5 — U.S. Naturalization record — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Key Evidence / Card 5 Card 5 · 4th Kurhessen reference U.S. Naturalization record 21 October 1868 · St. Louis Charles’s formal U.S. citizenship grant. The renunciation language is politically loaded: he formally renounces ’the King of Prussia’, not the Elector of Hesse — confirming that he both knew his homeland of Kurhessen had been absorbed by Prussia in 1866 and made the formal civic acknowledgment of that fact. Charles’s definitive U.S. naturalization is recorded on 21 October 1868 in St. Louis — three years after his discharge from Battery B, nine years after his 1859 immigration. The document records him as Charles Gannsen (the Anglicised form he kept under the Civil War clerk’s hand) and contains the standard renunciation language. The renunciation is politically loaded. Charles formally renounces “the King of Prussia” , not the Elector of Hesse. By autumn 1868 his homeland of Kurhessen no longer existed: Prussian armies had overrun Hesse-Kassel in summer 1866 and annexed the Electorate; Friedrich Wilhelm I, the only sovereign Charles had ever known as an adult and the namesake of his firstborn son in 1866 (Card 4), had been deposed two years earlier. Charles renouncing the King of Prussia rather than the Elector of Hesse confirms that he both knew his homeland had been absorbed and made the formal civic acknowledgment of that fact. This is the fourth Kurhessen reference in Charles’s U.S. documentary record (after the 1862 enlistment, the 1866 marriage register, and the 1866 firstborn naming). It is independently corroborated by the 1900 federal census, which records Charles as “naturalized.” Related links Full card on the homepage Imaged source ← Card 4 Seven St. Marcus baptisms · 1866–1880 Card 6 → 1875 St. Marcus baptism · Erwin Carl Konsen

d: The strongest direct surname link between Charles in St. Louis and the Konze line in Hesse: Pastor Hoffmann recorded his sixth child as Erwin Carl Konsen, preserving the German -en form. The 1890 daughter's death record reads Konsen a second time, fifteen years later — establishing two-record persistence by two different scribes.
b: Card 6 — 1875 St. Marcus baptism · Erwin Carl Konsen — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Key Evidence / Card 6 Card 6 · Konsen attestation · linguistic time capsule 1875 St. Marcus baptism · Erwin Carl Konsen 27 August 1875 · Pastor Julius Hoffmann The strongest direct surname link between Charles in St. Louis and the Konze line in Hesse: Pastor Hoffmann recorded his sixth child as Erwin Carl Konsen, preserving the German -en form. The 1890 daughter’s death record reads Konsen a second time, fifteen years later — establishing two-record persistence by two different scribes. 🔍 St. Marcus baptism · Erwin Carl Konsen · 27 Aug 1875 · St. Marcus Evangelical Church baptism register, Pastor Julius Hoffmann Open scan in new tab ↗ Loading high-resolution scan… St. Marcus baptism · Erwin Carl Konsen · 27 Aug 1875. 1875 St. Marcus baptism register entry for Erwin Carl Konsen, Charles & Auguste's sixth child — Pastor Julius Hoffmann recording the surname as 'Konsen', the older Hessian -en form. Pastor Julius Hoffmann , Braschler’s successor at St. Marcus from May 1875, recorded Charles’s sixth child’s baptism on 27 August 1875: “Erwin Carl Konsen · Geburt: 27. Aug. 1875 · Taufe: 3. Oct. 1875 · Eltern: Carl Konsen u. d. ihm Ehefrau Auguste geb. Kreichelt · Pathen: Erwin Sproul, der Vater des Kindes.” Hoffmann recorded the surname here as KONSEN — preserving the German-Hessian form 16 years after Charles emigrated. The closest German precedent is Kontzen / Contzen : only the soft t in the tz cluster collapses to s ; the terminal -en is identical. Near-direct match. The Hessian -en form recurs. Fifteen years later, on the 1890 death record of Erwin Carl’s sister Auguste, the maiden-name attestation reads “Auguste Somers geb Konzen” — the full Hessian z-form, by a different scribe (reading confirmed in the 17 May 2026 re-examination). Two-record persistence by two different scribes — Konsen 1875 with an s, Konzen 1890 with a z — is direct evid

t: Card 7 — Civil War Pension File F41-523680269E — Charles Research
d: The richest U.S. document — birthdate, addresses, witnesses. No family-side affidavits — Charles emigrated alone. Page-by-page audit confirms: the pension file does not contain any Charles-self-stated birthplace declaration; its Kurhessen attribution is solely via the embedded 1862 service record.
b: Card 7 — Civil War Pension File F41-523680269E — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Key Evidence / Card 7 Card 7 · Pension file · seven affidavits Civil War Pension File F41-523680269E 1890–1903 · NARA The richest U.S. document — birthdate, addresses, witnesses. No family-side affidavits — Charles emigrated alone. Page-by-page audit confirms: the pension file does not contain any Charles-self-stated birthplace declaration; its Kurhessen attribution is solely via the embedded 1862 service record. The full pension file (NARA F41-523680269E) is the richest U.S. document the project has obtained: pension applications, supporting medical opinions, claim agent correspondence, and seven witness affidavits collected by St. Louis claim agent Fred. W. Fout in 1889–1891. No family-side affidavits . Of the seven personal witnesses now identified, four were Missouri Light Artillery veterans — the same Battery B alumni network. The narrative phrasings recur word-for-word across affidavits, identifying Fout’s drafting hand. This pattern is the basis for the project’s “Pension Mutual-Aid Network” theme: a coordinated mutual-aid filing run through a single Light Artillery alumni network rather than through Charles’s family. The pension file’s Kurhessen reference is solely via the embedded 1862 Compiled Service Record bundled into the claim as proof of service. The pension file therefore reproduces, but does not independently re-attest, the Kurhessen identification on Card 2. Bonus finding from page 13: Charles’s own 1898 affidavit names Pastor “Geo. W. Wall” as the officiant of his 1866 St. Marcus marriage — confirming Wall’s role in his own hand 32 years after the wedding. Related links Full card on the homepage Imaged source Pension Mutual-Aid theme ← Card 6 1875 St. Marcus baptism · Erwin Carl Konsen Card 8 → 1896 St. Marcus death record · 'Auguste Conson'

t: Card 8 — 1896 St. Marcus death record · 'Auguste Conson' — Charles Research
d: The strongest single Charles-self-attestation of his surname. Charles, age 61, gave Auguste's name to Pastor Eilts at the funeral; Eilts wrote what he heard — the C-initial form (Conson) that ties straight back to the Trendelburg Contzen line.
b: Card 8 — 1896 St. Marcus death record · 'Auguste Conson' — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Key Evidence / Card 8 Card 8 · Charles-self-attestation 1896 St. Marcus death record · 'Auguste Conson' 1896 · Pastor Eilt Heeren Eilts The strongest single Charles-self-attestation of his surname. Charles, age 61, gave Auguste’s name to Pastor Eilts at the funeral; Eilts wrote what he heard — the C-initial form (Conson) that ties straight back to the Trendelburg Contzen line. 🔍 St. Marcus death register · Auguste 'Conson' · 1896 · St. Marcus Evangelical Church death register, Pastor Eilt Heeren Eilts Open scan in new tab ↗ Loading high-resolution scan… St. Marcus death register · Auguste 'Conson' · 1896. 1896 St. Marcus death register entry for Auguste Kreichelt — recorded as 'Auguste Conson' by Pastor Eilt Heeren Eilts at Charles's direct dictation, the strongest single Charles-self-attestation of the surname. St. Marcus death register, 1896 entry: the funeral of Auguste Kreichelt Konzen , Charles’s wife. Recorded by Pastor Eilt Heeren Eilts with Charles himself as the surviving informant . The maiden-name attestation reads “Auguste Conson ” — the C-initial form that pairs directly with the Trendelburg parish’s own Contze / Contzen entries (Card 1). This is the project’s strongest single Charles-self-attestation. Charles, age 61, walked into the parish, gave his deceased wife’s name and his own surname directly to Eilts, and Eilts wrote what he heard. The C-initial form preserves the German C -initial documented in the Trendelburg parish itself (the cross-reference note “siehe auch K. Contze, Cortze, Cordes, Contz” plus primary entries Andreas Contze 1800, Johann Christoph Contze 1808), drops the silent t , softens the tz affricate to s , and Anglicizes the suffix to -on . Bookend Charles-self-attestations. Charles is the documented speaker at both ends of the surname trail: at his 1866 marriage (groom giving his name to Pastor Wall →

The SVG is resolution-independent — you can also commission a larger framed print at a copy shop without quality loss. Margins Set browser margins to 6–8 mm for maximum poster area; the poster's own border accounts for the rest. Colour vs. black-and-white Both work. The cream background and warm gold accents reduce ink usage on colour printers; the design retains contrast in greyscale. For a copy shop Download the SVG (button above) and ask for "vector print, 11 × 17 portrait, on heavy stock." Most shops can convert the SVG to a high-resolution PDF on the spot. Notes on the content The poster is the project's single-page distillation: the founding couple at top (Charles and Auguste, with the eight phonetic surname renderings noted under Charles's name); the marriage glyph anchored to 7 January 1866 at St.

h: Johannes Grandjot — verification check on the 1855 emigrant brother
d: Johannes Grandjot (b. 14 Sep 1828 Schöneberg, FS PCXP-H6X) — Schuhmacher who left Schöneberg in August 1855 and arrived NYC 25 Sep 1855 with co-traveler Lisette Grandjot (relationship undocumented). The only confirmed transatlantic emigrant of Carl Ludwig Grandjot's nuclear family.
b: Johannes Grandjot · 1855 emigration — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / 3. Grandjot family / Johannes (sibling verification) Johannes Grandjot — verification check on the 1855 emigrant brother Sibling of focal candidate ★ Carl Ludwig · Schuhmacher · Bremen → NYC · 25 September 1855 VERIFICATION CHECK CLOSED · NOT CHARLES Opened & closed 4 May 2026 ★ At a Glance CLOSED · NOT CHARLES Johannes Grandjot · verification check opened & closed 4 May 2026 Johannes Grandjot (b. 14 Sep 1828 Schöneberg, FS PCXP-H6X ) is the only documented transatlantic emigrant in ★ Carl Ludwig Grandjot 's nuclear family. He left Schöneberg in August 1855 as a registered Schuhmacher (shoemaker), boarded a Bremen-line ship, and arrived in New York on 25 September 1855 traveling with a co-passenger named Lisette Grandjot (relationship to Johannes not stated on the manifest). He appears in the 1860 NY Ward 5 District 2 census as "Jos Grand" alongside one "Mary Grand" (relationship unstated), and his trail then goes cold — apparently with no surviving descendants under the Grandjot surname. Verdict: ★ Carl Ludwig Grandjot was the focal candidate (eliminated 4 March 2026 — stayed in Schöneberg). Johannes is decisively not Charles Gannsen — he was 7 years older, kept the Hugenot surname intact, settled NYC not St. Louis, and is independently documented in 1860 Lower Manhattan with a separate biography. The verification check is closed. Surviving NY Grandjot descendants (Suffolk County) trace to a separate 1882 Schöneberg emigration by Louis Grandgot, not to Johannes. d: Frequently asked questions about the Charles Research project — for descendants, family historians, and casual readers.
b: FAQ — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / FAQ Frequently asked questions For descendants, family historians, and curious readers. Click any question to expand. If your question isn't here, see how to get in touch . This page assumes no prior familiarity with the project. For the full investigation, start at the home page ; for the formal one-page summary, see the Abstract ; for the four signature diagrams, see the Visualize hub . About Charles & the project Who was Charles Konzen? A Hessian-born immigrant who arrived in the United States around 1859, served as a sergeant in Battery B, 2nd Missouri Light Artillery during the U.S. Civil War (1862–1865), married Auguste Kreichelt at St. Marcus German Evangelical Church in St. Louis on 7 January 1866, fathered seven children documented in the parish register between 1866 and 1880, was awarded a federal pension for war-onset rheumatism in 1891, and died in St. Louis on 16 December 1903. He was buried three days later, 19 December 1903, at Old Saint Marcus Cemetery (burial #3393). The first known photograph of him — taken at the Louis Roth studio in St. Louis between 1896 and 1903 — was discovered in September 2025 by a descendant in Independence, Missouri. It is the home-page hero on this site. Why is this project running? What's the open question? Charles never named his exact German parish on any U.S. record. The closest he came was "Deisse, Kreis Cassel, Churhessen " on his 1866 marriage register — phonetically the village of Deisel, in the Kreis Hofgeismar of the former Electorate of Hesse-Kassel. But the specific German parish baptismal entry recording his birth has never been recovered, despite multi-year searches across every digitised parish register in the surrounding 5 km × 5 km area. The two open questions, in plain English, are: (a) what was his actual German surname? and (b) which parish recorded his baptism on or around 26 January 1835? See the Open Questions page for th

d: The political, demographic, and social context of mid-19th-century Hesse-Kassel — the Electorate from which Charles Konzen emigrated in 1859.
b: Hesse-Kassel — Regional Context · Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Germany & Origins / Hesse-Kassel context Hesse-Kassel — Regional Context The political, demographic, and social setting of the Electorate of Hesse-Kassel — the small north-Hessian state from which Charles Konzen emigrated in 1859 — for readers who want the regional history before the genealogy. Charles Konzen's 1866 St. Marcus marriage register names his place of origin as "Deisse, Kreis Cassel, Churhessen ." A modern reader is likely to recognise none of those three terms. This essay supplies the regional context: what Churhessen was, what its mid-nineteenth-century life looked like for an ordinary villager, and why a young Hessian man might have left for the United States in 1859. 1 · A small state with a long name The Electorate of Hesse-Kassel — in German, Kurfürstentum Hessen-Kassel , vernacular Kurhessen , the older orthography Churhessen — was one of the four principal Hessian successor states of the medieval Landgraviate of Hesse. It occupied roughly the northern half of what is today the German federal state of Hesse, with its capital at Cassel (modern Kassel). To its east lay the Kingdom of Hannover and the Duchy of Brunswick; to its west, the Prussian Province of Westphalia; to its south, the smaller Hessian states of Hesse-Darmstadt and Hesse-Homburg, plus a thicket of Thuringian principalities. The name "Hesse-Kassel" distinguishes it from "Hesse-Darmstadt" — both were ruled by collateral branches of the same dynasty. The Electoral title — Kurfürst — was anachronistic by the 1820s. It referred to the right to elect the Holy Roman Emperor, a function that had ceased to exist in 1806 when Napoleon abolished the Empire. The ruler of Hesse-Kassel preserved the title for prestige; in practical political terms he was a sovereign prince of the German Confederation after 1815. The relevant point for genealogy is that "Churhessen" in a parish or marriage re

Eight distinct American renderings of the surname, recorded by at least five independent scribes across eight attestations between 1866 and 1896, all converge on this German source — including the 1890 daughter's-death-record reading Konzen (with z), the single American attestation of the full Hessian z-form. Henrich the younger were half-brothers. d: The 1890 St. Marcus death record of Charles's daughter Auguste reads KONZEN — the single American attestation of the full Hessian z-form documented in the Trendelburg parish complex since 1773. The 1875 Konsen baptism is its phonetic-s sibling. Together the two records bracket the surname trail with K-initial forms, fifteen years apart.
b: The KONZEN Puzzle — Charles Konzen Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Germany & Origins / The KONZEN Puzzle The KONZEN Puzzle The 1890 St. Marcus death record of Charles's daughter Auguste carries the full Hessian z-form Konzen — the only American attestation of the spelling documented in the Trendelburg parish complex since 1773. The 1875 son's baptism reads Konsen , its phonetic-s sibling. Two K-initial records bracket the surname trail, 15 years apart. GERMANY & ORIGINS Surname preservation · two records · 15 years apart ⚠ Schräder verdict (12 Apr 2026 · updated 4 May 2026): Charles is not a legitimate Konze son — only one Konze appears in the Deisel emigration list, and it is not Charles. On 4 May 2026 , the project's strongest single-record candidate — Entry 195 of Deisel KB 218842 — was also closed by Peter Schräder's Kurrent re-reading: the father is Köster , not Konze, and the child died unbaptized. This page exists because two American records (1875 St. Marcus baptism, 1890 daughter's death record) still carry the Hessian K-initial / -en-suffix surname forms Konsen and Konzen . → changelog Working conclusion. Charles's family belongs to the Konze line documented in the Trendelburg parish register (spelled Konze / Kontze there), in the territory Charles himself named on his 1866 St. Louis marriage record (“Deisel, Churhessen”). The family carried the name abroad in its spoken -en form, KONZEN — the dialectal inflection of Konze (“geb. Konzen” = née Konze) — which is the form every American record preserves. The 1890 St. Marcus death record of his daughter Auguste spells it with a z , the only American attestation of the full Hessian z-form. The puzzle is that the specific Hessian baptism record naming Charles himself has not been recovered. Three archival threads remain open and any one could surface the entry. The detailed argument and hypotheses below walk through the evidence chain and the candidates that have been eliminated. 8 American spe

b: Hesse Political History 1835–1903 · Charles Konzen Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Germany & Origins / Hesse Political History · 1835–1903 Hesse Political History · 1835–1903 Three political reorganizations · Electorate → Province → Empire GERMANY & ORIGINS Kurhessen 1835 → German Empire 1903 ★ At a Glance The political map of Charles's homeland was redrawn three times between his birth and his death. He was born a subject of the Kurfürstentum Hessen , an independent state at the centre of the loose German Confederation. He emigrated as one — and within seven years his country had ceased to exist as an independent polity, swallowed up by Prussia in the Austro-Prussian War of 1866. By the time he died, the Hesse he had left was a Prussian province inside the German Empire under Wilhelm II. Hesse during Charles's lifetime — 1835 to 1903 The political map of Charles's homeland was redrawn three times between his birth and his death. He was born a subject of the Kurfürstentum Hessen (Electorate of Hesse), an independent German state at the centre of the loose German Confederation. He emigrated as one — and within seven years his country had ceased to exist as an independent polity, swallowed up by Prussia in the Austro-Prussian War of 1866. By the time he died, the Hesse he had left was a Prussian province inside the German Empire under Wilhelm II. Kurhessen (Electorate of Hesse) 1803 – 1866 Kurfürstentum Hessen · Electorate of Hesse-Kassel Capital: Kassel . Territory included Trendelburg, Deisel, Hofgeismar — Charles's home district. Ruled by Elector Wilhelm II until 1847, then his son Friedrich Wilhelm I (regent from 1831, ruling Elector 1847–1866). A reactionary administration through the 1850s under Minister Hassenpflug; freedom-of-press restrictions and emigration permits were tightly controlled. Currency: Kurhessischer Taler. Member of the German Confederation. Charles is born here in ~Jan 1835 and emigrates from this state in ~1859 . Wikipedia ·

t: Visit Today — Trendelburg, Deisel, Hofgeismar · Charles Konzen
d: A practical guide to visiting Charles's home region in northern Hesse — the walled town of Trendelburg with its hilltop castle, the village of Deisel, the medieval Altstadt of Hofgeismar, the nearby Sababurg, and the genealogy resources at the Hessian State Archive in Marburg. All on the Deutsche Märchenstraße.
b: Visit Today — Trendelburg, Deisel, Hofgeismar · Charles Konzen Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Germany & Origins / Visit Today Visit Today — Trendelburg, Deisel, & Hofgeismar A practical guide to Charles's home region — castles, fairy-tale routes, and where to find the parish records GERMANY & ORIGINS Northern Hesse · Deutsche Märchenstraße ★ At a Glance All three of Charles's home places — Trendelburg , Deisel , and Hofgeismar — are still living villages on the Deutsche Märchenstraße (German Fairy Tale Route) , about 30 km north of Kassel in northern Hesse. Trendelburg's hilltop castle is a working hotel advertised as the inspiration for the Brothers Grimm's Rapunzel ; Hofgeismar's medieval Altstadt and Rathaus are intact; Deisel is today an Ortsteil of Stadt Trendelburg. The Trendelburg–Hofgeismar–Sababurg cluster is one of the route's densest, and is the natural base for parish-record genealogy in the Hofgeismar Kirchenkreis . Where to go ★ Burg Trendelburg — castle hotel ("Rapunzel's Tower") The 700-year-old hilltop castle — the same silhouette in the Merian, Reißert, and Bokelmann views on the main Germany & Origins tab — has operated as a romantic castle hotel since 1996. Hotel Burg Trendelburg has 22 individually designed rooms (some with antique four-poster beds), a restaurant, vaulted cellar, castle chapel, and registry office. The bergfried (keep) is promoted as the inspiration for Rapunzel's tower; visitors can climb the 130 steps to the top for views over the Diemel valley and the Reinhardswald. Sunday "Rapunzel" performances in the courtyard, May Fairy Tale Festival, late-November "Bewitching Lights." Links: burg-hotel-trendelburg.com · visit.kassel.de — Trendelburg Castle · Deutsche Märchenstraße — Trendelburg · TripAdvisor Trendelburg town & Stadt Trendelburg (incl. Deisel) The walled town of Trendelburg sits along the Diemel river. Deisel is today an Ortsteil (district) of Stadt Trendelburg, about 3 km east. The Trendelburg Stadtpfor

d: A glossary of the German and genealogical terminology used across the Charles Research site — Kirchenbuch, Stammtafel, Auswanderer-Beleg, Kurrent, and more.
b: Glossary — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Glossary Glossary Definitions of the German archival and genealogical terminology used across the project — for readers approaching this site without a background in 19th-century Hessian record-keeping. The site is bilingual by accident: it reports findings in English, but the underlying records — Kirchenbücher, Stammrollen, Auswanderer-Belege — are German, and the standard descriptive vocabulary is German too. This page defines the terms that recur most often. A Auswanderer-Beleg DE /ˈaʊsˌvandərɐ bəˈleːk/ Lit. "emigrant document." A formal emigration permit issued by the local Hessian authority, certifying that a subject of military age had discharged conscription obligations and had no outstanding civic debts. Required by law in 19th-century Hesse-Kassel; in practice, often evaded. Many Auswanderer-Belege survive in Arcinsys , the catalogue of the Hessian state archive at Marburg. cf. Open Questions, Priority 3 . Auswanderung DE Emigration. Auswanderungswelle = "wave of emigration"; refers in this project to the great mid-19th-century German emigration to the United States. B Bestattung / Bestattungsregister DE "Burial / burial register." The third book in a typical Hessian Lutheran Kirchenbuch , after Taufen (baptisms) and Trauungen (marriages). The Trendelburg Bestattungsregister 220096 is one of the project's pivotal sources; it contains the K-section index entries for the Kontze / Kontzen surname pool 1773–1824. C Churhessen DE The pre-1866 orthography for the Electorate of Hesse-Kassel. Modern German: Kurhessen . Charles's 1866 St. Marcus marriage register uses the Churhessen form, dating the language of the record to the pre-Prussian-annexation period. See also Hesse-Kassel context . b: Adjacent Emigrations & the Negative as Evidence — Charles Immigration Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Immigration Journey / Adjacent Emigrations Adjacent Emigrations & the Negative as Evidence Two Hildebrand cohorts that didn't match Charles · what the absence tells us IMMIGRATION JOURNEY Hildebrand chain-migration angle · Bremen-records-destruction context ★ At a Glance Two Hildebrand-cohort emigrations from Deisel surfaced in the LAGIS index — neither matches Charles, but both illuminate the chain-migration possibility. The 1850 Ludwig Hildebrand cohort went to Rockford, Illinois (a maternal-aunt route); the October 1853 Wilhelmine Maria Hildebrand cohort went to Philadelphia (manifest located, no extra teen). Beyond the Hildebrands, the absence of Charles from any indexed Bremen / Hamburg / NY / Baltimore / New Orleans manifest is itself the strongest single piece of evidence — pointing at the destroyed Bremen 1832–1872 records as the most likely explanation. Adjacent emigrations that surfaced Two Deisel-Hildebrand emigrations that did NOT match Charles but illuminate the chain-migration possibility: April / May 1850 · Ludwig Hildebrand cohort → Rockford IL Deisel KB 218836 (1850 confirmation class, Entry 1) records Friedrich Hildebrand confirmed early "weil die Eltern nach Amerika auswandern wollten" (because the parents wanted to emigrate to America). Located on the 1860 census in Monroe Township, Ogle County, Illinois — about 15 miles south of Rockford. Indexed by Ancestry as "Hillabrand." Implication: if Charles is Justus Köster (b. Jan 1835), he could have travelled with this 1850 Hildebrand cohort as a 15-year-old maternal nephew — but Rockford is not St. Louis. A delayed Rockford → St. Louis move between 1850 and 1862 remains possible but undocumented. d: Bremen-departure 1853 – Aug 1862 exhaustively cleared across all 4 U.S. ports. 1858 – Aug 1862 image-inspected at handwriting level + Soundex-swept; 1853 – 1857 ISTG corpus 100% sampled (36 of 36 ships, ~8,873 passengers). 0 Charles candidates. 10 K + -en surface near-misses all eliminated.
b: Bremen Passenger-List Clearance Record — Charles Immigration Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Immigration Journey / Bremen Passenger-List Clearance Record Bremen Passenger-List Clearance Record Bremen-departure 1853 – Aug 1862 · Clearance record · 0 Charles candidates IMMIGRATION JOURNEY Survey 28 April – 7 May 2026 ★ At a Glance Bottom line: the Bremen-departure hypothesis for Charles's immigration is comprehensively cleared across the entire 1853 – Aug 1862 window for the tracked surname filter and the Carl/Charles given-name assumption — a different given name on arrival, or a non-Bremen port (Antwerp, Le Havre, Rotterdam), would fall outside this sweep (see “still possible” below). The 1858 – Aug 1862 portion was image-inspected ship-by-ship at handwriting level on M237 (NY) and M259 (NOLA), plus Soundex-swept on M255 (Baltimore) and M575 (Galveston). The 1853 – 1857 pre-extension was sampled at 100% of the ISTG-transcribed corpus — 36 of 36 sailings, ~8,873 passengers — at line-by-line K/C/G + -en/-on/-in filter level. 0 Charles candidates surface anywhere in the combined window under the ten tracked surname variants (Konze · Konzen · Konsen · Kontze · Gannsen · Gonnson · Gansen · Gonsen · Gunnsen · Hansen). Historical context: the original Bremen passenger lists for 1832–1907 were destroyed in two waves — purged for archive space ca. 1907, and the surviving fragment lost in WWII bombing — leaving only 3,017 lists from 1920–1939. Every surviving reconstruction (ISTG U.S.-side transcriptions, Zimmerman & Wolfert print volume, Die MAUS / public-juling.de, Deutsche Auswanderer-Datenbank, the 2026 Zenodo CHUP dataset) and every direct U.S.-arrival manifest series has been queried for Charles. He is not findable in any of them. Detailed methodology, per-ship findings, and the 10 K + -en surface near-misses (all eliminated by 4-criterion testing) are in the Research Log at the bottom of this page. d: Charles Konzen's 1859 Atlantic crossing — what we know, what we have searched, and the still-missing passenger manifest.
b: Immigration Journey — Charles Research Skip to main content Charles Research / Immigration Journey Chapter Two · The Atlantic Crossing The Crossing Per the 1900 census, Charles immigrated in 1859 . Per the 1866 marriage record, he was already in St. Louis "since seven years." The trans-Atlantic crossing itself remains undocumented — the manifest his name lives on still unfound. Year of immigration ca. 1859 Likely route Bremen → New York → St. Louis Status Manifest unrecovered This is the section landing for the immigration investigation. The full narrative — adjacent emigrations, surname-variant searches, Bremen-side reconstructions — is on the home-page Immigration Journey tab ; this page surfaces the deep-dives and the still-missing manifest. ★ Open · 1859 manifest unrecovered The crossing is documented at both ends but not in the middle Both the U.S. census and Charles's own marriage register give 1859 as the arrival year. He was 24, almost certainly travelling alone, almost certainly without an Auswanderer-Beleg. NYC, New Orleans, and Baltimore arrivals 1858–1860 have been searched against the full surname pool — the five Hesse-attested forms (Konze, Kontze, Kontzen, Contze, Konzen) plus the eight American renderings later recorded for the family in St. Louis (Gonnson, Gansen, Gonsen, Konsen, Gonson, Jansen, Konzen, Conson) plus the related American mishearings (Jansen, Johnson) — without surfacing a candidate. The Bremen-side originals are largely lost (1875 fire); the Hamburg-side searches return nothing. → Open Questions · Priority 2: the 1859 manifest Read the full Immigration narrative → on the home-page tab — adjacent emigrations from the same parish complex, the Grandjot/Herwig parallel cases, the Bremen reconstruction methodology. Deep-dive pages 📋 Methodology Sources searched & name variants Every database queried, every surname spelling tried — the explicit map of where the project has looked for the manifest. NYC, New Orleans, Baltimore

b: Sources Searched & Name Variants — Charles Immigration Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Immigration Journey / Sources & Name Variants Sources Searched & Name Variants Every archive consulted · every spelling tried · every given-name combination tested IMMIGRATION JOURNEY 11 sources · 14 surname variants · 6 given-name combos ★ At a Glance Over the course of the investigation, every major U.S.-arrival passenger-list collection, every Hessian state-archive emigration index, and every aggregated emigrant database has been searched under two distinct families of name spellings — the "Gannsen / Gonnson" cluster (the form Charles used in America) and the "Konze / Konsen" cluster (the form preserved at his son's 1875 St. Marcus baptism). Searches were also run under six given-name combinations covering both the "L. Charles" enlistment record and the candidate Justus / Ludwig / Lorenz reconstructions of the German baptismal name. No match has surfaced for any combination. Sources searched Each source below was searched under the full set of name variants and date range 1853 – 1862. The verdict column captures the outcome. Bremen Auswanderer-Datenbank ↗ The German emigrant database (Die MAUS Bremen / Bremen Chamber of Commerce digitization). Indexes Bremen departures 1832 – 1939. Searched under all surname variants. no match — Bremen passenger lists for 1832 – 1872 were largely destroyed; surviving partial indices contain no Charles-shaped entry from Hessen. Hessisches Staatsarchiv (HHStAW / HStAM) ↗ Marburg state archive emigration cards via the Arcinsys portal. Documents requested-for-emigration permits from 1820s onward. no match for Charles under any spelling. The only "Gonnson"-like entry surfaced is the Bad Nauheim probate of Count Jouffroy Gonsons of Paris (see French Count panel ) — unrelated. LAGIS Hessen — Auswanderer ↗ The Hessian Land-Information-System emigration index. Indexed by surname and town. no Charles match . Surfaces Wilhelmine Ma

t: Charles (a.k.a. Gannsen / Johnson) Research — A Genealogical Investigation
d: A multi-month transatlantic genealogical investigation into Charles Konzen (recorded in U.S. records as Gannsen / Johnson; 1835–1903) — Hessian-born Civil War sergeant of Battery B, 2nd Missouri Light Artillery — and his probable Trendelburg / Deisel origins in Churhessen (a working conclusion — the German baptism remains unrecovered). Compiled by Jed Johnson with German genealogist Peter Schräder
b: Charles (a.k.a. Gannsen / Johnson) Research — A Genealogical Investigation Skip to main content A Genealogical Investigation Charles Konzen Deisel · Trendelburg · Churhessen → St. Louis · 1835 — 1903 Research compiled by Jed Johnson Charles Research A Genealogical Investigation Charles Konzen 1835 1903 A transatlantic detective story — eight surnames, seven children, one missing baptism — and the Hessian village a Civil War sergeant left behind in 1859 . Origin Deisel · Trendelburg · Churhessen Service Sgt., Battery B · 2nd Missouri Light Artillery Final residence St. Louis, Missouri Begin the investigation Scroll I Chapter One · 1835 — 1859 A baptism missing from the Kirchenbuch . Born in the parish complex of Trendelburg, Deisel, and Friedrichsfeld — somewhere inside the Konze / Kontzen / Contzen surname pool of Churhessen. After ninety-five research sessions across twenty-nine Hessian parishes, the German baptism record has not yet surfaced. 29 parishes searched 13 candidate families Konzen surname concluded · May 2026 Read Hesse & Origins II Chapter Two · ca. 1859 An ocean & a name that shifts . The Atlantic crossing — most likely Bremen to New York — ca. 1859. He arrives under a surname that American clerks would record in eight different spellings over the next thirty years before the family settled on Johnson. Phonetic-variant searches across Bremen, Hamburg, and New York harbor records have not yet returned the manifest. 8 distinct American spellings 5+ independent scribes Bremen → St. Louis Read the Crossing III Chapter Three · 1862 — 1865 Three years inside the Missouri war. Sergeant, Battery B, 2nd Missouri Light Artillery . Three years of garrison and fortification duty across three Missouri stations — the St. Louis garrison, the Mississippi River bend at New Madrid, and Fort Wyman at Rolla. A pension, granted 1891 for war-onset rheumatism, would later anchor the documentary trail. 3 Missouri postings 6 fellow veterans as witnesses Pension

d: The research standards, source-rating taxonomy, transcription conventions, citation style, and known limitations of the Charles Research project.
b: Methodology — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Methodology Methodology How this site decides what counts as evidence — the source-rating taxonomy, transcription conventions, naming conventions, citation style, and acknowledged gaps that govern the work. ⤓ Download PDF ← Abstract This page is the project's research standard. Every confidence badge on the site, every transcription, every spelling choice, and every citation traces back to the rules below. The goal is that another genealogist or family historian can audit any conclusion in this site and either reproduce it or challenge it on the same factual ground. 1 · Scope of the project The investigation began as a question: who, in Hesse, was Charles Gannsen of Battery B, 2nd Missouri Light Artillery, and which family did he leave behind in 1859? Over the course of 78+ documented research sessions, the question expanded into a full transatlantic biography: Charles's likely German origins in the Konze / Kontzen / Contzen surname pool of Churhessen ; his 1859 emigration (per the 1900 census; passenger manifest not yet located); his Civil War service in St. Louis garrison forts and at New Madrid, Missouri; his marriage to Auguste Kreichelt and the seven children baptised at St. Marcus German Evangelical Church; his pension claim and 1903 burial at Old Saint Marcus Cemetery. The project is not a tree-builder for an entire surname. It is a tightly bounded prosopography of one man, his immediate family in three countries (Germany, the U.S., and the family's correspondents in present-day Hesse), and the records that document them. 2 · The proof standard Conclusions on this site are written to align with the Board for Certification of Genealogists' Genealogical Proof Standard : reasonably exhaustive research, complete and accurate citations, analysis and correlation of the collected information, resolution of conflicting evidence, and a soundly reasoned and coherently written conclus

d: Every parish register searched and every candidate family eliminated — with date, scope, and the specific evidence that closed the lead. The auditable backbone of the project's 'reasonably exhaustive research' claim.
b: Negative-results audit — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Negative-results audit Negative-results audit Every Hessian parish register the project has searched and every candidate family the project has eliminated — with date of search, scope, what was looked for, and the specific evidence that closed the lead. The auditable backbone of the Genealogical-Proof-Standard "reasonably exhaustive research" claim. A finding is only as strong as the searches that did not produce it. This page lists each negative result the project has logged: which parish or candidate family was investigated, when, what scope was covered, what was searched for, and what evidence eliminated the candidate. Where a result is partial (some volumes searched, others outstanding) the gap is named. Where a result is open, it is logged on Open Questions with a "what would resolve this" pointer. Standing statement on the proof standard. The central parentage question — who were Charles's parents, and where exactly was he baptised? — does not yet meet the Genealogical Proof Standard . The research below is extensive but not yet exhaustive: principal registers remain unsearched (the rows marked open below — e.g. Heisebeck and pre-1822 Schöneberg, neither yet digitised — are unsearched ground , not eliminations), the Marburg emigration and conscription records have not been pulled, and DNA testing — though completed (Y-DNA and autosomal) — has not yet produced a confirming match to a documented Konze line. The parish-origin and surname conclusions are therefore held Working , and the baptism itself Open , rather than Confirmed. A negative row means a register was searched and Charles was not in it; a partial row means some volumes were covered and others remain; an open row means the register has not yet been accessible at all. Summary statistics 29 Parishes worked (26 Kurhessen + 3 Westphalian via Matricula) 13 Candidate families eliminated 3 Active leads remainin

b: Kontze_Family_Tree_and_Research_Paths.md — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Back to the changelog Source note Kontze_Family_Tree_and_Research_Paths.md Download original .md KONTZE / KONZE / CONTZEN FAMILY TREE Reconstructed from Archion Register 220096, Ancestry, FamilySearch, and Peter Schrader's Research Compiled: April 1, 2026 | Updated: April 26, 2026 — Jed Johnson + research sessions 11–74 Purpose: Map the Kontze/Konze/Contzen families of Trendelburg and Deisel to connect Charles Johnson Sr. (b. 26 January 1835, Deisel/Trendelburg area; d. 16 December 1903, St. Louis, Missouri) — identity confirmed FamilySearch PID LZXC-6GB — to his German birth record. American surname "Konzen/Konze/Kontze" may be a Hausname rather than official church surname. CURRENT STATUS: there is NO active candidate for Charles's German birth record. Entry 195 — once the leading candidate — is ELIMINATED (see banner below). 🛑 ENTRY 195 IS ELIMINATED — DO NOT RE-OPEN AS A CANDIDATE. Settled by Peter Schräder (expert paleographic reader), retraction dated 4 May 2026 (documented in correspondence/HStAM_Marburg_Stammrolle_Anfrage_May2026.md ). Entry 195 (KB 218842, image 40, Deisel Gänsewinkel, born 20 Jan 1835) is the record of a male Köster infant who died unbaptized at/near birth — NOT Charles. The child-name column reads only "Männl: Köster" (sex + surname, no given name) with the ‡/"ungetauft" marker and an empty godparent column , which is the standard register convention for a newborn who died without proper baptism. The earlier "Justus" reading was a misread of this entry and is void. Why it kept resurfacing — and the inference error to retire: older drafts (this file's pre-26-April text, the diagram/ASCII tree below, and parts of the website) reasoned "no burial found in register 218851 → emigration → strongest candidate." That is a false inference : an infant who died unbaptized in January 1835 is recorded in the baptism register's notation, not necessarily in the reg

b: Session84_KB218842_Entry195_Reverification_GodparentColumn_June4_2026.md — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Back to the changelog Source note Session84_KB218842_Entry195_Reverification_GodparentColumn_June4_2026.md Download original .md Session 84 — Live Archion Re-Verification of Entry 195 (KB 218842, Image 40) 🛑 CONCLUSION CORRECTED — ENTRY 195 IS ELIMINATED, NOT A CANDIDATE. This note as first written (4 June 2026) wrongly treated Entry 195's missing given name as merely "illegible (Nottaufe)" and concluded the candidate was strengthened . That conclusion is retracted. Per Peter Schräder's expert paleographic read (retraction dated 4 May 2026 , see correspondence/HStAM_Marburg_Stammrolle_Anfrage_May2026.md ), the "Männl: Köster" entry with no given name, the ‡/ungetauft marker, and the empty godparent column is the standard recording of a newborn who died unbaptized — so Entry 195 is NOT Charles and is eliminated. The "no burial found → emigration" reasoning is a false inference (an unbaptized infant death is noted in the baptism register, not necessarily the burials register). The investigation currently has no active candidate. The factual observations below (dates, parents' names, sibling scan, marriage-register negatives) remain valid as data; only the "strengthened candidate" framing is void. Date: 4 June 2026 · Researcher: Jed Johnson (+ assistant, live Archion session) Register: Deisel, "Taufen 1830–1861" = Archion churchRegister/218842 , Image 40 , Gemeinde Deisel · Jahr 1835 · Monat Januar · Seite 39 , entries 194–198 . Method: Archion AI text-recognition ("Erkannter Text" / KI-Texterkennung) for the full page, cross-checked against a max-zoom manuscript read of each cell (the AI text is explicitly flagged by Archion as machine-generated and error-prone, so every load-bearing claim below was confirmed visually). b: Session86_YFull_FTDNA_YDNA_Analysis_June6_2026.md — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Back to the changelog Source note Session86_YFull_FTDNA_YDNA_Analysis_June6_2026.md Download original .md Session 86 — Y-DNA review: YFull sample YF147577 + FTDNA Big Y Date: 6 June 2026 · live, signed-in YFull + FTDNA Discover; YFull STR panel supplied by Jed. Sample: Charles's tested male-line descendant = YFull YF147577 (= the "new" sample on the public I-Y38863 tree); FTDNA Big Y-700 kit 1032621 . Goals (all four requested) Confirm haplogroup position · mine the match list · interpret the Ashkenazi finding · log it in the research. 1. Haplogroup position — CONFIRMED, two labs agree Terminal subclade: I-Y38863 (I2 › I-M223 line). FTDNA terminal = I-Y38863 (parent I-BY2640, per the Discover Time Tree). YFull v14.03 places YF147577 on the identical I-Y38863 (formed ~750 ybp, TMRCA ~700 ybp ≈ 1300 CE). FTDNA Discover dates the branch ~1400–1450 CE. Two independent trees, same subclade, compatible medieval coalescence. STR panel (YF147577, ~780 markers, supplied 6 June): unremarkable I2-M223 modal haplotype — DYS393=13, DYS390=22, DYS19=16, DYS391=10, DYS385=16-17, DYS426=11, DYS388=13, DYS439=11, DYS389I=13, DYS392=13, DYS389II=29. No rare or null markers; a few multi-copy markers (DYS464, DYS458, DYS449) not called (normal). Adds no conflicting signal. (The raw upload could not be persisted into the repo — a transient uploads-mount issue left a 0-byte placeholder at peter_charts/STR_for_YF147577_20260606.csv ; re-drop the file if you want the full panel stored. Core values are recorded here and in dna.html .) 2. Match list — mined on both platforms; all Ashkenazi, no Konze YFull I-Y38863 branch = exactly 3 members: YF147577 (Charles's descendant) · YF078713 — Poland [PL-14] , carrying YFull's "yid" (Ashkenazi) origin tag · YF002081 . So the only two men on Charles's exact YFull branch are an Ashkenazi Pole + one further sample. FTDNA Big Y matches (9, retrieved 30 May

Jed asked to (1) add the Konde/Konten spellings to the by-town page's variant list and (2) find Christoph Kontze — the Hümme emigrant named in Session 95 — and where he went. Searched: FamilySearch record search + full-text + Family Tree (signed-in session), web search. Christoph Kontze, b. 13 Feb 1814 Hümme, son of the late Ackermann Friedrich Kontze × Marie Sophie geb. Busch; confirmed Pfingsten 25 May 1828; orphaned; brothers Johannes (b. 1810, → Stammen, d. 1871) and Friedrich (b. 1817, d. 1897, stayed). The only adult Konze male absent from Hümme's complete marriage (1830–91) and burial (1830–74) indexes. A. Two unverified US candidates found Candidate 1 — "Christopher Kontze," Hardin County, Ohio (1840–1847) Ohio Tax Records 1800–1887: "Christopher Kontze," tax assessments 1840–41 and 1844, Hardin, Ohio (FS arks 1:1:7DWC-36ZM and 1:1:789P-R6N2). The spelling Kontze — rare in the US — is exact. Continuation: "Christopher Kuntz," 1847 tax, Pleasant Township, Hardin Co. (same collection) — same man, spelling drifting to the common Kuntz. Gone by 1850 : the 1850 census of Hardin Co. has NO Christopher under Kuntz/Koontz/Kontze/Counts (only US-born Koontz/Kuhns families). He left the county (or died) 1847–50. Fit: timing matches Christoph's 1840s disappearance (he'd be 26 in 1840). Identity unproven — no record gives age or birthplace. Candidate 2 — "Christian Kontze," Atlanta, Georgia (1850) 1850 census, Atlanta, DeKalb Co., GA (household 338): Christian Kontze, 34, b. ~1816 Germany; wife Mary, 36, Germany

d: What this project has searched for but not yet found — open candidates, embargoed records, and the specific evidence that would resolve each gap.
b: Open Questions — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Open Questions Open Questions An explicit inventory of what the project has searched for and not yet found — with what would resolve each gap. World-class research is transparent about what it does not know. A research site that lists only its findings is incomplete. This page lists the documents the project has actively searched for and not yet located, the candidates that remain open, and the embargoed materials whose existence has been documented but whose contents are not yet imaged on the site. Each entry names the specific evidence that would resolve it — so a future researcher (or a descendant who happens to walk into the right archive) knows exactly what to look for. 4 priority gaps 3 embargoed materials 5 resolved & logged 7 June 2026 last updated Priority gaps — directly load-bearing on the central thesis Charles's German baptism record Priority 1 Last attempted: Session 24 · 18 April 2026 · multi-register Jan-1835 illegitimacy scan The single most-wanted document in the entire project. Charles's 1866 St. Marcus marriage register names "Deisse, Kreis Cassel, Churhessen " — phonetically Deisel — as his place of origin. The Trendelburg parish complex's Konze / Kontze / Contzen baptismal registers have been searched at maximum zoom for January 1835 with no Charles / Carl entry surfacing under the leading-hypothesis surname. What would resolve this A Trendelburg / Deisel / Friedrichsfeld / Helmarshausen / Hofgeismar baptismal entry dated late January 1835 (or up to several weeks before or after) for a male child whose surname is in the Konze pool, with a father or godfather name that matches one of the documented Konze branch heads. Alternative resolutions: an illegitimate-birth entry whose father is later legitimised; a parish jurisdiction the project has not yet identified. Heisebeck post-1831 baptisms — undigitised register Open · undigitised Status: Marriages 1808–192

b: Adam Herweck — Battery B sergeant, Charles's wedding witness — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / People who knew Charles / Adam Herweck Adam Herweck ⚜ Battery B comrade · Charles's wedding witness · Lampertheim → St. Louis → Edwardsville BATTERY B · 2ND MO LIGHT ARTILLERY Pvt → Sgt Wedding witness · 7 Jan 1866 FamilySearch · K4DW-PXM ★ At a Glance ★ WEDDING WITNESS · BATTERY B SERGEANT & RECRUITER The first witness named on Charles and Auguste's 1866 marriage register entry. Adam Herweck (canonical Johann Adam Herweck · FS K4DW-PXM · b. 16 July 1831 Lampertheim, Hessen · d. 11 May 1897 Edwardsville, Madison Co., IL · age 65) is the first-named witness in the witness column of the St. Marcus marriage register entry for Charles Konzen × Auguste Kreichelt (7 January 1866) and the only Battery B veteran in the wedding-witness column per a May 2026 NPS Soldiers and Sailors System search. Two pages from his Compiled Service Record ( NARA M405 Roll 0329) — the Nov–Dec 1862 Detachment Muster Roll and a 9 December 1862 Captain's letter from New Madrid — definitively confirm K4DW-PXM as Charles's Battery B comrade and document Adam's role on recruiting service at St. Louis in late 1862 — making him the project's documented Battery B recruiter. Why this matters for understanding Charles: Adam Herweck is the documentary anchor that ties three of Charles's St. Louis spheres into a single network: (1) Battery B — fellow sergeant, both promoted from Pvt. to Sgt., both at New Madrid in the same months; (2) the wedding party — the named witness on the 1866 marriage; (3) the recruiter pipeline — Adam was on detached recruiting duty at St. Louis in December 1862, the same month Charles enlisted on 2 December 1862. The wedding-party as a whole (Adam from Pfalz-adjacent Hessen-Darmstadt, the Kreichelts from Hannover, the Goetz family from Bavaria, and Charles from Hessen-Cassel) is consistent with a pan-German pension-witness / parish-sponsor / wed

Her 1870 sponsorship alongside her not-yet-married brother-in-law Carl Kreichelt and fiancée Catharina Albrecht stitches the Mincke / Kreichelt / Albrecht / Pillmann families into the same single-baptism sponsor pool — among the most densely in-law-networked sponsorship rosters across the seven baptisms. t: August Derr — 1st Sergeant Co. B — Charles Research
d: 1st Sergeant of Co. B · Charles's direct NCO at New Madrid · Pension affidavit witness
b: August Derr — 1st Sergeant Co. B — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / People who knew Charles / August Derr August Derr 1st Sergeant of Co. B · Charles's direct NCO at New Madrid · Pension affidavit witness BATTERY B · 2ND MO LIGHT ARTILLERY · 1ST SERGEANT 2 affidavits in 1891 ★ At a Glance PENSION WITNESS · 1ST SERGEANT CO. B Charles's direct supervisor during the Civil War — the 1st Sergeant of Company B, 2nd Missouri Light Artillery. August Derr (b. ~1833 Germany — likely Saxony; mustered out 1865 age 32) was the 1st Sergeant of Company B, 2nd Missouri Light Artillery — Charles's direct supervisor during the September 1863 New Madrid station. He swore two pension affidavits for Charles in 1891 (29 September and 14 May), independently corroborating Charles's hearing loss from heavy cannonading at New Madrid and his rupture-related disability. Derr's NCO rank gives his testimony the heaviest evidentiary weight of any soldier-witness in the file: he could speak with personal supervisory authority about Charles's medical history during active service. Why this matters for understanding Charles: Derr's affidavits are the keystone of Charles's pension narrative — the only witness named in the pension file whose chain-of-command position lets him swear, under oath, that the New Madrid cannonading and the resulting deafness occurred while Charles was under his direct supervision in September–October 1863. He filed his own Civil War pension in May 1889, the same year Charles began his application — suggesting Charles's pension witnesses, including Derr, were themselves working through a coordinated post-war disability claim cycle in St. Louis. Born ~1833 · likely Saxony, Germany Civil War unit Co. B, 2nd Reg't Missouri Light Artillery (Pvt. → Sgt.) Connection Charles's direct NCO at New Madrid, 1863 1891 St. Louis address 1857 South 14th Street Affidavits filed 2 (Sept 1891 + May 1891) Own pension Filed 24 May 1889 · same year as Char

The two served together in Battery B, 2nd Missouri Light Artillery. Researcher view (sources, citations, bottom-line) Carl Friedrich "August" Kreichelt (1844–1877, Clausthal) was Auguste's eldest brother, Charles's brother-in-law, and a Civil War comrade-in-arms — they served together in Battery B, 2nd Missouri Light Artillery . Husband of Maria Emma Mincke . He sponsored the 1872 Auguste baptism — the infant Auguste Gonsen carries both her mother's and her uncle's name (the standard masculine-→-feminine namesake convention). He died young at age 32 in 1877, only five years after this baptism. Why this matters for understanding Charles: August is a critical Charles-link figure: same regiment, same battery, same German-immigrant peer-group, same St. Louis post-war rebuild years. The "Charles & August served together" pattern is one of the strongest Charles-side documentary anchors. The 1872 baptism that names a daughter for him reads as a deliberate honoring of this comrade-brother bond. His early death at 32 narrowed the surviving Kreichelt male generation — meaning by 1880 Charles's primary in-law male peer was Carl, not August. d: ★ Charles Gannsen's wife · the maternal anchor of all seven Gannsen children · Clausthal-born → St. Louis 1853 → m. Charles 1866
b: Auguste Kreichelt (1847–1896) — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / People who knew Charles / Auguste Kreichelt Auguste Kreichelt (1847–1896) ★ Charles Gannsen's wife · the maternal anchor of all seven Gannsen children · Clausthal-born → St. Louis 1853 → m. Charles 1866 CHARLES GANNSEN'S WIFE b. 12 May 1847 Clausthal, Hannover d. 1896 St. Louis 7 children · 4 surviving ★ At a Glance ★ CHARLES'S WIFE · MATERNAL ANCHOR OF ALL SEVEN GANNSEN CHILDREN Auguste was Charles's wife and the mother of all seven of his children. She was born 12 May 1847 in Clausthal — a mining town in what is now Lower Saxony — and came to the United States with her parents in 1853 when she was about six. She and Charles married at St. Marcus German Evangelical Church in St. Louis on 7 January 1866 and remained married for thirty years; she died in 1896, eight years before Charles. The 1896 St. Marcus death register records her surname as Conson — one of the eight American spellings of the family name that anchor the surname-trail evidence on this site. Researcher view (sources, citations, bottom-line) Auguste Kreichelt (FS LZKV-9F5 · b. 12 May 1847 Clausthal, Kingdom of Hannover · imm. 1853 age ~6 with parents Georg Kreichelt × Louise Henriette Weiss · d. 1896 St. Louis age 49 · "Auguste Conson" on death record) is Charles Konzen's wife — married 7 January 1866 at St. Marcus by Pastor Georg Wendelin Wall (entry No. 676) — and the maternal anchor of every documented Gannsen relationship in St. Louis . The 1896 St. Marcus death register records her surname as "Conson" , a C-initial form that ties directly back to the Trendelburg parish's documented Contzen variant — the third independent attestation of the family's Hessian K/C-initial origin (after Erwin Carl's 1875 baptism reading "Konsen" with s and daughter Auguste's 1890 death record reading "geb Konzen" with z — the single American attestation of the full Hessian z-form). b: Bertha Barbara "Babette" Kreichelt — Auguste's youngest sister, sponsor at TWO baptisms — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / People who knew Charles / Bertha Barbara "Babette" Kreichelt Bertha Barbara Kreichelt "Babette" Auguste's youngest sister · the namesake aunt for Bertha Johnson (1880) · sponsor at two Gannsen baptisms (1873 Louise & 1880 Bertha) · died 1892 St. James, Phelps Co., Missouri at 32 SPONSOR · 1873 + 1880 Sister-in-law to Charles Gannsen Born St. Louis · 1859 FamilySearch · L5FH-BRP ★ At a Glance SPONSOR PROFILE · IDENTIFIED · IN-LAW FS L5FH-BRP · name reconciled Charles's sister-in-law — Auguste's youngest sister, known in the family as Babette. She sponsored two of Charles and Auguste's children at baptism. Researcher view (sources, citations, bottom-line) Bertha Barbara "Babette" Kreichelt (1859–1892, St. Louis) was Auguste's youngest sister and the eighth and last child of Georg and Henriette. She sponsored two Gannsen baptisms — first as junior 13-year-old co-sponsor at the 1873 Louise baptism (recorded as "Barbara Kreichelt"), then as the single named sponsor at the 1880 Bertha Johnson baptism (recorded as "Babette") — the namesake aunt for Bertha Johnson. She died at age 32 in 1892 at St. James, Phelps County, Missouri , buried Holy Ghost Cemetery in St. Louis. The "Barbara/Babette = Bertha Barbara" reconciliation was confirmed via FS L5FH-BRP's documented Alternate Name "Babette" (May 2026). Why this matters for understanding Charles: Babette is the only Gannsen baptism sponsor whose later residence — St. James, Phelps County, Missouri — overlaps with the Phelps County Otta Delien × William King 1866 marriage that's currently the closest non-St-Louis Delien lead. Whether Babette's 1880s-90s relocation to Phelps County reflects an earlier Kreichelt-Delien social tie (or is independent) is open. Her two-baptism sponsorship plus the namesake to Bertha Johnson (1880) tightens the Kreichelt-side kin network

Daughter of Martin Albrecht and Catharine Wilhelmine Lieber. The Albrecht-Kreichelt marriage on 5 March 1871 — paired with sister Fredericka Albrecht's marriage to Friedrich Pillmann on 5 April 1870, just seven weeks before this baptism — tied the Pillmann / Albrecht / Kreichelt families into a single marriage chain. The infant Katherine Carolina carries Catharina's name as middle namesake. Why this matters for understanding Charles: Catharina is part of the Kreichelt-Albrecht-Pillmann marriage chain — one of the most densely interconnected kin clusters in the Charles & Auguste social world. Her 1870 sponsorship preceded her own marriage by ten months, meaning she stood as godmother to her future niece — a pre-formal-kin courtesy consistent with Lutheran-Evangelical sponsorship customs. b: Dr. Charles Hess — Charles's first treating physician — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / People who knew Charles / Dr. Charles Hess Dr. Charles Hess Charles Gannsen's first treating physician · post-discharge Limited record Treating physician · post-1865 ★ At a Glance FIRST TREATING PHYSICIAN · IDENTITY OPEN The only treating physician Charles ever named — cited by Charles himself in a handwritten letter to the Commissioner of Pensions in 1898. Dr. Charles Hess is the only treating physician Charles Konzen ever names by name in the entire pension file. The reference appears in Charles's own c. 1898 handwritten letter to the Commissioner of Pensions: "I went to Hospital Surgeon and got medicine, was not in hospitals. Dr. Charles Hess, treated me first…" A multi-database directory search (May 2026) returned five non-physician Charles Hess listings in 1865-1880 St. Louis directories but no physician — leaving the doctor's individual identity open. Why this matters for understanding Charles: The reference establishes that Charles sought private medical care in St. Louis rather than the federal Soldiers' Home / VA system — this is consistent with his immediate return to civilian work as a railroad conductor on Gates Street and Broadway. Identifying Dr. Hess would re-anchor Charles's earliest post-discharge medical record and possibly back-date his disability narrative by ~25 years before the 1890 pension claim. b: Erwin Spraul — Anheuser-Busch Superintendent & namesake of Erwin Carl Konsen — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / People who knew Charles / Erwin Spraul Erwin Spraul ⚜ Superintendent of the Anheuser-Busch Brewing Association · Baden-born German immigrant friend of Charles · the sole named sponsor — and probable namesake — of Erwin Carl Konsen at the 1875 baptism · died young at 41 in 1889 of post-operative complications SOLE SPONSOR · 1875 ANHEUSER-BUSCH SUPERINTENDENT Born Baden · ~1848 FamilySearch · KLP1-N62 ★ At a Glance SPONSOR PROFILE · IDENTIFIED FS KLP1-N62 · obituary surfaced May 2026 The sole named sponsor at the 1875 baptism of Charles's son Erwin Carl — the keystone Konsen-spelling record. Erwin Spraul (~1848 Baden – 19 June 1889 St. Louis, age 41) was Superintendent of the Anheuser-Busch Brewing Association at his death — the most senior trade title at one of St. Louis's largest German-immigrant employers. The 20 June 1889 St. Louis newspaper obituary establishes his role at A-B, his 1340 South Ninth Street address (in the Soulard / Frenchtown German neighborhood, walking distance from the brewery), Baden birthplace, age 41 at death, "very good circumstances" estate, wife Anna Maria Grassner (m. 1868), and son Frank (b. 1869, d. young 1894). He died of post-operative complications from a shoulder abscess. Why this matters for understanding Charles: Erwin is one of a small handful of non-Kreichelt-side sponsors across the seven Gannsen baptisms (alongside Jacob Reck , Maria Agnes Götz , and Wilhelmine Ebeling ) and the sole named sponsor at the 1875 Erwin Carl Konsen baptism, and the child’s probable namesake (the name-sponsor coincidence is suggestive; no record states the naming motive — cf. the parallel Pillmann case). The 1889 obituary sharpens Jed's December 2025 reading of him as a "successful fellow immigrant" to Anheuser-Busch Superintendent — the top tier of St. Louis German-immigrant brewing. The Anheuser-B

b: Frederick Wilhelm Pillmann — sponsor at two Gannsen baptisms — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / People who knew Charles / Frederick Pillmann Frederick Wilhelm Pillmann Baptism sponsor at TWO Gannsen baptisms (1866 firstborn & 1870 Katherine Carolina) · husband of Fredericka Albrecht (Catharina's sister) · St. Louis butcher · German-born immigrant SPONSOR · 1866 + 1870 Brother-in-law to Carl Kreichelt Born Germany · 1846 FamilySearch · L17N-8Q6 ★ At a Glance SPONSOR PROFILE · IDENTIFIED FS L17N-8Q6 A St. Louis butcher and German immigrant who sponsored two of Charles and Auguste's children at baptism, and later married into the Albrecht family. Friedrich Wilhelm Pillmann (b. 1846 Germany) was a St. Louis butcher and the only person to sponsor TWO Gannsen baptisms across the seven-baptism series — the 1866 firstborn (where he was unmarried, age 20) and the 1870 Katherine Carolina baptism (where he had just married into the Albrecht family seven weeks earlier). His marriage to Fredericka Albrecht (5 April 1870) — sister to Catharina Albrecht who married Carl Kreichelt nine months later — made him brother-in-law to Carl and tied the Pillmann-Albrecht-Kreichelt families into a single marriage chain. Why this matters for understanding Charles: The 1866 sponsorship is exceptional: at age 20, unmarried and not yet kin to Auguste, Pillmann was clearly already part of the wider Albrecht / Kreichelt social circle — meaning the Charles & Auguste social world in 1865-1866 already included the man who would later marry Catharina's sister. The first child Friedrich Wilhelm Gonnson shares both given names with Pillmann — a suggestive parallel, though "Friedrich Wilhelm" was a very common German given-name pairing of the period, so a direct naming-after cannot be asserted without further documentation. FamilySearch profile L17N-8Q6 Born 1846, Germany Wife Fredericka Albrecht — m. 5 April 1870 Occupation Butcher · St. Louis Sponsorships 2 — 186

b: Georg Andreas Kreigelt — Auguste's father, Battery B veteran, sponsor at the 1866 firstborn baptism — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / People who knew Charles / Georg Andreas Kreigelt Georg Andreas Kreigelt ⚜ Charles's father-in-law · Clausthal-born Hannoverian miner-turned-Civil-War-soldier · Battery B veteran & pensioner · sponsor at the 1866 firstborn baptism · walked Auguste down the aisle BAPTISM SPONSOR · 1866 Father-in-law to Charles Gannsen BATTERY B veteran Born Clausthal · 1819 FamilySearch · LZXC-NH9 ★ At a Glance SPONSOR PROFILE · IDENTIFIED · IN-LAW FS LZXC-NH9 Charles's father-in-law — Auguste's father. A Hannoverian who brought his eight-child family to St. Louis in 1853 and later served alongside Charles in the same Civil War battery. Researcher view (sources, citations, bottom-line) Georg Andreas Kreigelt / Kreichelt (1819–1883) was Charles's father-in-law and the patriarch of the Hannoverian Kreichelt-Kreigelt St. Louis household. Born Clausthal, Lower Saxony; emigrated 1853 with his eight-child family; served as a Civil War soldier in Battery B, 2nd Missouri Light Artillery (the same regiment as Charles); walked his daughter Auguste down the aisle at her 7 January 1866 wedding to Charles. He sponsored three of the seven Gannsen baptisms — 1866 (firstborn), 1868 (Maria Josephine), and 1872 (Auguste) — making him the most-frequent sponsor across the series. He died 1883 St. Louis with a Civil War pension awarded six months earlier. Why this matters for understanding Charles: Georg is the deepest Kreichelt-side anchor of the Gannsen baptismal sponsorship pattern — three appearances over six years, plus the wedding-procession role, plus the Battery B comrade-in-arms tie to Charles. The Charles–Georg relationship spans family + military + parish bonds simultaneously. His 1866 + 1868 + 1872 baptismal pattern shows he was the senior male anchor of the Gannsen family network. FamilySearch profile LZXC-NH9 Born 1819

She sponsored three of Charles and Auguste's seven baptisms. Researcher view (sources, citations, bottom-line) Louise Friederike "Henriette" Weiss (1820, Clausthal) was Charles's mother-in-law and the matriarch of the eight-child Kreichelt-Kreigelt St. Louis household. She married Georg Andreas Kreigelt in 1844 in Clausthal; emigrated 1853. She sponsored three Gannsen baptisms — 1866 (firstborn, recorded as "Henriette"), 1872 (Auguste, recorded as "Louise" using her formal first name), and 1873 (Louise, recorded as "Louise Henriette Kreichelt née Weis" — the granddaughter named directly for her). The three different-name renderings across the same person are a worked example of 19th-c. parish-clerk name-form variation. Why this matters for understanding Charles: Henriette is the female Kreichelt-side sponsor with the most appearances (3, vs. Babette's 2) and the senior matron of the Charles & Auguste household network. Her 1873 namesake-sponsorship for granddaughter Louise is the clearest namesake-line in the series. The "Henriette/Louise/Louise Henriette" name-form variation across three baptism entries provides a methodological precedent for reading other unstable name-forms in the parish register. b: Henry Niemeier — 26-year acquaintance — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / People who knew Charles / Henry Niemeier Henry Niemeier 26-year acquaintance · 1891 pension witness · north-side German immigrant (Charles was south-side) Limited record Pension witness · 1891 Knew Charles since 1865 ★ At a Glance CIVILIAN WITNESS · 26-YEAR ACQUAINTANCE The longest-tenure civilian witness in Charles's pension file — a Westphalia-born acquaintance whose 1890 affidavit attested to 26 years of personal acquaintance. Henry Niemeier (Herman Heinrich Niemeyer · FS LKY7-31G · b. 11 April 1840 Westphalia — Tecklenburg/Lengerich district per surname cluster · d. 22 January 1908 Old Monroe, Lincoln County, MO) is the longest-tenure civilian acquaintance witness in Charles's pension file. His 15 October 1891 General Affidavit attests 26 unbroken years of intimate acquaintance, beginning 1865 — the year Charles came home from the war. He swears Charles was already hard of hearing at first acquaintance in 1865 and that the deafness has worsened since. Why this matters for understanding Charles: Niemeier's testimony bridges the 1865-1890 gap in Charles's medical history that the pension file otherwise covers only at its endpoints. By placing Charles's hearing loss at 1865 — not 1890 when the pension was filed — Niemeier's account is consistent with the Battery-B comrades' affidavits placing the origin of the deafness at the September 1863 New Madrid cannonading, two years earlier. He is a north-side German immigrant (1300 N. 9th Street) — a different St. Louis sub-community from Charles's south-side Soulard / DeKalb / Sidney neighborhood — making his testimony cross-network rather than coming from Charles's immediate parish circle. Born 11 April 1840 · likely Westphalia / Tecklenburg, Prussia Died 22 January 1908 · Old Monroe, Lincoln Co., MO Wife Caroline / Cathrina (Goldschmidt?)
