The list is taken from the 40-page Civil War Pension File and the St. Marcus Marriage Register entry of 7 January 1866. Each profile collects what we know about the person — their age, their St. Louis address, when they first met Charles, what they swore to, and whatever later civic record (city directory, census, FamilySearch tree) we have been able to pin down. Click any name to open its profile. Battery B comrades Three men of Battery B, 2nd Missouri Light Artillery — Charles's company. All three independently confirmed by the U.S. National Park Service's Soldiers and Sailors System and the War Department's records check inside the pension file. Battery B · Sergeant · wedding witness Adam Herweck ⚜ Senior groomsman at Charles's 1866 wedding. Same rank trajectory as Charles (Pvt → Sgt). Born 16 July 1831 Lampertheim, Hessen. Married Marguerite Wamsganz 21 July 1868 St. Louis; relocated to Edwardsville Illinois between 1873 and 1880; died 11 May 1897 there. Two earlier children of Adam & Margaretha (Emma 1870, Margaretha 1873) traced at St. Marcus — sponsors were Wamsgans-side relatives, NOT Charles Gannsen. Third child Charles C Herweck (b. 1874) was likely baptized in Edwardsville; the namesake-Charles-Gannsen hypothesis is now weakly disfavored. b: Jacob Reck (1837–1898) — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / People who knew Charles / Jacob Reck Jacob Reck ✓ FamilySearch KLPB-52X · Pfalz-born German Evangelisch-Reformierte (Calvinist) · co-sponsor at the 1 June 1868 St. Marcus baptism of Maria Josephine Gonnson · buried Old St. Marcus Cemetery alongside Charles Gannsen BAPTISM SPONSOR · 1868 ✓ IDENTIFIED · FS KLPB-52X b. 1 Feb 1837 Freinsheim, Pfalz d. 29 May 1898 St. Louis ★ At a Glance SPONSOR PROFILE · IDENTIFIED Latest research pass: 7 May 2026 A Bavarian-born St. Louis Calvinist who co-sponsored the 1868 baptism of Charles and Auguste's second child, and was later buried alongside Charles at Old St. Marcus Cemetery. Jacob Reck is fully identified as FS KLPB-52X — born 28 January 1837 Freinsheim, Pfalz, Bavaria, christened German Reformed (Calvinist), the son of Georg Conrad Reck × Sophia Schmidt. He arrived NY on 20 November 1865 on the ship Louis Henry from Bremen at age 26, listed as Farmer (Germans to America Index, FS ark:/61903/1:1:KD7J-CTS ). In St. Louis he had three successive marriages — Josephine née von Delien (~1866–1867, died ~1868–69) , Emma Weingardt (1869–1891) , and Frances née Bertke Stanze (1893–1898) — plus four documented children with Emma. He died 29 May 1898 and was buried at Old Saint Marcus Cemetery , the same parish-circle burial ground where Charles Gannsen was interred five years later. Why this matters for understanding Charles: Jacob is one of a small handful of non-Kreichelt-side sponsors across the seven Gannsen baptisms — alongside Erwin Spraul (1875 Erwin Carl baptism), Maria Agnes Götz (also 1868), and Wilhelmine Ebeling (1866 firstborn). His Pfalz birth (~250 km south of Charles's Trendelburg ) makes a shared-home-village old-country tie very unlikely — the Charles–Jacob bond was St. Louis-formed through the St. Marcus parish circle . The Old St. Marcus Cemetery alignment with Charles is one of the strongest single pieces of cemetery

The 1860 Lewis Thurbecker household at St. Louis County is the only Missouri Thurbecker entry surfaced so far in indexed records — placing the family in St. Louis a year before the war and consistent with the Westphalian emigration pattern that also produced the Hebbeler, Niemeier, and Adam Herweck branches of Charles's circle. b: Josephine née von Delien (~1830s/40s–1868/69) — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / People who knew Charles / Josephine née von Delien Josephine née von Delien ✓ First wife of Jacob Reck (FS KLPB-52X ) · co-sponsor at the 1 June 1868 St. Marcus baptism of Maria Josephine Gonnson · the child's middle name is her direct namesake BAPTISM SPONSOR · 1868 ✓ SURNAME CONFIRMED · DELIEN / DELIAN ★ ORIGIN: Belgian-Flemish (Antwerp / Brabant) individual ID still open ★ At a Glance SPONSOR PROFILE · INDIVIDUAL ID OPEN Latest research pass: 7 May 2026 First wife of Jacob Reck and a co-sponsor at the 1868 baptism of Charles and Auguste's second child. Josephine is the first wife of Jacob Reck and the only one of the fifteen distinct named sponsors at the seven Gannsen baptisms whose individual identity remains open. The 1 June 1868 St. Marcus parish-clerk hand reads "Jakob Reck u. J. G. · Josephine geb. von Delien" — the surname reading is well-supported as Delien / Delian (the 2020 transcription, a 2 May 2026 high-res re-read of the same entry, and a Concordia Cemetery cross-reference) — though the individual's identity remains open, and the 7 May 2026 deep search produced the project's first solid geographic-origin lock: Delien is unequivocally a Belgian-Flemish surname (Forebears.io: 503 worldwide bearers, ~84 % Flemish Belgium; FS: 553 tree people in Noord-Brabant). The German von is the parish-clerk's transliteration of Dutch van . Josephine was therefore most likely a Catholic Brabant-Antwerp immigrant who reached St. Louis via Antwerp port, met Jacob there 1865–67, sponsored Maria Josephine on 1 June 1868, and died sometime before 27 July 1869 (when Jacob remarried) — most likely in childbirth. Why this matters for understanding Charles: Of the fifteen distinct named sponsors, Josephine is the last open ID. The new Belgian-Flemish lock reframes the Reck-Gannsen relationship as a St. Louis-formed bond crossing the Belgian-Catholic / Germ

Researcher view (sources, citations, bottom-line) Katherine Misch (FS 2B2C-KQQ · b. 1875 St. Louis · d. 1948 St. Louis age 73) is the second-generation knot that connects the Charles Konzen line to the Theodore Gottlob line . She was the daughter of Michael Misch × Christina Hahl , and via her mother's second marriage (to Theodore Gottlob Sr.) she became half-sister to Frederick Gottlob — the documented namesake of her own son Frederick Charles Johnson (b. 1905). She married Charles's son Charles Johnson Jr. (formally Erwin Carl Konsen) and was a lifelong St. Louis resident at 168/170/175 Sidney Street through six US censuses (1880–1940). Why this matters for understanding Charles: Katherine Misch is the documentary linchpin that explains the previously-mysterious "named after his Uncle Frederick Gottlob" custom fact on Frederick Charles Johnson's FS tree. d: Identified · FS M6G9-FJR · Dorothea Wilhelmine Ebeling Leue · b. 1810 Fallersleben, Germany · d. 19 Dec 1872 St. Louis · m. Heinrich Andraes Daniel Leue 1840 · 1866 St. Marcus baptism sponsor for the firstborn Friedrich Wilhelm Gonnson
b: Dorothea Wilhelmine Ebeling Leue (1810–1872) — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / People who knew Charles / Wilhelmine Ebeling Leue Dorothea Wilhelmine Ebeling Leue ★ Identified on FamilySearch as M6G9-FJR · wife of Heinrich Andraes Daniel Leue · 1866 St. Marcus baptism sponsor for the firstborn Friedrich Wilhelm Gonnson BAPTISM SPONSOR · 1866 ✓ IDENTIFIED · FS M6G9-FJR b. 1810 Fallersleben · d. 19 Dec 1872 St. Louis ★ At a Glance SPONSOR PROFILE · IDENTIFIED Identification confirmed: 6 May 2026 A sponsor at the 1866 baptism of Charles and Auguste's firstborn — a senior Soulard / Lafayette-area German Lutheran neighbour during the family's South 7th Street years. Dorothea Wilhelmine Ebeling Leue is now positively identified as the 1866 St. Marcus baptism sponsor previously recorded only as "Wilhelmina Ebeling." She was a senior Soulard / Lafayette-area German Lutheran neighbor of Charles & Auguste during their 1518 South 7th Street residence period, b. 1810 Fallersleben (Lower Saxony), m. Heinrich Andraes Daniel Leue 1840 in Hornburg, emigrated to St. Louis, lived at the NW corner of Ann Avenue and Elizabeth Street , d. 19 December 1872 — buried Gatewood Gardens (formerly New Picker's) Cemetery. The match was confirmed by six aligning evidence threads (surname match, given-name exact match, walking-distance Soulard geography, Lutheran religion, age-fit at 56–59, timing-fit with subsequent absence) and an FS-tree cross-check where her death date, both surviving daughters' death years, and the predeceased third daughter all align with the 1872 Westliche Post obituary. Why this matters for understanding Charles: Three of the four 1866 firstborn-baptism sponsors were Auguste's Kreichelt-side kin; Wilhelmine was the fourth , the one non-kin sponsor — a senior parish-circle neighbor. Her identification opens up the Soulard / Lafayette German Lutheran social network as Charles's working immediate-environment in 1865–1867 — and notably,

b: Deisel, Kreis Hofgeismar — Research Log — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Key Evidence / "Deisse, Churhessen" "Deisse, Kreis Cassel, Churhessen" — Research Log The 1866 St. Marcus marriage register's place-of-origin · 2020 toponym anchor · still the project's strongest village-level identifier 2020 → 2026 search arc Source: 1866 St. Marcus marriage register · entry 676 ★ What this tells us — why Deisse = Deisel "aus Deisse, Kreis Cassel, Churhessen " is the project's most explicit village-level identifier — and it decodes to Deisel, Kreis Hofgeismar , Hessen-Nassau (Lutheran parish: Deisel) . Unlike the Civil War clerk's "Freundburg," this reading is supported by the German-Lutheran pastor's own pen, a published gazetteer match, and direct geographic continuity with the Trendelburg anchor 5 km away. Step 1 — the pastor's transcription (St. Marcus, 7 January 1866): When Charles married Auguste Kreichelt at St. Marcus, the German-Lutheran pastor G.W. Wall took down Charles's home origin in his own German hand. Pastor Wall was a native German speaker writing in German for a German-speaking congregation — exactly the conditions under which a Hessian villager's place-name would be captured most faithfully. The entry reads: " aus Deisse, Kreis Cassel, Churhessen " The mishearing here is much smaller than the Civil War clerk's. Pastor Wall hears Deisel and writes Deisse — collapsing the final unstressed "-l" into a fricative "-sse" ending, which is a regular feature of German handwritten 19th-century parish registers when capturing rural place-names. Every other element of the place-string is correctly captured: Deise l → Deis se final "-el" → fricative "-sse" (regular pastor's transcription) Kreis Hofgeismar → Kreis Cassel substitution of capital-city locator for the precise Kreis-level subdivision (see explanation below) Churhessen → Kurhessen archaic "Chur-" spelling = "Kur-" (same political unit) Why "Kreis Cassel" instead of "K

t: Freundburg, Kurhessen — Research Log — Charles Research
d: "Freundburg, Kurhessen" is the project's foundational geographic anchor — and it most likely decodes to Trendelburg, Kreis Hofgeismar. The decoding is a phonetic inference: a proposed substitution by an English-speaking Civil War…
b: Freundburg, Kurhessen — Research Log — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Key Evidence / "Freundburg, Kurhessen" "Freundburg, Kurhessen" — Research Log The Civil War enlistment record's place-of-birth · 2020 Freudenberg-on-Rhine eliminated · 2026 Trendelburg phonetic reading is the project's working interpretation 2020 → 2026 search arc Source: 1862 Compiled Service Record (Doc O) ★ What this tells us — why Freundburg → most likely Trendelburg "Freundburg, Kurhessen " is the project's foundational geographic anchor — and it most likely points to Trendelburg, Kreis Hofgeismar . This is a phonetic inference — Peter Schräder's proposed re-reading of a German place-name misheard by an English-speaking Civil War clerk in 1862 — not a documentary fact. It is a strong inference because a second, independent record (the 1866 marriage register's "Deisel") corroborates it from a different direction, but it remains an inference until a parish record positively identifies Charles's home village. Step 1 — the phonetic substitution (Peter Schräder, 26 Feb & 6 May 2026): Charles, a native German speaker one year out of basic English, gives his birthplace orally to a recruiting clerk on 2 December 1862. The clerk hears the spoken German place-name Trendelburg and writes the English-shaped Freundburg . The mishearing is regular, not random: Tr- endel-burg → Fr- eund-burg "Tr-" cluster flattens to "Fr-" in an English ear Tr- endel -burg → Fr- eund -burg meaningless "endel" → familiar German Freund ("friend") Trendel- burg → Freund- burg German place-suffix -burg survives intact (clerk recognises it) The clerk substitutes a meaningful German word he knows ( Freund ) for a meaningless place-name fragment ( Trendel ) — exactly the substitution mechanism documented in the Toeppe → Deppe naming-variant methodology . "Freundburg" is therefore not a real place; it's a phonetic fingerprint of "Trendelburg" passed through one Civil-War-clerk's ear. Step 2

t: German Baptism / Birth Record — Research Log — Charles Research
h: German Baptism / Birth Record — Research Log
d: The identity of Charles's biological parents — the central question of the entire investigation. A baptism entry in the right Hofgeismar Kirchenkreis parish would carry:
b: German Baptism / Birth Record — Research Log — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Key Evidence / Evidence not found German Baptism / Birth Record — Research Log Identity of Charles's biological parents · ~30-session sweep · all confidently-identified candidates eliminated EVIDENCE NOT FOUND Hofgeismar Kirchenkreis · 1834-1836 window ★ What this record would tell us if found The identity of Charles's biological parents — the central question of the entire investigation. A baptism entry in the right Hofgeismar Kirchenkreis parish would carry: Father's name — resolving the Konze-vs-other-surname question. Establishes whether the Konsen surname recorded at the 1875 St. Marcus baptism of Erwin Carl Konsen reflects a Konze paternal line, a Gansen line, or another spelling. Mother's name and maiden name — testing the Wilhelmine Hildebrand hypothesis (the project's working candidate for Charles's mother). Godparents (Pathen) — typically maternal/paternal kin, naming siblings, uncles, and aunts in a single entry. The Hessian baptism convention names 2-4 Pathen, often the parents' own siblings. Exact birth date and birthplace — settling the 25 Jan / 26 Jan birthdate ambiguity (death certificate vs. St. Marcus burial record vs. pension affidavit). Marginalia — German parish books often record later events in the margin: confirmation, marriage, emigration, death. A single marginal "ausgewandert nach Amerika 1859" annotation would close the entire emigration question. Legitimate vs. illegitimate — relevant for any birth record: illegitimate Hessian children often name only the mother. Many illegitimate Hessian children have only the mother named, with the father added separately if paternity was acknowledged. Why this record should exist Lutheran and Reformed parishes in Kurhessen kept comprehensive baptism registers ( Taufregister ). Every Christian birth in the 1834-1836 window — Charles's claimed birth window — should appear in some paris

t: Hessian Auswanderer-Beleg — Research Log — Charles Research
d: The Hessian state archive's emigration registry recorded — for legal emigrants — a structured set of biographical fields:
b: Hessian Auswanderer-Beleg — Research Log — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Key Evidence / Evidence not found Hessian Auswanderer-Beleg — Research Log State-archive emigration registration · Hessisches Staatsarchiv Marburg · all searches negative · re-searched 25 May 2026 EVIDENCE NOT FOUND Marburg HStAM Best. 16 / Best. 17b ★ What this record would tell us if found The Hessian state archive's emigration registry recorded — for legal emigrants — a structured set of biographical fields: Home parish (Heimat) — would resolve the central geographic question of the entire investigation : which Hofgeismar Kirchenkreis village did Charles actually come from. Trendelburg? Deisel? Schöneberg? Hümme? Helmarshausen? Friedrichsfeld? An entry would name it. Age and birth year — recorded by the state at the moment of registration, free from American clerks' approximations. Profession — which would establish his pre-emigration trade (Schuhmacher / Tagelöhner / Bäcker / Schmied?). Comparable Belege for adjacent emigrants record this field consistently. Religion — Lutheran / Reformed / Catholic / Jewish — a field worth recording for any emigrant. Destination country — recorded as "Amerika" or specific U.S. state if known to the registrar. Date of registration — usually 1-3 months before actual departure. Reference to the underlying parish or local emigration list — chaining backward to even earlier records. Its absence is itself diagnostic. If Charles is not in any Auswanderer-Beleg , that suggests his emigration was un-registered with the Hessian authorities — consistent with an illegal / unauthorised departure. See the Hessian Stammrolle research log for the linked draft-evasion hypothesis. Why this record should exist (or not) Kurhessen (Electorate of Hesse) required emigrants to register with the state before departing, especially if they had not yet fulfilled military obligation. The registers (Auswanderer-Belege) are held at Hessisches S

d: The Electorate of Hesse maintained Stammrollen (recruitment rolls / military registers) for every male citizen who reached age 20 (the conscription threshold). The system functioned without…
b: Hessian Military Draft / Stammrolle — Research Log — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Key Evidence / Evidence not found Hessian Military Draft / Stammrolle — Research Log Kurhessen recruitment register · 1855-1859 conscription window (20th-birthday cohort + flight period) · all searches negative · likely most informative single record if recovered EVIDENCE NOT FOUND Marburg HStAM Bestand 4h ★ What this record would tell us if found The Stammrolle would carry, in a single state-recorded entry made when Charles turned 20 (~1855): Full birth name — including any second / middle name dropped in U.S. records. Resolves the "L. Charles Gannsen" L-prefix question (Ludwig? Louis?). Exact birth date — locks the 25 Jan / 26 Jan ambiguity. Parish of registration — the same field every other German-side record needs but does not yet provide. Localises Charles's home village within the Kreis Hofgeismar . Parents' names — usually the father's name and occupation, sometimes the mother. Equivalent to the baptism record's parental field but recorded by the state. Height, eye colour, hair colour, distinguishing marks — usable for cross-reference with the 1862 U.S. enlistment record (which captured: 5'5", fair complexion, blue eyes, blond hair). Trade / occupation at age 20 — would establish his pre-emigration profession in 1855, four years before he left Germany. Conscription disposition — Drafted? Excused (medical / family)? Fled / desertiert? Why the Stammrolle is potentially the highest-value record in the entire project: the conscription disposition field would directly explain why the project finds no Hessian Auswanderer-Beleg for Charles. A " desertiert " or " nicht erschienen " annotation would establish that Charles emigrated illegally to evade conscription — a documented 1850s pattern in Hesse-Kassel. That would close the question of why he is missing from every Hessian state-emigration record, and would also explain the surname-spellin

t: Parish Search Coverage — German Confirmation Registers — Charles Research
h: Parish Search Coverage — German Confirmation Registers
d: A scannable record of which Hessian parish confirmation registers have been searched for Charles's birth — the June 2026 'any Carl, 1833–1841, surname open' sweep across the Kirchenkreis Hofgeismar. Parish, register, window, method, and result, plus what remains for the on-site trip.
b: Parish Search Coverage — German Confirmation Registers — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Parish Search Coverage Parish Search Coverage — German Confirmation Registers What has been searched for Charles's birth in the Hessian parishes, and what has not. The June 2026 widened sweep: any boy named Carl, born 1833–1841, surname open — read straight off the confirmation registers. A conclusion is only as strong as the searches behind it. This page is the at-a-glance ledger for the 2026 confirmation-register sweep across the Kirchenkreis Hofgeismar — one row per parish, with the register searched, the birth window covered, the method, and the result. It is the companion to the longer-running negative-results audit (which logs the earlier Konze-focused baptism searches) and feeds the GPS test of reasonably exhaustive research. 8 parishes in this sweep 3 boys named Carl found in window 0 with a Konze / *n-s-n surname 1833–1841 birth window searched What this sweep was looking for The earlier searches (logged in the negative-results audit ) filtered on the surname — they looked for a Konze -family child, and outside the core parishes they narrowed to roughly January–February 1835. A negative-results re-audit exposed two blind spots in that approach, so this sweep deliberately widened all four criteria: Given name is the anchor Any Carl / Karl (the German form of Charles) — not a surname. Charles named a son Charles, and put “Charles” in a grandson's name, so the forename is the thread. Surname open Any surname, especially endings that could be heard as *n·s·n (Gansen, Ganssen, Gonsen, Gonson, Hansen, Jansen, Konzen…), legitimate or illegitimate. Full window Birth 1833–1841 (core 1834–1838). The U.S. records scatter Charles's birth across these years; 26 Jan 1835 is the working anchor, not the boundary. See Birth date across the records . Both confessions Lutheran and Reformed registers wherever a parish kept both — Kurhessen was split

d: Peter Schräder re-read the original Kurrent script of Entry 195 in May 2026 and corrected the project's prior interpretation on multiple counts. The Entry 195 child cannot be Charles Gannsen. Per…
b: Entry 195 — Deisel KB 218842 Baptism — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Source Archive / Primary Sources Entry 195 — Deisel KB 218842 Baptism High-resolution image · January 1835 · ELIMINATED 4 May 2026 PRIMARY SOURCE Archion image · Deisel KB 218842 Sent to Peter Schräder · 26 April 2026 ELIMINATED · 4 May 2026 ★ Eliminated · Peter Schräder reading · 4 May 2026 Peter Schräder re-read the original Kurrent script of Entry 195 in May 2026 and corrected the project's prior interpretation on multiple counts. The Entry 195 child cannot be Charles Gannsen. Per Peter's email of 4 May 2026 ("Re: Quentins" thread): The child received no first name — the field previously transcribed as "Justus" actually reads "männlich" (male), the convention used in Lutheran registers when an infant dies without baptism. The child was born and died three days later, without baptism . Charles, by contrast, lived to age ~60 in St. Louis. Entry 195 cannot be him. The father's family-name was Küster / Kuester (Köster) , not Konze; profession Tagelöhner (day-laborer) , not Kaufmann. The mother is therefore not the same Wilhelmine geb. Hildebrand who appears at the 1849 Confirmation Entry 261 (which records the Joh. Köster Kaufmann household — a separate Deisel Köster family). Peter notes the absence of baptism is itself unusual: sick infants were typically baptised quickly. He infers the boy was healthy and died suddenly. This page is preserved as the historical record of how the project reached and then resolved this hypothesis. The corrected entry on the home page is in Section "Key Evidence" #6 ; the Open Question A around the child's name is also now resolved. About this document This is the high-resolution scan of Entry 195 in Deisel Kirchenbuch KB 218842 — for several weeks in April 2026 the leading-hypothesis entry for Charles's birth registration. The image was sent to and discussed with Peter Schräder on 26 April 2026 in the course of the Andreas K

b: Charles Gannsen — Fold3 / NARA M405 Compiled Service Records — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Source Archive / Primary Sources Fold3 / NARA M405 — Compiled Service Records Charles Gannsen · Battery B, 2nd Missouri Light Artillery · 25-page muster-card scan via Fold3 PRIMARY SOURCE Civil War service · PDF · 25 pp. NARA M405 · Roll 0327 Companion to Doc O ★ Sister source: a second, independent scan of the same M405 carded series — the NARA mail-order Compiled Service Record packet (G11-422819443E, 13 pp.) → — has its own dedicated page with full transcription, Fold3-vs-NARA diff, and consolidated service summary. Treat the two pages as separate, paired primary sources. The NARA scan is dramatically clearer on the spring–fall 1865 cards; for any reading where the two packets disagree, the NARA page documents the cleaner reading. About this document This is the complete Compiled Service Record for Charles Gannsen as it appears in the National Archives microfilm series NARA M405 ("Compiled service records of volunteer Union soldiers who served in organizations from the State of Missouri"), retrieved via Fold3 from image #228767500 on Roll 0327 ( Second Light Artillery, Fo–Gh ). It expands on the single-card Compiled Service Record (Doc O) that the 2020 AncestryProGenealogists journal indexed: where Doc O reproduces only the enlistment-summary card, this 25-page Fold3 download reproduces the complete card series the National Archives compiled for Charles between 1890 and 1912 from the original muster rolls. The "Compiled Service Records" are the result of a federal post-war records-consolidation project: in 1890 Congress directed the War Department to abstract every appearance of every Union volunteer soldier from the original company muster rolls onto individual cards. Each card records one event — an enlistment, a muster-in, a presence-or-absence at each subsequent muster, hospital admissions, a discharge. The cards for a given

b: Charles Gannsen — NARA Mail-Order Compiled Service Record (G11-422819443E) — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Source Archive / Primary Sources NARA Mail-Order Compiled Service Record — G11-422819443E Charles Gannsen · Battery B, 2nd Missouri Light Artillery · 13-page muster-card packet from the National Archives, Washington · ordered June 2018 PRIMARY SOURCE Civil War service · PDF · 13 pp. NARA M405 · NATF Form 86 Sister source to Fold3 + pension file About this document This is the NARA mail-order Compiled Military Service Record for Charles Gannsen — the photocopy packet that the National Archives produces in response to a NATF Form 86 request for a Civil War volunteer soldier's carded service file. The packet was ordered 26 June 2018 (NARA service ticket G11-422819443E) and arrived as a 13-page PDF from the Adjutant General's Office Compiled Service Records series — NARA M405 , the same microfilm publication that the Fold3 download (25 pp.) draws from. This 13-page NARA mail-order packet is a distinct artifact from the 25-page full carded service record as scanned by Fold3: both reproduce the same underlying M405 carded series, but the NARA copy binds 2–3 cards per page and omits Fold3's duplicate-clerk transcripts, so the smaller page count reflects packaging — not a smaller record. Both packets reproduce the same underlying carded-records series; they differ in scan source (NARA paper photocopy vs. Fold3 digital pull), completeness (NARA includes one card per muster period; Fold3 carries duplicate-clerk transcripts of several periods), and legibility (the NARA scan is dramatically clearer on several previously-illegible cards, in particular Special Order No. 92 and the spring-1865 Rolla detachment cards). Together with the 40-page pension file (NARA F41-523680269E) and the Fold3 scan , this NARA packet is one of the three primary-source pillars of the Civil War record. It is the single richest U.S.-side primary source on Charles's family, residences, military career, and physical condition. ★ → Open the "People who knew Charles" index for individual profile pages on every named witness, comrade, and physician below. d: A baptism-by-baptism sponsor audit for Charles Konzen × Auguste Kreichelt's seven children at St. Marcus, 1866–1881 — with each sponsor's documented kin relationship to Auguste and the explicit absence of any Charles-side blood relative.
b: Sponsor reconciliation — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Sponsor reconciliation Sponsor reconciliation A baptism-by-baptism audit of every named sponsor across the seven Gannsen children's baptisms at St. Marcus German Evangelical Church, St. Louis, 1866–1881 — with each sponsor's documented kin relationship to Auguste Kreichelt, and the explicit absence of any documented Charles-side blood relative. The "immigrated alone" hypothesis on this site rests on a structural claim: across seven baptisms over fifteen years, not a single named sponsor is a documented blood relative of Charles. This page turns that narrative into an auditable artifact — one row per baptism × sponsor, with the kin link to Auguste named explicitly and the absence of a Charles-side link logged in every row. Thesis Across the seven Gannsen baptisms at St. Marcus (1866–1881), 17 distinct sponsorship slots were filled by 15 named individuals — two people came back to stand up at a later baptism, most prominently Auguste's father Georg Kreichelt , with the affinal in-law Friedrich Pillmann also recurring, which is why the 17 slots map to 15 distinct named individuals. Of those 15 individuals, every one is documented as a Kreichelt-side relative, in-law, or St. Louis parish-circle associate of Auguste's family . Zero are documented blood relatives of Charles. German-Lutheran baptism sponsorship in this period was structurally a kin-default convention; the asymmetry across all seven baptisms over fifteen years is consistent with Charles having no German blood family accessible by mail or in person between 1866 and 1880 — corroborating the working hypothesis that he immigrated alone in 1859 and never re-established contact close enough to bring kin to the baptismal font. 7 Baptisms 17 Sponsorship slots 15 Distinct sponsors 0 Charles-side blood relatives 5 Pastors 15 Years (1866–1881) Legend Auguste-side blood — documented Kreichelt parent / sibling / aunt August

t: Start Here — For Researchers — Charles Research
d: The researcher's front door to the Charles Konzen project: the working conclusion and its status, the core evidence pages, what's already been searched, and the open threads — each linked and grouped by purpose.
b: Start Here — For Researchers — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Start Here Start Here — For Researchers A front door for collaborating genealogists. The working conclusion and where it stands, the core evidence, what has already been searched, and the threads still open — each linked and grouped by purpose. If you're picking this project up to evaluate the evidence or carry the search forward, start here. The handful of pages below are the load-bearing ones; everything else on the site supports them. Where things stand Charles Konzen (recorded in U.S. records as Gannsen / Gonson / Johnson; ~26 January 1835 – 16 December 1903) was a Hessian-born sergeant of Battery B, 2nd Missouri Light Artillery. His birth is held as ~26 January 1835 in Kurhessen on a working basis — the date rests on a single post-mortem parish burial entry, and no German baptism has yet been found. The origin is a working conclusion pointing to the Trendelburg / Deisel area of the Kirchenkreis Hofgeismar; the most-wanted document remains Charles's baptism register. Birth date: 26 Jan 1835 — Working Origin: Trendelburg/Deisel — Working German baptism — not yet found If you read three things The fastest orientation ★ Abstract The formal one-page summary of who Charles was, the evidence, and the working conclusions. ★ Claims dashboard Every factual claim in the project with its confidence status (Confirmed / Working) and the evidence behind it. ★ Parish search coverage What's already been searched in the German registers, and what hasn't — so you don't repeat ground or miss a gap. 1 · The conclusion What the project argues, and how sure ★ Abstract The formal summary — claims, evidence, and open questions in one page. ★ Claims dashboard Each claim with a Confirmed / Working status and its supporting documents. t: Story 1 · A photograph in a moving box — Charles Research
h: A photograph in a moving box
d: For more than a hundred years no one had a face to put to Charles. Then in September 2025 a descendant in Independence, Missouri found one in a moving
b: Story 1 · A photograph in a moving box — Charles Research Skip to main content Charles Research / Stories / Story 1 A photograph in a moving box Story 1 of 9 · from the cinematic investigation Story 01 A photograph in a moving box September 2025 · Independence, Missouri The portrait. Louis Roth studio, 2006 South Broadway, St. Louis, c. 1896–1903. For more than a hundred years no one had a face to put to Charles. Then, in September 2025, a descendant in Independence, Missouri found one in a moving box he was unpacking. His name is Maximilian J. He was hanging family photographs on a wall when he came across a small studio portrait of an elderly man with a stiff collar and a heavy moustache. On the back, a previous owner had written in pencil: "Johnson · Father of Charles · Father of Henry · Father of Robert." The portrait was taken at the Louis Roth photo studio at 2006 South Broadway in St. Louis — 0.7 miles from Charles's DeKalb Street home. The studio operated from 1890 to 1907. The man in the photograph is in late middle age, somewhere between sixty-two and sixty-eight years old. Charles was sixty-one when his wife Auguste died in 1896, sixty-eight when he himself died in December 1903. The dating brackets the photograph between those two deaths. Maximilian J. emailed Jed on 18 September 2025. The historical context — the studio dates, the age range, the inscription naming three sons in the right family — was established three days later, in the 21 September reply. By that evening the portrait had been uploaded to Charles's FamilySearch profile and the project had its first face.

t: Story 2 · The recruiter and the recruit — Charles Research
d: Charles's enlistment, the man who signed him up, and how the two of them would meet again — four years later, at the marriage entry.
b: Story 2 · The recruiter and the recruit — Charles Research Skip to main content Charles Research / Stories / Story 2 The recruiter and the recruit Story 2 of 9 · from the cinematic investigation Story 02 The recruiter and the recruit 2 December 1862 → 7 January 1866 · St. Louis & New Madrid "…of my Company, [is on] recruiting service at St. Louis." Captain of Battery B, writing from New Madrid on 9 December 1862. The Sergeant on St. Louis recruiting service was Adam Herweck — who had recruited Charles into Battery B one week earlier. Charles enlisted in St. Louis on 2 December 1862. Eighteen days later, the captain of Battery B wrote home from New Madrid that one of his sergeants was on recruiting service at that exact place that exact week. The sergeant's name was Adam Herweck . Three years later he stood as Charles's senior groomsman at the wedding. The captain's letter is dated 9 December 1862, written from New Madrid, Missouri. "Adam Herweck, of my Company, [is on] recruiting service at St. Louis." The dates triangulate perfectly: Charles enlisted 2 December, Adam was the recruiter on the ground that week, the captain confirmed it a week later. Documentary alignment of who was where. Adam was Hessian — born 16 July 1831 in Lampertheim, the same Hesse-Darmstadt region that would later record Charles's birthplace as the mishearing-friendly *Trendelburg*. He had emigrated earlier and had been in Battery B since April 1861. At Charles's enlistment Adam was 31, Charles was 27. Same Pvt → Sgt rank trajectory followed. Three years later, on 7 January 1866 — eighteen days after Battery B's discharge — Adam Herweck appears as the first-named witness in the witness column of the St. Marcus marriage register entry for Charles & Auguste's wedding. He was 34 and still unmarried; he wouldn't marry Marguerite Wamsganz for another 18 months. Two weeks later, on 25 January, he appears again as a witness at the Gels-Morlock wedding in the same register. He was being

t: Story 3 · The brother in the same battery — Charles Research
h: The brother in the same battery
d: How Charles came to fight in a Civil War battery alongside his own brother-in-law.
b: Story 3 · The brother in the same battery — Charles Research Skip to main content Charles Research / Stories / Story 3 The brother in the same battery Story 3 of 9 · from the cinematic investigation Story 03 The brother in the same battery 1862 – 1866 · St. Louis & New Madrid The 7 January 1866 St. Marcus marriage entry — eighteen days after Battery B's discharge. Four men in the room had served together: the groom, the senior groomsman Adam Herweck, the bride's father Georg, the bride's brother August. Two months before he proposed to Auguste, Charles had spent three years in the same artillery battery as her brother. Her father served too. The wedding eighteen days after the unit's December 1865 discharge brought four Battery B veterans to the same St. Marcus altar — the groom, the senior groomsman, the bride's father, and the bride's brother. Battery B, 2nd Missouri Light Artillery, was a German-immigrant unit. The roll included Charles (Hessian, just-arrived), Adam Herweck (Hessian, the recruiter), Georg Kreichelt (Hannoverian, age 43), and August Kreichelt (Hannoverian, age 18 — Georg's son and Auguste's brother). Four men, four different German regional origins, fighting together at New Madrid in 1863 and Vicksburg in 1864. How exactly Charles met Auguste isn't documented anywhere directly. But Battery B accounts for nearly all the male introductions in his St. Louis life. Adam Herweck recruited him in. Georg and August Kreichelt served beside him. Auguste was Georg's daughter and August's sister. The introduction was structurally inevitable — the parish (St. Marcus) had been the Kreichelt family's Lutheran congregation since their 1853 emigration, and three Hessian-Hannoverian men returning from war would naturally have ended up at the same altar. The wedding tells the rest. On 7 January 1866 — eighteen days after Battery B's discharge — Charles married Auguste at St. Marcus. The four Battery B men gathered in one room: Charles (groom), Adam Her

t: Story 4 · The firstborn and the deposed Elector — Charles Research
h: The firstborn and the deposed Elector
d: Why Charles named his first son after a Hessian prince who had been deposed three months before the baptism.
b: Story 4 · The firstborn and the deposed Elector — Charles Research Skip to main content Charles Research / Stories / Story 4 The firstborn and the deposed Elector Story 4 of 9 · from the cinematic investigation Story 04 The firstborn and the deposed Elector June – September 1866 · St. Louis & Stettin The 2 September 1866 St. Marcus baptismal entry — christened Friedrich Wilhelm , the same first names the deposed Elector of Hesse-Kassel had carried until ten weeks earlier. Charles & Auguste 's first child was born on 20 June 1866. Three days later, the Elector of Hesse — Friedrich Wilhelm I — was deposed. The baby was christened on 2 September with the same first names: Friedrich Wilhelm . The Austro-Prussian War had been declared on 17 June 1866. The trans-Atlantic telegraph cable was not operational until 27 July. So when Auguste went into labour on 20 June in St. Louis, Charles couldn't yet have known. By the September baptism he did. The Elector had been transferred as a prisoner to Stettin on 23 June; the Electorate of Hesse-Kassel was annexed by Prussia in September; the political world Charles had left in 1859 ceased to exist. Two readings are possible. Reading A: a coincidence of names — the parents had picked Friedrich Wilhelm before any of this happened, the news caught up later, and the September baptism simply followed through. Reading B: by the September baptism Charles did know, and the choice of sponsors was deliberate. The sponsors were Friedrich Wilhelm Pillmann (godfather) and Wilhelmina Ebeling (godmother) — a matched pair carrying the masculine and feminine forms of the deposed Elector's first name. The matched-pair detail makes Reading B the stronger interpretation. Either way: in a German-Evangelical parish register in St. Louis, in the very weeks the Hessian state of Charles's birth was being dismantled by Prussian troops, a German-speaking immigrant family registered the deposed Elector's name in their first child. It is hard not

t: Story 5 · The man who sponsored twice — Charles Research
d: Of the fifteen distinct named sponsors across the seven Gannsen baptisms (seventeen sponsorship slots), one man — a German butcher — stood up twice.
b: Story 5 · The man who sponsored twice — Charles Research Skip to main content Charles Research / Stories / Story 5 The man who sponsored twice Story 5 of 9 · from the cinematic investigation Story 05 The man who sponsored twice September 1866 · April 1870 · May 1870 — St. Louis 2 Sept 1866 → 5 April 1870 → 22 May 1870 Three Pillmann events in the Konzen orbit, in order: sponsor at the firstborn baptism · marriage to Fredericka Albrecht · sponsor again, seven weeks later, at Katherine Carolina's baptism. Of the named sponsors across Charles & Auguste's seven baptisms, one person — and one only — sponsored more than once. Friedrich Pillmann appears at the 1866 firstborn baptism and again at the 1870 Katherine Carolina baptism. The repetition isn't coincidence — it's a man being absorbed into the family network in real time. At the September 1866 baptism, Pillmann was 20, single, and not yet kin to the Kreichelts. He was a Soulard butcher whose family lived a few blocks from Charles & Auguste's South 7th Street home. He carried the masculine form of the deposed Elector's first name — Friedrich Wilhelm Pillmann — and stood alongside Wilhelmine Ebeling Leue (the matched-pair pair the previous story argues for). At that baptism he was a parish-circle outsider to the Kreichelts. Between the 1866 baptism and 1870, three things happened. Carl Kreichelt — Auguste's brother — became engaged to Catharina Albrecht . Catharina's sister Fredericka Albrecht became engaged to Friedrich Pillmann. The two marriages were synchronized: Pillmann × Fredericka on 5 April 1870 , Carl × Catharina on 5 March 1871. By April 1870, Pillmann was formally Kreichelt-by-marriage. Seven weeks after Pillmann's wedding, on 22 May 1870, Charles & Auguste baptised Katherine Carolina. The four named sponsors were a single tightly-intermarried Kreichelt-Albrecht-Pillmann kin pool. Pillmann appeared again — but this time as kin, not as parish-circle outsider. This is what the seven-baptism rec

t: Story 6 · The pastor who wrote two different vowels — Charles Research
h: The pastor who wrote two different vowels
d: The same pastor wrote two different spellings of the family name in two consecutive years — the "smoking gun" that the spelling came f
b: Story 6 · The pastor who wrote two different vowels — Charles Research Skip to main content Charles Research / Stories / Story 6 The pastor who wrote two different vowels Story 6 of 9 · from the cinematic investigation Story 06 The pastor who wrote two different vowels 1870 and 1872 · St. Louis 1870 (left) the surname is Gansen . 1872 (right) the same hand writes Gonsen . Pastor Braschler. Same family. Two years apart. In 1870 Pastor Braschler of St. Marcus baptised one of Charles & Auguste's daughters and recorded the family surname as Gansen . Two years later he baptised another daughter and recorded it as Gonsen . Same pastor, same family, two different vowels, two years apart. If the spelling reflected the pastor's preference, both entries would say the same thing. They don't. Braschler wrote down what he heard. The vowel he heard varied. Which means the spelling reflected Charles's voice rather than the pastor's pen. This is what genealogists sometimes call a "smoking gun" of vocal-versus-scribal capture. It is the project's strongest single piece of evidence that the eight distinct American renderings of Charles's surname are not many independent scribes' guesses at an unknown name — they are at least five different scribes' transcriptions of one spoken Hessian original. The original is the Hessian Konzen , with the silent t in the tz -affricate that English-speaking ears reduced to a simple s , and the unstressed -en ending that an American clerk could just as easily have written -on . The cluster of phonetic captures — Gonnson, Gansen, Gonsen, Konsen, Conson — all sit close to a single spoken target [ˈkɔn.sən]. It just happened to land in the middle of Pastor Braschler's vocabulary, twice, with two different vowels.

t: Story 7 · The surname spelled twice, fifteen years apart — Charles Research
h: The surname spelled twice, fifteen years apart
d: The two American records that bracket the surname trail with K-initial forms — Konzen (1890 daughter's death record, the only American attestation of the Hessian z-form and the closest American match to the Hesse cluster) and Konsen (1875 baptism, the phonetic-s sibling), fifteen years apart.
b: Story 7 · The surname spelled twice, fifteen years apart — Charles Research Skip to main content Charles Research / Stories / Story 7 The surname spelled twice, fifteen years apart Story 7 of 9 · from the cinematic investigation Story 07 The surname written twice in K-initial form, fifteen years apart 1875 and 1890 · St. Louis The 1890 death entry — Auguste's maiden name recorded as Konzen , with a z. This is the single American record carrying the full Hessian z-form documented in the Trendelburg parish since 1773. (Fifteen years earlier, a different scribe wrote Konsen with an s on her brother's baptism — the phonetic-s sibling of the same Hessian root.) Of the eight American renderings of Charles's surname, two are K-initial Hessian forms — and they bracket the surname trail fifteen years apart. Konsen appears once, on his son Erwin Carl 's 1875 baptism (Pastor Hoffmann's phonetic-s rendering). Konzen , with a z, appears once, on his daughter Auguste 's 1890 death record — the only American record we have located that carries the full Hessian z-form Konzen , the documented Trendelburg parish spelling. Two scribes, fifteen years apart, capturing two parallel orthographic variants of the same Hessian root. Maiden-name attestations on death records are among the most carefully captured forms in genealogical practice. Probate, cemetery indexing, and inheritance verification all reference the maiden name. A pastor or clerk recording a 25-year-old woman's death in 1890 was being asked to write the family name as the family used it — and what he wrote was Konzen , with the z that the Trendelburg parish register had used for Charles's ancestral surname pool since 1773. The 1890 Konzen reading does not contradict the 1875 Konsen reading — it complements it. The 1875 baptism captures the Hessian K-initial -en form through an English-speaking ear (the German tz affricate softens to s ); the 1890 death record captures the same Hessian root from a hand that knew

t: Story 8 · The brewing-trade godfather — Charles Research
d: The Anheuser-Busch Superintendent who stood as the sole named sponsor at the most-evidence-bearing baptism in the entire series.
b: Story 8 · The brewing-trade godfather — Charles Research Skip to main content Charles Research / Stories / Story 8 The brewing-trade godfather Story 8 of 9 · from the cinematic investigation Story 08 The brewing-trade godfather 3 October 1875 · St. Louis Erwin Carl Konsen The 1875 baptism that broke every rule — no Kreichelt sponsor, sole godfather Erwin Spraul , the only Konzen baptism preserving the Hessian Konsen spelling. The seven Konzen baptisms follow a pattern: Kreichelt sponsors, Kreichelt-by-marriage sponsors, Kreichelt-side parish circle. Six of them. The seventh — the 1875 Erwin Carl baptism — breaks the pattern entirely. There are no Kreichelt sponsors. There are no Albrecht-side or Pillmann-side relatives. The sole named godfather is Erwin Spraul , a non-relative. The child is named directly after him — Erwin Carl Konsen. Spraul was 27 years old at the baptism, born around 1848 in Baden, Germany. He had married Anna Maria Grassner in St. Louis in 1868 and lived at 1340 South 9th Street in Soulard. By 1880 he held the trade title of Superintendent at the Anheuser-Busch Brewing Association — the most senior position at one of the largest German-immigrant employers in St. Louis. Charles was a labourer; Spraul ran a brewery floor. Why him? The most direct answer comes from Jed in December 2025: Charles chose Spraul "because he was a successful fellow immigrant" — sharpened by the obituary to successful at the very top of the brewing trade . Charles, the labourer's labourer, picked the most respected fellow-Hessian-by-circumstance he knew as the namesake-godfather for his sixth child. There is a two-generation echo in this: Charles's grandson Frederick Charles Johnson (b. 1905) later worked as a Bottler at Anheuser-Busch, picking up the brewing-trade thread three decades after Erwin Spraul's death. The same baptism carries the closest American phonetic capture of the Hessian spelling. Of the seven children, only this one is recorded as Konsen

t: Story 9 · Seven baptisms with no one from his side — Charles Research
h: Seven baptisms with no one from his side
d: Across seven baptisms, seventeen sponsorship slots, and fifteen distinct named sponsors, not one is a Konze, Konzen, Kontzen, or Gannsen — the structural argument that Charles immigrat
b: Story 9 · Seven baptisms with no one from his side — Charles Research Skip to main content Charles Research / Stories / Story 9 Seven baptisms with no one from his side Story 9 of 9 · from the cinematic investigation Story 09 Seven baptisms with no one from his side 1866 – 1880 · St. Louis Charles & Auguste had seven children baptised at St. Marcus between 1866 and 1880. Across all seven baptisms, not a single named godparent is a documented blood relative of Charles. Every kin sponsor traces to Auguste's side. Charles's side (Hessen) — no sponsor across seven baptisms — Auguste's side (Kreichelt + parish kin) Georg Kreichelt Auguste's father · sponsor 1866, 1868, 1872 Henriette Kreichelt Auguste's mother · sponsor 1866, 1873 Carl Kreichelt Auguste's brother · sponsor across the later baptisms Bertha Barbara Kreichelt Auguste's sister The Albrechts Kreichelt-by-marriage The Pillmanns parish-circle, m. into Albrecht line The Münkes extended Soulard kin Same convention. Same parish. One side of the family present at every font; the other, not once. This is not how 19th-century German-Lutheran baptismal sponsorship usually worked. The structural convention was kin-default: maternal grandparents at the firstborn's font; paternal grandparents at the second; siblings of the parents at later children. The godparent role functioned as a public marker of family closeness inside the parish. Auguste's side honoured the convention rigorously across her brother Carl Kreichelt 's ten St. Marcus baptisms in the same register. Charles's seven baptisms honour it on her side too — Kreichelt parents, Kreichelt brothers, Kreichelt sister Bertha Barbara , Kreichelt-by-marriage Albrechts , Pillmanns , and Münkes — but never on his. The asymmetry is structural, not accidental. Across fourteen years of family life and seven baptismal events, Charles had no Hessian-side cousin, no Hessian-side uncle, no fellow-village Hessian friend close enough to invite to the font. He had n

t: The Pension Mutual-Aid Network — Charles Research
d: How Charles and his St. Louis circle worked the 1890 Dependent and Disability Pension Act together
b: The Pension Mutual-Aid Network — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Themes / The Pension Mutual-Aid Network The Pension Mutual-Aid Network How Charles and his St. Louis circle worked the 1890 Dependent and Disability Pension Act together THEMATIC 1889 – 1891 7 affidavits · 5 witnesses · 1 claim agent The pattern Charles Gannsen's 1891 pension award was not the work of one man working through paperwork alone. It was the product of a cross-city mutual-aid network of St. Louis Civil War veterans and civilian neighbors who knew each other through Battery B service, post-war workplaces, and the German Lutheran / Evangelical parish circle — spanning both halves of the city. Charles himself lived on the south side at 2408 DeKalb (Kosciusko) by 1890; only Theodore Gottlob (next door at 2409 DeKalb) was a literal neighbor. The other 1891 declaration witnesses — Hebbeler at 916 N. 6th, Thurbecker at 638 N. 7th, Niemeier at 1300 N. 9th — were all on the north side in the historic Hyde Park / Bremen / Old North St. Louis German corridor. The network was held together by long-term Battery B and parish ties, not address. Five separate witnesses swore seven affidavits across an eighteen-month window in 1890-91, all funneled through a single U.S. Claim Agent — Fred. W. Fout — and notarized by the same handful of St. Louis notaries. The names recur on each other's pension files in this same period. Several of the witnesses filed their own pensions in the same 1889-1891 window in which they testified for Charles. The pattern is not coincidence; it is the fingerprint of a working network. Why 1889–1891? The legal trigger The Dependent and Disability Pension Act of 27 June 1890 changed federal Civil War pension eligibility in a way that suddenly made it worth filing. Before 1890, a Union veteran had to prove that his disability was service-connected — that the rheumatism, hernia, or hearing loss originated in his time in uniform. That was a high ev

b: Deisel — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Towns / Deisel Deisel — Ortsteil von Trendelburg ★ The contested candidate village · "of Deisse, Kreis Cassel, Churhessen" — Charles's own 1866 St. Marcus marriage record ★ CONTESTED CANDIDATE Trendelburg parish Kreis Hofgeismar At a glance German name Deisel · also recorded as Deisse , Theisel , Deusel in 18th- and 19th-century parish hands Modern administrative Ortsteil of Stadt Trendelburg, Landkreis Kassel, Hessen, Germany Period administrative Kurfürst entum Hessen-Kassel until 1866 · Provinz Hessen-Nassau (Prussia) 1867–1918 Coordinates ~51.57°N 9.42°E · ~2 km south of Trendelburg · in the Diemel valley First documentary mention ~1300, as Deysel Population (mid-19th c.) ~600 inhabitants · primarily agricultural village Lutheran parish Part of Trendelburg parish (no separate church); register entries appear in Trendelburg KB 218842 and the separate Deisel baptism volume KB 218824 Project status ★ Contested candidate . Charles's 1866 St. Marcus marriage register entry names "Deisse" as his place of origin. Two Konze male births in January 1835 surfaced here. Schräder's 12 April 2026 verdict closes the legitimate-Konze-son hypothesis but leaves the surname-link unexplained. The 1866 St. Marcus marriage register entry — the documentary anchor "Carl Gonnson, aus Deisse, Kreis Cassel, Churhessen , und Clausthal, Königr. Hannover" — St. Marcus German Evangelical Church, St. Louis, Missouri · marriage register 1865–1867, image 72, entry No. 676 · 7 January 1866 · officiating pastor Rev. Geo. W. Wall This is the only documentary instance where Charles himself names a German place of origin. The 2020 AncestryProGenealogists journal proposed reading Deisse as a phonetic / clerical-shorthand for Deisel , a real village in Kreis Hofgeismar (the "Cassel" reference being administrative — the village fell under the broader Kreis Cassel jurisdiction at the time). That reading anchored seven month

h: Friedrichsfeld — small parish west of Trendelburg
d: Small parish · KB 218959 searched · Konze marriage came back negative · also seat of one Quentin candidate from the LAGIS emigration register
b: Friedrichsfeld — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Towns / Friedrichsfeld Friedrichsfeld — small parish west of Trendelburg Small parish · KB 218959 searched · Konze marriage came back negative · also seat of one Quentin candidate from the LAGIS emigration register SEARCHED · NEGATIVE Kirchenkreis Hofgeismar KB 218959 At a glance German name Friedrichsfeld Modern administrative Ortsteil of Stadt Trendelburg, Landkreis Kassel, Hessen, Germany Period administrative Kurfürst entum Hessen-Kassel until 1866 · Provinz Hessen-Nassau (Prussia) 1867–1918 Coordinates ~51.59°N 9.37°E · ~4 km west of Trendelburg Lutheran parish Friedrichsfeld parish · part of Kirchenkreis Hofgeismar Project status Searched · negative for the Konze × Hildebrand marriage. Also seat of one Quentin candidate (Auguste Quentin, ledig , 1853 emigration) — see the Schräder Stammtafel . Historical overview Friedrichsfeld is a small Hessian colonial-era settlement founded in 1755 by Landgraf Friedrich II of Hesse-Kassel as a Huguenot-style refugee colony, on land cleared specifically for the purpose. The original 30 settler families were predominantly French Huguenot exiles from the Pfalz and Saarland, but the village quickly absorbed Hessian families through intermarriage. By the 1830s the population was ~250–300 and Friedrichsfeld functioned as a typical small Lutheran agricultural village within Kreis Hofgeismar . The village's administrative church book is KB 218959 — the principal Friedrichsfeld parish register volume. The Hessian state archive HStAM Marburg holds emigration permits, military conscription rolls, and Volkszählung schedules under Bestand 180 (Landratsamt Hofgeismar). During the Austro-Prussian War of 1866 Friedrichsfeld passed to Prussia along with the rest of Kurhessen and became part of Provinz Hessen-Nassau. Modern administrative status: Ortsteil of Stadt Trendelburg. Significance to the investigation Friedrichsfeld was one of seven parishes s

b: Heisebeck — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Towns / Heisebeck Heisebeck — ⚠ undigitized · highest-value untouched parish Principal unsearched parish in the Hofgeismar-area search radius · pre-1845 registers not digitized on Archion · access requires Landeskirchliches Archiv Kassel ⚠ UNDIGITIZED HIGHEST-VALUE LEAD Kirchenkreis Hofgeismar At a glance German name Heisebeck Modern administrative Ortsteil of Stadt Hofgeismar, Landkreis Kassel, Hessen, Germany Period administrative Kurfürst entum Hessen-Kassel until 1866 · Provinz Hessen-Nassau (Prussia) 1867–1918 Coordinates ~51.59°N 9.66°E · in the Wesertal near the Weser · ~20 km northeast of Hofgeismar Lutheran parish Heisebeck parish · part of Kirchenkreis Hofgeismar Project status ★ Heisebeck is the highest-value untouched parish in the entire investigation . It is the principal unsearched parish remaining in the Hofgeismar-area search radius: Archion digitization is INCOMPLETE — the pre-1845 Lutheran registers are not online and have never been searched. Access requires the Landeskirchliches Archiv Kassel directly. Historical overview Heisebeck is a small village in the Wesertal in the far north of Hesse — near the Weser and the Lower-Saxony border, northeast of Hofgeismar — with a typical Hessian agricultural population. The village had its own Lutheran parish church serving the immediate community (population ~250–350 in the mid-19th century). Records of the parish go back to the late 17th century but are not digitized on Archion for the pre-1845 window — a critical gap given the investigation's focus on a marriage that would have occurred in the 1820s–1830s. The village passed to Prussia along with Kurhessen in September 1866 and became part of Stadt Hofgeismar in modern administrative consolidations. b: Helmarshausen — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Towns / Helmarshausen Helmarshausen — parish northeast of Deisel Searched · negative · marriage register 1820–1860 fully indexed; no Konze × Hildebrand match SEARCHED · NEGATIVE Kirchenkreis Hofgeismar Index complete At a glance German name Helmarshausen Modern administrative Stadt Bad Karlshafen, Landkreis Kassel, Hessen, Germany Period administrative Kurfürst entum Hessen-Kassel until 1866 · Provinz Hessen-Nassau (Prussia) 1867–1918 Coordinates ~51.61°N 9.49°E · ~6 km northeast of Deisel · on the Diemel near its confluence with the Weser Lutheran parish Helmarshausen parish · part of Kirchenkreis Hofgeismar Project status Searched · negative for the Konze × Hildebrand marriage. Marriage register 1820–1860 fully indexed (10 April 2026 + Session 63). Historical overview Helmarshausen is a medieval town with deep monastic roots — the Benedictine Abbey of Helmarshausen (founded 944, dissolved during the Reformation in 1538) was one of the most important scriptoria of medieval Germany, producing the famous Gospel of Henry the Lion in 1188. After the abbey's dissolution the town became a small Lutheran market town serving the Diemel-Weser confluence area. By the 19th century Helmarshausen was a typical Hessian Ackerbürger town of ~700–900 inhabitants, organized around its parish church. The parish records from 1820 onward are well-preserved on Archion as KB volumes covering baptism, marriage, and death entries. Helmarshausen passed to Prussia along with Kurhessen in September 1866 and became part of Regierungsbezirk Kassel, Provinz Hessen-Nassau. In modern times it is part of Stadt Bad Karlshafen, the spa town that developed at the Diemel-Weser confluence. Significance to the investigation Helmarshausen was one of seven parishes searched for a Joh. Heinrich Konze × Wilhelmine Hildebrand marriage — a parent pair drawn from the Entry 195 reading later retracted (corrected 4 May 2026).

d: Seat of the Kirchenkreis · two parishes (Altstadt + Neustadt) · administrative center for the entire Kreis Hofgeismar
b: Hofgeismar — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Towns / Hofgeismar Hofgeismar — Kreisstadt & Kirchenkreis seat Seat of the Kirchenkreis · two parishes (Altstadt + Neustadt) · administrative center for the entire Kreis Hofgeismar KIRCHENKREIS SEAT Two parishes Searched · negative At a glance German name Hofgeismar · also recorded as Hoffgeismar , Hof zu Geismar Modern administrative Kreisstadt of Landkreis Kassel, Hessen, Germany Period administrative Kurfürst entum Hessen-Kassel until 1866 · seat of Kreis Hofgeismar ( Kurhessen ) · Provinz Hessen-Nassau Regierungsbezirk Kassel 1867–1944 Coordinates ~51.50°N 9.39°E · ~22 km north of Kassel · in the Esse valley Stadtrechte Town rights granted in 1255 by Heinrich I, Landgraf of Hessen Population (1820) ~3,500 inhabitants Population (1875) ~4,500 inhabitants Lutheran parishes Two — Altstadt (medieval old town) and Neustadt (post-1700 expansion) Project status Both Hofgeismar parishes searched for a Joh. Heinrich Konze × Wilhelmine Hildebrand marriage — a parent pair drawn from the Entry 195 reading later retracted (corrected 4 May 2026) — Sessions 63 + 64; both negative. Historical overview Hofgeismar is the historic Kreisstadt (district seat) for the entire northwestern corner of Kurhessen. The town was founded in the early 13th century around a Cistercian-affiliated monastery (the Hof zu Geismar from which it takes its name) and received Stadtrechte in 1255. Through the 18th and 19th centuries it served as the administrative, judicial, and ecclesiastical center for ~50 surrounding villages including Trendelburg , Deisel , Friedrichsfeld , Helmarshausen , Hümme , and Schöneberg . The town has two distinct Lutheran parishes: Altstadt — the medieval old-town parish, centered on St. Peter's Church (built 13th c.). The dominant parish for old Hofgeismar families. Neustadt — the post-1700 expansion parish, formed when the town outgrew its medieval walls. The Hessian state archive HStAM

b: Hümme — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Towns / Hümme Hümme — parish south of Trendelburg Searched · negative · marriage register 1815–1840 verified in Kurrent script; no Konze marriage match SEARCHED · NEGATIVE Kirchenkreis Hofgeismar Kurrent-verified At a glance German name Hümme Modern administrative Ortsteil of Stadt Hofgeismar, Landkreis Kassel, Hessen, Germany Period administrative Kurfürst entum Hessen-Kassel until 1866 · Provinz Hessen-Nassau (Prussia) 1867–1918 Coordinates ~51.54°N 9.41°E · ~4 km south of Trendelburg · ~5 km north of Hofgeismar Lutheran parish Hümme parish · part of Kirchenkreis Hofgeismar Project status Searched · negative for the Konze × Hildebrand marriage. Marriage register 1815–1840 was Kurrent -verified (Session 61, 25 April 2026). Historical overview Hümme is a small village in the Esse valley, between Trendelburg and Hofgeismar, with documented mention from the 12th century. By the 19th century it was a typical Hessian agricultural village of ~400–500 inhabitants. The village had its own Lutheran parish church and parish records, but the population was small enough that emigration patterns are easily traced. The marriage register volume 1815–1840 is on Archion. The Kurrent-script verification (Session 61) involved cross-checking the difficult late-Kurrent handwriting against transcriptions to ensure the negative finding for the Konze marriage was accurate. Hümme passed to Prussia along with Kurhessen in September 1866 and became part of Stadt Hofgeismar in 20th-century administrative consolidations. Significance to the investigation Hümme was one of seven parishes searched for a Joh. Heinrich Konze × Wilhelmine Hildebrand marriage — a parent pair drawn from the Entry 195 reading later retracted (corrected 4 May 2026). t: Towns of the Hofgeismar Kirchenkreis — Charles Research
d: The geographic core of the German-side investigation — ten villages and parishes in northern Kurhessen
b: Towns of the Hofgeismar Kirchenkreis — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Towns of the Hofgeismar Kirchenkreis Towns of the Hofgeismar Kirchenkreis A gazetteer of the ten places in the German-side investigation — northern Kurhessen 10 TOWNS · KURHESSEN · NORTH OF KASSEL Looking for where the search stands? This page is a geographic reference — what each place is, where it sits, and the per-town index of research notes. All search status — candidate rankings, open gaps, and eliminated parishes — lives on the Hessian Parish Map , which tracks all 29 investigated parishes and is kept current. The setting — the Hofgeismar Kirchenkreis The investigation has concentrated on a small cluster of villages and parishes in the Hofgeismar Kirchenkreis — the Lutheran ecclesiastical district covering the northwestern corner of former Kurhessen, just north of Kassel. Click any pin for detail and a town-page link; hover any town card below to highlight its pin on the map . Konze family documented — Trendelburg · Friedrichsfeld Charles's stated origin — Deisel Registers partly undigitised — Heisebeck (post-1831 baptisms) Registers partly undigitised — Schöneberg (pre-1822) Other parishes — Helmarshausen · Hümme Kirchenkreis seat — Hofgeismar Reference — Sielen Capital · archive — Kassel The ten places Each place has its own page with geography, parish history, register holdings, and visiting links. Hover a tile to highlight its pin on the map above. For the current research status of any parish, see the Hessian Parish Map . Trendelburg Hilltop castle town on the Diemel; the parish church for Deisel and surrounding villages. Documented Kontze residence 1773–1830s — multiple generations of Rathsverwandte . Deisel The village Charles named at his 1866 St. Louis marriage ( "aus Deisse, Kreis Cassel, Churhessen" ). Three "geb. Konzen" mothers attested 1828–1829; churched from Trendelburg. Friedrichsfeld Small colonist village just west of Trendelburg.

h: Kassel — capital of Kurhessen · seat of state & church archives
d: Capital of the Electorate of Hesse-Kassel until 1866 · seat of the Kurhessen state government, the Provinz Hessen-Nassau Regierungsbezirk · home of the Landeskirchliches Archiv and Hessen state archive holdings
b: Kassel — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Towns / Kassel Kassel — capital of Kurhessen · seat of state & church archives Capital of the Electorate of Hesse-Kassel until 1866 · seat of the Kurhessen state government, the Provinz Hessen-Nassau Regierungsbezirk · home of the Landeskirchliches Archiv and Hessen state archive holdings CAPITAL · ARCHIVE SEAT Kurhessen 1815–1866 Provinz Hessen-Nassau 1867+ At a glance German name Kassel Modern administrative Kreisfreie Stadt Kassel, Hessen, Germany · ~200,000 population Period administrative Capital of Kurfürst entum Hessen-Kassel 1567–1866 · seat of Regierungsbezirk Kassel within Provinz Hessen-Nassau 1867–1944 · Hessen 1945–today Coordinates ~51.32°N 9.49°E · in the Fulda valley · ~25 miles south of Trendelburg Lutheran parish Multiple Lutheran parishes (Martinskirche, Lutherkirche, etc.) · seat of the Evangelical Church of Kurhessen -Waldeck (EKKW) Project status Capital and archival seat. The Landeskirchliches Archiv Kassel (EKKW) holds the original parish books not yet digitized on Archion (notably Heisebeck pre-1845, Schöneberg pre-1822). The Hessisches Hauptstaatsarchiv Marburg (HStAM) — though physically in Marburg — holds the Kurhessen state administrative files including the Kreis Hofgeismar Bestand 180. Historical overview Kassel was the seat of the Hessian state from the 16th century onward. It was the residence of the Landgraves and later Electors of Hessen-Kassel until 1866, and one of the largest German cities by population in the 18th century — peaking at ~50,000 inhabitants by 1860 before the Austro-Prussian War. The city is known for its Wilhelmshöhe palace complex (UNESCO World Heritage), its art museums, and historically as the capital of a politically reactionary state under Friedrich Wilhelm I (Elector 1847–1866). Charles Gannsen, born in 1835 in Kurhessen, would have known Kassel as his state capital. Newspapers, cultural events, and political news flowed throu

b: Schöneberg — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Towns / Schöneberg Schöneberg — parish south of Trendelburg Partially digitized · post-1822 volume on Archion · pre-1822 records require Landeskirchliches Archiv Kassel PARTIALLY DIGITIZED Kirchenkreis Hofgeismar Pre-1822 undigitized At a glance German name Schöneberg Modern administrative Ortsteil of Stadt Hofgeismar, Landkreis Kassel, Hessen, Germany Period administrative Kurfürst entum Hessen-Kassel until 1866 · Provinz Hessen-Nassau (Prussia) 1867–1918 Coordinates ~51.52°N 9.42°E · ~7 km south of Trendelburg · just north of Hofgeismar Lutheran parish Schöneberg parish · part of Kirchenkreis Hofgeismar · related to Carlsdorf French Huguenot colony Project status Searched post-1822 only · pre-1822 volumes are not yet digitized on Archion. Pre-1822 access requires Landeskirchliches Archiv Kassel direct inquiry. Historical overview Schöneberg is a small village just north of the town of Hofgeismar, in Kreis Hofgeismar , overlooking the Esse and Diemel valleys. It is closely linked to the neighboring Carlsdorf , a French Huguenot refugee colony founded in 1686 by Landgraf Carl von Hessen-Kassel for refugees fleeing Louis XIV's revocation of the Edict of Nantes. Many Schöneberg families have French Huguenot surnames (Grandjot, Person, etc.) that thread through the candidate-family search. The Lutheran parish church serves both Schöneberg and surrounding villages. Parish records exist back to the late 17th century but Archion has only digitized the post-1822 volumes; pre-1822 books remain at the Landeskirchliches Archiv Kassel and require direct inquiry. Schöneberg passed to Prussia along with Kurhessen in September 1866. In modern administrative consolidation it became part of Stadt Hofgeismar. Significance to the investigation Schöneberg was searched as part of the seven-parish sweep for a Joh. Heinrich Konze × Wilhelmine Hildebrand marriage — a parent pair drawn from the Entry 195

h: Sielen — small village northeast of Trendelburg
d: Mentioned in a few Konze-related register entries · not yet exhaustively searched · part of Trendelburg parish complex
b: Sielen — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Towns / Sielen Sielen — small village northeast of Trendelburg Mentioned in a few Konze -related register entries · not yet exhaustively searched · part of Trendelburg parish complex REFERENCE VILLAGE Trendelburg parish Kirchenkreis Hofgeismar At a glance German name Sielen Modern administrative Ortsteil of Stadt Trendelburg, Landkreis Kassel, Hessen, Germany Period administrative Kurfürst entum Hessen-Kassel until 1866 · Provinz Hessen-Nassau (Prussia) 1867–1918 Coordinates ~51.61°N 9.46°E · ~3 km northeast of Trendelburg Lutheran parish Part of Trendelburg parish complex Project status Reference village · not separately indexed. Sielen residents appear in Trendelburg KB volumes (218842, 220093, 220099) under their village-of-origin notation. Historical overview Sielen is a small Hessian village northeast of Trendelburg, with a population of perhaps 200–300 in the 19th century. It does not have its own Lutheran parish church — Sielen residents attended church in Trendelburg, and their baptisms, marriages, and deaths appear in the Trendelburg parish register volumes with a "Sielen" village-of-origin notation. The village passed to Prussia along with Kurhessen in September 1866 and became an Ortsteil of Stadt Trendelburg in modern administrative consolidations. Significance to the investigation Sielen is referenced in a few Konze-related register entries from the Trendelburg books — typically as a village-of-residence for collateral relatives of the main Trendelburg / Deisel Konze line. It has not been exhaustively searched as an independent parish (because it does not have its own register volume). Searches in Trendelburg KB 218842 and 220XXX series automatically cover any Sielen residents whose entries were filed in Trendelburg. If a future research lead surfaces a Sielen-specific Konze branch, the relevant search would be in the Trendelburg parish books filtered for Sielen-village-of

b: Trendelburg — Charles Research Skip to main content ← Charles Research / Towns / Trendelburg Trendelburg — Stadt Trendelburg, Hofgeismar Kirchenkreis Earliest substantial Kontze paper trail · 1773–1830s · Trendelburg's Lutheran parish covered Deisel and several surrounding villages DOCUMENTED KONZE RESIDENCE Kirchenkreis Hofgeismar Kurhessen → Prussia 1866 At a glance German name Trendelburg · also recorded historically as Trendelnburg Modern administrative Stadt Trendelburg, Landkreis Kassel, Hessen, Germany (BRD) Period administrative Kurfürst entum Hessen-Kassel until 1866 · Provinz Hessen-Nassau (Prussia) 1867–1918 · Volksstaat Hessen 1919–1945 · Hessen 1945–today Coordinates ~51.59°N 9.41°E · ~25 miles north of Kassel · west bank of the Diemel river Stadtrechte Town rights granted in 1305 by Heinrich I, Landgraf of Hessen Population (1820) ~1,200 inhabitants · the largest village in the Trendelburg parish complex Population (1875) ~1,400 inhabitants · post-Prussian-annexation Lutheran parish Trendelburg parish · also serves Deisel , Sielen , Eberschütz, and several smaller villages Project status Documented Kontze residence 1773–1830s ; multiple generations served as Rathsverwandte (town councillors) Historical overview Trendelburg sits on the west bank of the Diemel river at the northern edge of historic Kurhessen , dominated by the medieval Burg Trendelburg — a 13th-century hilltop castle that gives the town its name. The town received its Stadtrechte (town rights) in 1305 and developed as a small market town serving the agricultural villages of the surrounding Diemel valley. Through the 18th and 19th centuries Trendelburg was a typical Hessian Ackerbürger town: townspeople who worked their own farms, with a small artisan and council class layered on top. The Kontze / Konze / Contze family appears in Trendelburg parish records from at least 1773, when Arnold Philipp Kontze is recorded as Rathsverwandter und Ackermann (councilman and farmer). Thr
